title: Binning globs reused per-profile output dir, including stale NetCDFs from prior runs with different input sets
description: Output directories are content-hashed and *reused* (not cleared) when a matching .params_sha256 signature exists (config_base.resolve_output_dir lines 212-223). Per-profile NetCDFs are written with input-stem-embedded names (`{stem}_prof{NN}.nc` in rsi/profile.py), so they accumulate across runs. The binning stage then globs the WHOLE shared dir: `prof_ncs = sorted(output_dirs['profiles'].glob('*.nc'))` (line 1881), and identically for diss (line 1892) and chi (line 1912). If a user re-runs with the SAME config but a *reduced or changed* set of input .p files (e.g. drops a file, or reprocesses a single station), resolve_output_dir returns the existing populated dir, and the glob picks up the prior run's stale per-profile NCs. Those stale profiles are then silently folded into binned.nc and the combo products, producing wrong aggregate fields with no warning. There is no cleanup anywhere in the pipeline/CLI (confirmed: no unlink/rmtree/shutil, and no force/clean config option — only files.force_trim, which only re-trims). This is a silent-correctness hazard, not merely cosmetic, because the binned/combo deliverables would contain data from files that are no longer in the requested input set.
suggested_fix: Bind the binning/combo inputs to the explicit per-file outputs produced in THIS run (e.g. collect the `result['profiles']/result['diss']/result['chi']` paths returned by process_file across all files and pass that exact list to bin_by_depth/bin_diss/bin_chi) instead of globbing the shared directory. Alternatively, stamp each reused output dir with a manifest of the current input set and prune/ignore NCs not in it, or clear stale `*_prof*.nc` for files no longer present before binning.
verify_reasoning:
Verified against the actual code; the finding holds.

(1) Reuse without clearing: `resolve_output_dir` (src/odas_tpw/config_base.py:193-229) returns an existing `{prefix}_NN` dir whenever a `.params_sha256_<hash>` signature matches (lines 219-223), without clearing its contents. The hash is computed solely from config params + upstream config (compute_hash/canonicalize, lines 181-189) — it never incorporates the input .p file set. `_setup_output_dirs` (pipeline.py:1536-1587) resolves `output_dirs["profiles"]/["diss"]/["chi"]` through this same mechanism, so these are shared, reusable dirs.

(2) Outputs accumulate: per-profile NCs are written as `{stem}_prof{pi:03d}.nc` (rsi/profile.py:137) with mode "w" on the individual file only — no dir clearing. Diss and chi reuse that stem-embedded name via `out_name = Path(prof_path).name` (pipeline.py:1190, 1312). Distinct input files therefore write distinct NCs that persist across runs.

(3) No cleanup / no clean option: grep found no rmtree/unlink/os.remove/prune/manifest in perturb or config_base. The only `shutil` use is file-copy in merge.py. The only force flag is `files.force_trim` (config.py:26, pipeline.py:1437), which only re-trims — confirming the finding's claim.

(4) Binning globs the whole shared dir: pipeline.py:1881 (`output_dirs["profiles"].glob("*.nc")`), 1892 (diss), 1912 (chi). Meanwhile `process_file` already returns the exact paths produced in THIS run as `result["profiles"]` (line 1094), `result["diss"]` (1194), `result["chi"]` (1315), but the orchestrator consumes `result` only via `_done_message` logging (lines 1821, 1852) and discards the paths. So the precise per-run input list to binning is available but unused; the glob instead picks up stale NCs from prior runs.

Net effect: re-running with the same config but a reduced or changed input set (drop a file, reprocess one station) into the same output_root yields the prior populated dir, and binning silently folds stale per-profile/diss/chi NCs into binned.nc and the combo products — a silent-correctness defect with no warning. Suggested fix (bind binning to the explicit result[...] paths from this run, or stamp/prune by input-set manifest) is correct and feasible since the data is already in hand.

Severity major rather than blocker: it produces silently-wrong aggregates only under a specific usage pattern (same config + changed input set into the same output_root); the normal path (stable input set or fresh output_root) is unaffected. Minor doc nuance: actual name is `{stem}_prof{pi:03d}.nc` (3-digit), not `{stem}_prof{NN}.nc`, but this does not affect the defect.

title: chi is skipped for any profile whose dissipation output is missing, even when use_epsilon=False does not require epsilon
description: The chi loop iterates result['profiles'] but hard-requires a matching diss output per profile: `diss_path = diss_by_profile.get(prof_path); if diss_path is None: ... continue` (lines 1226-1233), and the whole chi block is gated on `result['diss']` being non-empty (line 1200). When chi.use_epsilon=False (Method 2, pure spectral fit — the documented mode for MR on a vibrating glider where shear epsilon is contaminated), chi does not need epsilon at all, yet a profile whose dissipation computation errored out is silently dropped from chi as well. The coupling is reasonable in the default config (mixing=True needs epsilonMean), but for the use_epsilon=False + mixing=False combination it drops valid, computable chi estimates. The log message ('no matching diss output') also mischaracterizes the cause for that configuration.
suggested_fix: When chi.use_epsilon is False AND chi.mixing is False, allow chi to run for a profile without a diss output (pass epsilon_ds=None and skip the diss-path requirement), rather than skipping the profile. Otherwise keep the current requirement but clarify the skip log to note it is because mixing/epsilon coupling needs the diss product.
verify_reasoning:
Verified against the actual code in src/odas_tpw/perturb/pipeline.py.

CORE CLAIM CONFIRMED:
- Line 1200: chi block gated on result["diss"] being non-empty.
- Lines 1226-1233: per-profile, requires diss_by_profile.get(prof_path) non-None; otherwise logs "chi skipped for %s: no matching diss output" and continues, dropping the profile from chi.
- Line 1242: diss_ds = xr.open_dataset(diss_path) if chi_use_epsilon else None — when use_epsilon=False, the diss dataset is NOT used by the chi computation (_compute_chi is called with epsilon_ds=None, line 1259-1261).
- The only other consumer of diss_path/diss_ds in the chi loop is the mixing branch (lines 1276-1293, gated on chi_cfg.get("mixing", True)), which is skipped when mixing=False.
=> With use_epsilon=False AND mixing=False, the diss output is genuinely unnecessary, yet a profile lacking one is still skipped.

REACHABILITY / NOT-INTENTIONAL:
- Both use_epsilon and mixing are user-configurable booleans (config.py:114 and 121). use_epsilon=False is an explicitly documented supported mode (Method 2 spectral fit for "MR on a vibrating glider has unreliable epsilon and should not seed chi from it").
- Epsilon and chi consume independent sensors: _compute_epsilon raises on missing shear/accelerometer channels (dissipation.py:115, 117) and returns [] when no profiles, while _compute_chi works off FP07 thermistor gradient channels (chi_io.py:86). An epsilon failure (dead/excluded shear probe, missing accelerometer for Goodman, or any exception caught at pipeline.py:1196) does NOT imply a chi failure, so the dropped profile is genuinely computable for chi.
- Existing test test_chi_use_epsilon_false_passes_none (tests/test_perturb_pipeline.py:869) sets use_epsilon=False but writes a diss output and a non-empty mock_eps return, so it never exercises the missing-diss skip path. The coupling is incidental, not a tested invariant.

ADDITIONAL: The skip log message ("no matching diss output") misattributes the cause in the use_epsilon=False configuration, as the finding notes.

SEVERITY: minor is correct. It only triggers in a non-default configuration combination (defaults are use_epsilon=True and mixing=True), and only for profiles where epsilon specifically failed/produced no output. It silently drops valid, computable chi estimates (data-completeness/error-handling defect) rather than corrupting produced data. Not a blocker or major.

title: bin_by_time ignores diagnostics flag (no n_samples/_std/_n in time combos)
description: bin_by_time() accepts a `diagnostics` parameter (passed through from bin_diss/bin_chi at lines 597 and 614, and reachable from the pipeline whenever binning.method=='time', pipeline.py:1863/1939) but its body never references `diagnostics`. The depth path (bin_by_depth -> _bin_snapshot) emits per-bin `n_samples`, `<var>_std`, and `<var>_n` when diagnostics=True; the time path emits none of them. A user who runs the pipeline with bin_method='time' and diagnostics enabled silently gets a diss/chi/profile time-combo with zero diagnostic variables, with no error or warning. This is a real behavioral inconsistency between the two binning modes. The existing test suite only exercises diagnostics on bin_by_depth (tests/test_perturb_binning.py:194-211); there is no test asserting bin_by_time diagnostics, so the gap is uncaught.
suggested_fix: In bin_by_time, when diagnostics=True, additionally compute and emit n_samples (via _bin_array counts), <var>_std (via _bin_std), and <var>_n (via _bin_finite_counts) per variable, mirroring _bin_snapshot. Add a test that bin_by_time(..., diagnostics=True) yields T_std and n_samples.
verify_reasoning: Verified against the actual code. bin_by_time (src/odas_tpw/perturb/binning.py:508-580) declares `diagnostics: bool = False` at line 512 but its body never references `diagnostics`; binned_data only ever receives aggregated values (lines 560, 566, 569). The diagnostics arg IS forwarded into it: bin_diss line 597, bin_chi line 614, and pipeline.py:1939-1940 (profile time path), with bin_method and diagnostics both sourced from config (pipeline.py:1863, 1866). So the (method='time', diagnostics=True) path is reachable end-to-end. By contrast the depth path DOES emit diagnostics: bin_by_depth emits n_samples (line 486), <var>_std (lines 466-467/475) and <var>_n (lines 469-471/476-479). Net effect: with bin_method='time' and diagnostics enabled, the user silently gets diss/chi/profile time combos with zero diagnostic variables, no error/warning -- a real behavioral inconsistency between the two binning modes. Test gap confirmed: the only diagnostics assertion (tests/test_perturb_binning.py:194-211, test_diagnostics) uses bin_by_depth; every bin_by_time test (lines 249,272,286,402,419,423) omits diagnostics=True and asserts no diagnostic vars. The helpers the suggested fix relies on exist: _bin_std (line 48), _bin_finite_counts (line 123), _bin_array (line 70). I downgraded major->minor: this is a silently-ignored opt-in diagnostic feature in one of two modes; it produces no incorrect numeric values and no crash -- the primary binned products are unaffected -- so it is a feature/test gap rather than a correctness or data-integrity defect.

title: rho variable labeled sea_water_density but holds density anomaly (in-situ density - 1000)
description: seawater.py:57 computes `rho = gsw.rho(SA, CT, P) - 1000.0` (in-situ density MINUS 1000, ~25 kg/m^3), and this value is written into the CTD product and combos. The schema (COMBO_SCHEMA['rho'], reused by CTD_SCHEMA['rho']) assigns standard_name='sea_water_density' with units='kg/m^3'. The CF standard name 'sea_water_density' denotes the actual in-situ density (~1025 kg/m^3), not density-minus-1000. The long_name ('in-situ density - 1000') even documents the offset, confirming the mismatch. A CF-aware consumer selecting by standard_name + units gets values ~1000 kg/m^3 too small with no indication. (Contrast sigma0, which is correctly labeled sea_water_sigma_theta — that standard name IS the minus-1000 anomaly.)
suggested_fix: Either store full in-situ density (drop the -1000 in seawater.py and keep standard_name='sea_water_density'), or keep the anomaly and remove the CF standard_name (there is no CF standard name for in-situ-density-minus-1000; use only a descriptive long_name), so the metadata no longer claims a quantity the data are not.
verify_reasoning:
Verified end to end. seawater.py:57 computes `rho = gsw.rho(SA, CT, P) - 1000.0`, i.e. in-situ density minus 1000 (~25 kg/m^3), stored under key "rho". This array is written into the CTD product (ctd.py:351-352, then ds.to_netcdf) and the schema is applied via combo.py:101 `apply_schema(combo, schema)`. CTD_SCHEMA['rho'] (netcdf_schema.py:373) aliases COMBO_SCHEMA['rho'] (lines 192-197), which sets standard_name='sea_water_density', units='kg/m^3', long_name='in-situ density - 1000'.

CF semantics confirm the mismatch: the CF Standard Name Table defines 'sea_water_density' as the in-situ density (full value, ~1025 kg/m^3, no offset), with canonical units kg m-3. By contrast 'sea_water_sigma_theta' is explicitly defined as potential density minus 1000 kg m-3. So sigma0 (labeled sea_water_sigma_theta, also -1000 via gsw.sigma0) is correctly labeled, but rho is labeled with a standard name whose definition does NOT include the -1000 offset that the data actually carries. The variable's own long_name ('in-situ density - 1000') documents the offset, confirming the data is the anomaly, not full density. A CF-aware consumer selecting by standard_name + units gets values ~1000 kg/m^3 too small with no indication.

No downstream re-add of 1000 was found (grep of combo.py/cli.py/pipeline.py shows none). No test asserts rho's value range (test_perturb_seawater.py only checks keys present, SP range, shapes, and depth monotonicity), so nothing guards this — the only rho>1000 assertion is in test_scor160_ocean.py, an unrelated module (scor160.ocean.density, which returns full density). 

Not in the known/intentional exclusion list (that list covers only the bin-coordinate units mislabel and pyturb third-party code). Severity major is appropriate: silent ~40x relative error for any standard-name-driven consumer, though it does not corrupt the stored numeric data or break the pipeline. Suggested remediation in the finding is sound (either drop -1000 and keep sea_water_density, or keep the anomaly and remove the standard_name since no CF standard name exists for in-situ-density-minus-1000).

title: Per-probe QC metrics (fom/FM/K_max/K_max_ratio/mad) are 2D and silently dropped by both binning paths
description: Dissipation/chi NetCDFs carry per-probe diagnostics as 2D variables on dims ['probe','time'] (dissipation.py:448-484: fom, FM, K_max, K_max_ratio, mad). mk_epsilon_mean (epsilon_combine.py:55-60) only splits `epsilon` into 1D e_1/e_2; it does NOT create 1D per-probe fom/FM/K_max/etc. The binning snapshot loader keeps only 1-D variables (binning.py:251 `if len(shape) != 1 ... continue`), and bin_by_time likewise skips ndim!=1 (binning.py:549). Consequently NO per-probe QC metric ever enters the binned diss/chi combo, in either depth or time mode. The schema even declares FM_1/FM_2/K_max_1/K_max_2/fom_T_1/... (netcdf_schema.py:233-325) — variables that are never produced anywhere in the codebase (no f"FM_"/f"K_max_" producer exists), so apply_schema's entries for them are dead. This is established behavior with no test asserting these reach the combo; flagged as a coverage/expectation gap rather than a crash.
suggested_fix: Decide intent: if per-probe QC should be binned, split the 2D probe metrics into 1D K_max_1/FM_1/fom_1 etc. before binning (as done for e_1/e_2) so they survive the 1-D filter and match the schema; otherwise remove the unproduced FM_1/K_max_1/fom_T_1 schema entries to avoid implying they exist.
verify_reasoning: Verified against actual code. (1) dissipation.py:430-494 emits fom/FM/K_max/K_max_ratio/mad/method as 2D vars on dims ["probe","time"]. (2) mk_epsilon_mean (epsilon_combine.py:55-60) only splits the 2D `epsilon` into 1D e_1/e_2 and adds 1D epsilonMean/epsilonLnSigma; it never produces 1D per-probe fom/FM/K_max/etc., and leaves the 2D metrics untouched in the diss_combo NetCDF written at pipeline.py:1192. mk_chi_mean (chi_combine.py:71-77) likewise only splits `chi` (reads epsilon_T), producing no fom_T/K_max_T/kB per-probe vars. (3) Both binning paths drop non-1D vars: binning.py:251 `if len(shape) != 1 or shape[0] != n_depth: continue` (depth) and binning.py:549 `if arr.ndim != 1 ...: continue` (time). So the 2D per-probe QC metrics are silently dropped in BOTH depth and time modes and never enter the binned/combo products. (4) netcdf_schema.py declares FM_1/FM_2/K_max_1/K_max_2 (233-252), fom_T_1/fom_T_2/K_max_T_1/K_max_T_2 (306-325), and kB_1/kB_2 (296-305); a repo-wide grep finds NO producer of any of these names anywhere — apply_schema (netcdf_schema.py:455-462) only applies attrs to variables actually present in the dataset, so these ~10 schema entries are dead/never-matched. All factual claims hold. This is a genuine defect (dropped diagnostics + dead schema declarations that overstate what the product contains), not a crash; minor severity is appropriate. Note: the finding's 'test-gap' category undersells it slightly — the defect exists in the code itself independent of test coverage — but that does not change the is_real verdict.

title: NetCDF fill/masked values exposed as real lat/lon/time via .data
description: GPSFromNetCDF reads the time, lat, and lon variables with `t_var[:].data` / `ds.variables[lat_var][:].data` / `[...lon_var][:].data`. netCDF4 returns masked arrays by default (auto-masking is NOT disabled in this module — only binning.py calls set_auto_mask(False)). Accessing `.data` on a masked array yields the raw buffer INCLUDING the _FillValue (e.g. -999, 1e20) rather than NaN. Confirmed empirically: a variable with one masked entry yields `[10., -999., 30., 40.]` via `.data` vs `[10., nan, 30., 40.]` via `.filled(np.nan)`. The standard way a GPS file marks a missing fix is exactly a masked/fill value, so a -999 latitude (or a fill-valued time) is fed straight into interp1d and produces wildly wrong interpolated positions near the gap. These positions then drive the TEOS-10 absolute-salinity conversion (perturb/ctd.py:344-345, 167-201) and the file geospatial_lat/lon_* attributes, silently corrupting results with no warning. The fast-path decoder also multiplies fill-valued times: `_decode_cf_times_fast` does `raw.astype(float64)*factor+epoch_s` on the same `.data` buffer.
suggested_fix: Use `.filled(np.nan)` when the variable returns a masked array (e.g. `arr = ds.variables[v][:]; arr = arr.filled(np.nan) if np.ma.isMaskedArray(arr) else np.asarray(arr)`), or call `ds.set_auto_mask(False)` only after explicitly mapping fill values to NaN. Apply to the time variable too (line 250).
verify_reasoning: Verified by reading the code and empirical testing with netCDF4 1.7.4. gps.py:250,280-281 use `t_var[:].data` / `ds.variables[lat_var][:].data` / `[...lon_var][:].data`. netCDF4 auto-masks by default; `.data` on the returned MaskedArray exposes the raw _FillValue (confirmed: `[10., -999., 30., 40.]`) instead of NaN. GPSFromNetCDF never calls set_auto_mask(False) or .filled() — that guard exists only in binning.py:220, a separate path. Downstream flow confirmed: interpolated lat/lon feed ctd.py:344-345 and _assign_gps_with_casts (167-201) -> add_seawater_properties TEOS-10 SA conversion (binning.py:351) and combo.py:132-148 geospatial attrs. Demonstrated harm: with a -999 lat fill present, interp1d returns -494.45 near the gap (nonsensical latitude) instead of NaN; a fill-valued time entry yields a non-monotonic time axis (raw*factor+epoch at gps.py:114) fed to interp1d, silently corrupting interpolation globally. The .filled(np.nan) path instead yields NaN, which nanmin/nanmax and the coverage warning handle. No test exercises masked/fill GPS input (tests/test_perturb_gps.py creates vars without fill_value and never masks). Downgraded from blocker to major because triggering requires a GPS NetCDF that actually contains masked/fill entries (data-dependent); when it triggers it is silent and corrupts positions/salinity/metadata.

title: Hotel NetCDF channels/time expose fill values via .data
description: _load_netcdf reads the default time (`ds.variables[time_column][:].data`), every data channel (`var[:].data.astype(np.float64)`), and per-channel time columns (`ds.variables[tc][:].data`) using `.data`, which exposes _FillValue from masked netCDF variables as real numbers instead of NaN (same root cause as the gps.py finding; auto-masking is not disabled here). Hotel telemetry (glider speed, pitch, CTD) routinely has gaps stored as fill values; those become e.g. -999 and are then interpolated onto the instrument time axis and registered as physical channels, silently injecting bad speed/pitch/CTD values that propagate into the dissipation/chi computations and the output NetCDF.
suggested_fix: Convert masked arrays to NaN with `.filled(np.nan)` (or read via a helper that maps fill values to NaN) for the time variable, each data variable, and each per-channel time array.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed by reading src/odas_tpw/perturb/hotel.py:281, 293, 303. _load_netcdf reads the default time, every data channel, and per-channel time columns via netCDF4 `[:].data`. With netCDF4's default auto-masking (never disabled here), `variable[:]` returns a MaskedArray and `.data` returns the raw buffer that still contains _FillValue at masked positions; `.filled(np.nan)` would instead map them to NaN. I empirically verified this: a test variable with fill_value=-999 and gaps returns `[0.5, 0.6, -999.0, 0.7, -999.0]` via `.data` vs `[0.5, 0.6, nan, 0.7, nan]` via `.filled(np.nan)`. Downstream is real: these arrays feed interpolate_hotel -> _interp_one, which uses scipy interp1d/PchipInterpolator with no fill-value filtering, so a -999 is interpolated as a legitimate value onto the instrument time axis and registered as a physical channel (speed/pitch/CTD) that propagates into dissipation/chi and output NetCDF. No guard exists (no set_auto_mask(False), no .filled, no sentinel filtering). The cross-reference to gps.py (lines 250, 280-281) is accurate -- identical .data pattern. Test gap confirmed: hotel tests create variables without fill_value and write complete data, so the masked path is never exercised; this is not a known/intentional case. Severity major rather than blocker because the corruption is conditional on hotel files actually using netCDF fill values for gaps (realistic for glider/AUV telemetry but not guaranteed every deployment); when it occurs it silently injects bad physical values into scientific output.

title: _parse_time 'auto' silently mis-decodes numeric time in the 1e6..1e9 window (and on any NaN)
description: In the default time_format='auto' path, when the median of numeric time values is NOT < 1e6 and NOT > 1e9, the code falls through to `pd.to_datetime(raw_time)` on the RAW NUMERIC array. pd.to_datetime interprets bare numbers as nanoseconds-since-epoch, so e.g. 5e7 (relative seconds) becomes 0.05 s and is returned with is_relative=False — silent garbage, no exception (the try/except never triggers because pd.to_datetime succeeds). The [1e6,1e9) window corresponds to relative-seconds files spanning ~11.6 days to ~31.7 years, plausible for a long glider/mooring deployment. Worse, `np.median` (not np.nanmedian) returns NaN if the time column contains ANY NaN, so both `< 1e6` and `> 1e9` are False and EVERY such file (any single NaN timestamp) is pushed through the nanosecond misinterpretation. This is the default code path (config default time_format: 'auto').
suggested_fix: Use np.nanmedian, and in the ambiguous band do NOT feed numeric arrays to pd.to_datetime; only attempt ISO parsing when the dtype is non-numeric/string. For genuinely ambiguous numeric magnitudes, raise or treat as relative seconds rather than silently reinterpreting as nanoseconds.
verify_reasoning:
Verified against actual code at src/odas_tpw/perturb/hotel.py:152-167 and confirmed config default time_format='auto' (config.py:43, pipeline.py:1795). Both defect mechanisms reproduce empirically:

1) Ambiguous numeric band [1e6, 1e9): In the 'auto' path, vals=raw_time.astype(float64); median=np.median(vals). When 1e6 <= median <= 1e9 the code falls to the else branch and runs pd.to_datetime(raw_time) on the RAW NUMERIC array. Verified: pd.to_datetime([5e7,...]) yields [0.05, 0.05, ...] seconds (nanoseconds-since-epoch interpretation) with NO exception, returned with is_relative=False. The try/except never fires because pd.to_datetime succeeds. This window corresponds to relative-seconds deployments of ~11.6 days to ~31.7 years (plausible for long glider/mooring records).

2) NaN poisoning: The code uses np.median, not np.nanmedian. Verified: a single NaN in the time array makes np.median return NaN, so both (median<1e6) and (median>1e9) are False for ANY file (regardless of true magnitude), forcing the data through the same nanosecond misinterpretation; NaN entries become a huge negative epoch value (~-9.2e9 s). This is the broader, more dangerous trigger since occasional NaN time gaps are common in hotel/CTD/glider data.

No upstream guard prevents this: all three loaders (_load_csv:249, _load_netcdf:282, _load_mat:365) pass numeric raw_time arrays (mat/csv cast to float64, netCDF time vars are numeric) straight into _parse_time with the default 'auto'. ISO-string columns would instead raise at .astype(float64) (line 153), so the silent corruption is specific to numeric input — but numeric time is the dominant real-world case and 'auto' is the documented default.

Severity major (not blocker): clean numeric data with median<1e6 (relative) or >1e9 (epoch) still decodes correctly, so the silent-mis-decode for NaN-free data is confined to the [1e6,1e9) magnitude window; however the NaN trigger and the silent (no-exception) nature of the corruption of the primary time axis under the default config justify major. The suggested fix (np.nanmedian; do not feed numeric arrays to pd.to_datetime in the ambiguous band; restrict ISO parse to non-numeric dtypes; raise/treat-as-relative for ambiguous numeric magnitudes) is sound.

title: GPSFromNetCDF leaks the open Dataset on any init-time exception
description: `ds = nc.Dataset(str(file), 'r')` is opened at line 246 and only closed at line 282 with no try/finally. Any exception in between — a KeyError from a wrong `time_var`/`lat_var`/`lon_var`, a num2date failure on the slow path, or an astype error — propagates before `ds.close()`, leaking the file handle. In batch processing across many files (or repeated config-driven retries) this accumulates open handles.
suggested_fix: Wrap the body in `try/finally: ds.close()` or use `with nc.Dataset(...) as ds:`.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed by reading src/odas_tpw/perturb/gps.py:246-282. The Dataset is opened at line 246 with `ds = nc.Dataset(str(file), "r")` (not a context manager), and `ds.close()` only appears once, at line 282, on the success path. grep confirms exactly one open and one close with no try/finally or `with` wrapping the __init__ body (the only try blocks, lines 103/106, are inside the unrelated module-level helper `_decode_cf_times_fast`). Multiple statements between open and close can raise before close: `ds.variables[time_var/lat_var/lon_var]` (lines 247, 280, 281) raise KeyError for a wrong/configurable var name (these are __init__ params defaulting to "time"/"lat"/"lon"); `nc.num2date(...)` (line 262) can raise on malformed CF units; `.astype(np.float64)` (lines 280-281) can raise. On any of those, the open file handle leaks until GC/__del__ reclaims it. The defect is real. Severity stays minor: it only fires on the error path (misconfigured names/units), and GPSFromNetCDF wraps a single GPS reference file so it is normally constructed once per config rather than per data file — the description's "batch across many files / repeated retries" amplification is somewhat speculative, but the missing try/finally is genuine.

title: Hotel _load_netcdf leaks Dataset when _parse_time or a variable read raises
description: `ds = nc.Dataset(...)` (line 280) is only explicitly closed before raising in the per-channel-time branch (line 301). If `_parse_time` raises (line 282, e.g. an unknown time_format reaching here) or a data-variable read/astype fails (line 293), the function raises with the Dataset still open. Cleanup is inconsistent — one error path closes, the others don't.
suggested_fix: Use `with nc.Dataset(str(path), 'r') as ds:` so all error paths close the handle, and drop the manual ds.close() calls.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed by reading src/odas_tpw/perturb/hotel.py:271-309. _load_netcdf opens ds = nc.Dataset(str(path), "r") at line 280 without a context manager. The handle is only closed at line 306 (normal path) and line 301 (the per-channel-time "column not found" branch, immediately before its raise). Several intervening operations can raise while ds is open and is NOT closed: (a) line 281 ds.variables[time_column] raises KeyError if the default time variable is missing; (b) line 282 _parse_time can raise — verified at hotel.py:169 it raises ValueError("Unknown time_format") for an unrecognized format, and the "iso"/"auto" branches call pd.to_datetime and .astype which can raise on bad data; (c) line 293 var[:].data.astype(np.float64) can raise on read/dtype errors. In all these cases the function propagates the exception with ds still open, leaking the netCDF4 handle. Cleanup is genuinely inconsistent: one error path (line 301) closes, the others do not. I verified the only caller, load_hotel (hotel.py:189-231), has no try/except, and its caller pipeline.py:1790-1797 also does not wrap the call, so nothing upstream closes the handle. The other loaders (_load_csv via pandas, _load_mat via scipy.loadmat) do not hold persistent file handles, so the leak is specific to the NetCDF path. Severity is minor: it requires an error path (malformed/mismatched hotel NetCDF) to trigger, and a leaked handle is reclaimed at process exit; impact is real only for long-running or repeated-call usage. The suggested fix — use `with nc.Dataset(str(path), "r") as ds:` and drop the manual ds.close() calls — is correct and idiomatic (netCDF4.Dataset supports the context-manager protocol).

title: GPSFromCSV/NetCDF: single-row GPS file silently yields all-NaN positions with no warning
description: A GPS file with a single time/lat/lon row builds an interp1d with one point; scipy then returns NaN for every query (confirmed: `interp1d([5.],[10.],fill_value='extrapolate')(3.0)` -> nan with a RuntimeWarning). Unlike interpolate_hotel, which guards `hotel_t.size < 2` and skips (hotel.py:441-442), GPSFromCSV/GPSFromNetCDF have no size guard and no warning, so a degenerate single-fix GPS file produces all-NaN positions silently rather than failing loudly or falling back to a fixed position.
suggested_fix: Validate that the GPS record has >= 2 finite time samples in __init__; raise a clear error or warn and degrade to a GPSFixed-like single value.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed by reading the code and empirically testing scipy. GPSFromCSV.__init__ (gps.py:178-189) and GPSFromNetCDF.__init__ (gps.py:287-288) build interp1d(t, ..., fill_value="extrapolate") with no guard on the number of samples. Verified: interp1d([5.],[10.], bounds_error=False, fill_value="extrapolate") constructs silently and returns nan for every query (including at the data point itself), emitting only a generic RuntimeWarning ("invalid value encountered in divide") from the zero-width slope division. So a single-row GPS file silently produces all-NaN positions. The contrast with interpolate_hotel is real: hotel.py:441-442 explicitly guards `if hotel_t.size < 2: continue`, while the GPS providers have no analogous size check and create_gps (gps.py:303-340) does no row-count validation. The all-NaN positions propagate downstream: ctd.py:344-345 calls gps.lat/lon and ctd.py:351 feeds them into add_seawater_properties (TEOS-10 absolute-salinity conversion) plus the file geospatial attributes, so corruption is silent rather than loud. Test coverage gap confirmed: tests/test_perturb_gps.py only exercises 3-row inputs, no single-row/degenerate case. Severity minor is correct: trigger requires a malformed single-fix GPS file (operator error, not a normal input) and the consequence is silent NaN corruption rather than a crash; it is a legitimate robustness/error-handling gap, not a false positive.

title: Hotel 'cubic' interp with exactly 3 samples raises due to tuple fill_value
description: _interp_one passes `fill_value=(data[0], data[-1])` to interp1d for all non-pchip kinds, including 'cubic'/'quadratic' which are accepted by _INTERP_KINDS. For a cubic spline, scipy interprets a 2-tuple fill_value as boundary derivatives when the point count is small; with exactly 3 hotel samples this raises `ValueError: The number of derivatives at boundaries does not match` (confirmed for n=3 cubic; n>=4 is fine). A user configuring `interp: cubic` on a sparse 3-sample channel hits a hard failure instead of edge-clamped extrapolation.
suggested_fix: For spline kinds, set bounds_error=False with `fill_value=np.nan` and clamp edges separately (as done for pchip), or require >=4 points for cubic and document the constraint.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed by reading the code and empirically reproducing the failure. In src/odas_tpw/perturb/hotel.py:401-405, _interp_one passes fill_value=(data[0], data[-1]) (a 2-tuple) to scipy interp1d for every non-pchip kind, including 'cubic'. 'cubic' is a valid, user-selectable kind: it is in _INTERP_KINDS (line 43) and config validation (lines 124-128) only rejects kinds NOT in that set, so `interp: cubic` (per-channel) or `interpolation: cubic` (global) is fully supported. The only size guard before _interp_one is hotel_t.size >= 2 (line 441), so a 3-sample channel reaches _interp_one. I reproduced the bug on the installed scipy 1.17.1: interp1d(t, d, kind='cubic', bounds_error=False, fill_value=(d[0], d[-1])) with n=3 raises ValueError: 'The number of derivatives at boundaries does not match: expected 1, got 0+0', because scipy reinterprets the 2-tuple as below/above boundary derivative specs for the cubic spline path. n>=4 works, and quadratic/slinear are unaffected at n=3. So a user configuring cubic interpolation on a sparse 3-sample hotel channel hits a hard crash instead of the intended edge-clamped extrapolation. One inaccuracy in the finding: it lists 'quadratic' alongside cubic, but quadratic does NOT raise at n=3 in my test — only cubic does; the core cubic/n=3 claim is correct. Severity minor: real crash but requires the non-default cubic kind (default is pchip, unaffected) combined with the narrow exactly-3-samples condition; not a default-path failure.

title: _to_epoch_seconds / GPSFromCSV mishandle missing/string timestamps
description: Two related gaps in the CSV path: (1) `_to_epoch_seconds` converts a datetime64 NaT to INT64_MIN/1e9 (~ -9.2e9 s) rather than NaN (confirmed), so a missing timestamp would poison interp1d's x-axis and make _t_min ~ -9.2e9, breaking both interpolation ordering and the coverage-warning math. (2) GPSFromCSV reads with `pd.read_csv` (no parse_dates), so a human-readable ISO-string time column stays object/str dtype, is not datetime64, and `_to_epoch_seconds`'s `.astype(np.float64)` raises 'could not convert string to float' (confirmed). The module docstring implies datetime columns are supported, but only an already-numeric epoch column or an already-datetime64 column works; ISO-string CSVs crash and NaT becomes garbage. Hotel's CSV loader, by contrast, exposes a time_format='iso' option.
suggested_fix: Map NaT to NaN in _to_epoch_seconds, and give GPSFromCSV a time_format option (or attempt pd.to_datetime on non-numeric columns) so ISO-string GPS CSVs parse instead of raising.
verify_reasoning:
Both technical claims in the finding are empirically confirmed by running the actual code from src/odas_tpw/perturb/gps.py.

CLAIM 1 (NaT mishandling) — REAL. _to_epoch_seconds (gps.py:29-30) does `values.astype("datetime64[ns]").astype(np.int64) / 1e9`. A datetime64 NaT casts to INT64_MIN, yielding -9223372036.854776 s (~-9.2e9), NOT NaN. Verified: np.isnan() of the result is False, so np.nanmin/np.nanmax at gps.py:175-176 do NOT filter it — _t_min becomes ~-9.2e9. That poisons (a) the interp1d x-axis (gps.py:178-189: a garbage, out-of-order x value), and (b) the coverage-warning math in _warn_outside_coverage (gps.py:215-216). The module docstring (gps.py:7-9) explicitly states the interpolator "normalizes datetime64 columns to epoch seconds," so a datetime64 GPS column is a documented-supported input, and a missing entry (NaT) is a realistic occurrence. This is a genuine latent bug producing silent garbage rather than NaN.

CLAIM 2 (ISO-string CSV crashes) — REAL but contractually borderline. GPSFromCSV uses pd.read_csv with no parse_dates (gps.py:173), so an ISO-string time column stays str/object dtype; _to_epoch_seconds then hits the numeric branch `values.astype(np.float64)` (gps.py:31) which raises ValueError: could not convert string to float (verified end-to-end via GPSFromCSV). Mitigating: the GPSFromCSV class docstring (gps.py:154) says the time column is "expected in epoch seconds," and the GPS config docs (docs/perturb/configuration.md:48) offer no time_format option for GPS (unlike hotel, which has time_format auto|seconds|epoch|iso and a working _parse_time at hotel.py:138-169). So ISO CSVs are out-of-contract for GPS, and the failure is a loud ValueError, not silent corruption. The finding's framing that "the module docstring implies datetime columns are supported" is only half-true: the datetime64 normalization path exists but is unreachable from the CSV loader because read_csv never yields datetime64 without parse_dates.

Net: a real error-handling/robustness gap. The happy path (numeric epoch-seconds CSV) works and is tested (tests/test_perturb_gps.py:104-179). Severity minor is appropriate — the NaT half is the substantive defect (silent garbage on a documented-supported datetime64 input); the ISO half is a usability inconsistency with hotel. The suggested fixes (map NaT->NaN; add a time_format/pd.to_datetime path) are sound and match hotel.py's existing approach.

title: canonicalize/compute_hash crash on NaN or inf float config values
description: _normalize_value does `if v == int(v)` for any float. int(float('nan')) raises ValueError and int(float('inf')) raises OverflowError. A config value of `.nan` or `.inf` (e.g. `gps.lat: .nan`, which ruamel.yaml parses to a float NaN) therefore crashes canonicalize() -> compute_hash() -> resolve_output_dir() with an opaque 'cannot convert float NaN to integer' error instead of a clear validation message. Verified end-to-end: `odas_tpw.perturb.config.canonicalize('gps', {'lat': float('nan')})` raises ValueError. gps participates in the profiles upstream hash (pipeline.py:98,118 via _upstream_for), so the crash path is reachable and aborts output-dir resolution. Low severity because it requires an unusual config value.
suggested_fix: In _normalize_value, guard the float branch: `if math.isfinite(v): if v == int(v): return int(v); return round(v, 10)` and return the float (or its repr) unchanged for non-finite values, so hashing degrades gracefully instead of crashing.
verify_reasoning: Verified by reading the code and reproducing end-to-end. config_base.py:29 (`if v == int(v)`) runs for any float, and int(float('nan')) raises ValueError, int(float('inf')) raises OverflowError. Direct call to _normalize_value(float('nan')) raises 'ValueError: cannot convert float NaN to integer'; inf/-inf raise OverflowError. The crash propagates through canonicalize() -> compute_hash() -> resolve_output_dir() (config_base.py:178/188/208), all reached from perturb/pipeline.py:1545,1555,... The gps section is part of the upstream hash chain (_upstream_for at pipeline.py:80,98,118), and merge_config (config_base.py:140) only filters None — a NaN/inf lat/lon survives into the hash input. validate_config (config_base.py:93) only checks unknown keys/sections, never value finiteness, so there is no guard. Confirmed ruamel.yaml (the real loader at config_base.py:84) parses `.nan`->float('nan') and `.inf`->float('inf'), so a user YAML like `gps: {source: fixed, lat: .nan, lon: .inf}` reaches the crash. Repro through merge_config+canonicalize raises 'ValueError: cannot convert float NaN to integer', an opaque error instead of a clear validation message. Severity correctly minor: requires an unusual/invalid config value (NaN/inf coordinates), produces an opaque crash rather than data corruption or silent wrong results. Note the same defect also affects _normalize_nested (dynamic_key_sections) since it delegates to _normalize_value, so any non-finite float in config triggers it, but gps.lat/lon is the most concretely reachable case.

title: QC gate silently mis-samples a non-slow-length drop_from channel
description: compute_segment_drop computes segment boundary indices lo/hi via searchsorted on pf.t_slow, then slices `arr_u[a:b]` with no check that pf.channels[name] is the same length as t_slow. If a drop_from channel (epsilon_drop_from / chi_drop_from) is actually fast-rate or otherwise mismatched in length, the slow-grid indices select the wrong time region (or an empty/partial slice), so the segment OR-reduce samples the wrong data and silently under- or over-flags segments. The qc_rules path explicitly pads/truncates rule arrays to n_slow (qc_rules.py:329-335) so rule-generated channels are safe, but hotel-injected channels named directly in *_drop_from bypass that and are not validated here. The docstring assumes slow rate but nothing enforces it.
suggested_fix: Before the per-segment loop, skip-and-warn (or raise) when `arr.shape[0] != t_slow.size`, e.g. `if arr.shape[0] != len(t_slow): logger.warning(...); continue`.
verify_reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL. In src/odas_tpw/perturb/qc_gate.py:compute_segment_drop, segment boundary indices lo/hi are computed via np.searchsorted on pf.t_slow (lines 66,73-74), then used to slice pf.channels[name] as arr_u[a:b] (line 90) with no validation that the channel length equals len(t_slow).

A drop_from channel CAN legitimately be fast-rate. hotel.py:merge_hotel_into_pfile (lines 466-488) interpolates each hotel channel onto pf.t_fast or pf.t_slow depending on is_fast (interpolate_hotel line 434/436), and registers it on pf.channels under its output name. The hotel schema explicitly exposes a per-variable `fast` option (hotel.py:20-21) and fast_channels defaults to {"speed","P"}. So a fast hotel channel has length len(pf.t_fast). Per p_file.py:470-471, t_fast = arange(matrix_count*n_rows)/fs_fast while t_slow = arange(matrix_count)/fs_slow, with n_rows=8, so t_fast is ~8x longer than t_slow.

The same merged pf (created pipeline.py:850, hotel-merged 859) is passed to apply_qc_to_dataset (pipeline.py:1174-1175, 1299-1300), which calls compute_segment_drop with that pf. The qc config (config.py:159-160, docstring 149-155) lets *_drop_from name any channel and never requires slow rate. The pipeline itself even creates a fast channel `speed_fast` and adds it to pf._fast_channels (pipeline.py:874-875).

Impact: because the fast array is LONGER than t_slow, slow-grid indices a,b in [0,n_slow] are valid indices into the fast array (no IndexError) — silent mis-sampling. A segment centered at slow-time T (slow index ~T*fs_slow) indexes fast samples ~T*fs_slow, corresponding to fast-time ~T/8, i.e. a region ~8x earlier in the record. The OR-reduce then samples the wrong window and silently over/under-flags. The only existing guard, `if a >= b: continue` (line 88), catches empty slices only, not length mismatch.

The finding's distinction is correct: qc_rules-generated channels are padded/truncated to n_slow (qc_rules.py:329-336) so they are safe; only hotel-injected (or pipeline-derived fast) channels named directly in *_drop_from bypass that protection. The codebase elsewhere guards exactly this fast-length mismatch (pipeline.py:279-284 truncates/pads a fast channel to len(pf.t_fast)), showing awareness — qc_gate.py lacks the equivalent check.

Severity MINOR is appropriate: real silent correctness bug, but it only manifests under a specific misconfiguration (naming a fast-rate channel in *_drop_from). Typical QC drop channels are slow boolean bitfields, so it is an edge case rather than a default-path failure. No crash; degraded QC flagging when misconfigured. The suggested fix (skip-and-warn or raise when arr.shape[0] != t_slow.size) is sound.

title: Float/fractional drop_from channel loses flags via astype(uint8) truncation
description: `arr_u = arr.astype(np.uint8, copy=False)` is applied before the bitwise OR-reduce. Hotel channels are loaded as float64 (hotel.py:405) and, with the default interpolation 'pchip' (config.py:46), a 0/1 flag channel is smeared into fractional values (e.g. 0.5 between flagged/unflagged samples). astype(uint8) truncates fractional values toward zero, so 0.4-0.9 become 0 and flags are silently dropped (under-flagging). Conversely inf/large values cast to 255 (all bits set), over-flagging. The docstring states channels are 'expected to be uint8 CF bitfields', but nothing enforces integer/nearest handling, and all QC-gate tests use only pre-cast uint8 arrays (test_perturb_qc_gate.py), so this path is untested. Affects only the hotel-injected drop_from path; qc_rules channels are already uint8.
suggested_fix: Round/clip before casting, e.g. flag the segment on any sample with abs(arr)>=0.5 rather than a raw uint8 cast, and document that flag channels should use a step interpolation (nearest/previous), or reject float drop_from channels.
verify_reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL. At src/odas_tpw/perturb/qc_gate.py:85, `arr_u = arr.astype(np.uint8, copy=False)` is applied to each drop_from channel before the bitwise-OR reduce. The float drop_from path is genuinely reachable: the config (config.py:149-151) and the qc_gate module docstring explicitly document naming a "hotel-injected channel (uint8 bitfield or boolean)" in `*_drop_from`. Hotel channels are always materialized as float64 — `_interp_one` returns `np.asarray(..., dtype=np.float64)` at hotel.py:400 and 405, and `merge_hotel_into_pfile` (hotel.py:473) stores those float64 arrays on `pf.channels`. The default interpolation is `pchip` (config.py:46), and PchipInterpolator produces fractional values (verified: 0.156, 0.5, 0.844) at flag-region boundaries when the slow grid is finer than the hotel grid. `astype(np.uint8)` truncates all values in [0,1) to 0 (verified numerically), so boundary/fill-in samples lose their flag bit — under-flagging that narrows a flagged region. The inf→255 over-flagging claim is also confirmed.

Two scoping/accuracy corrections to the finding, which lower its real-world severity from what the text implies: (1) The finding's claim that "large values cast to 255" is WRONG — uint8 cast is modulo-256 wraparound, not saturation (300.0 -> 44, verified); only inf/nan and values in [255,256) map to 255. (2) The "flags are silently dropped" framing overstates impact: PchipInterpolator is shape-preserving and passes through original data points, so any hotel sample landing on the slow grid (and an isolated lone flag's peak) retains value 1.0 and survives the cast; only the interpolated fractional fill-in samples between grid points are lost. Combined with the per-segment OR-reduce over a multi-sample window, the practical effect is narrowing a flagged region by up to ~1 hotel sample-interval at each edge, not wholesale flag loss. The other drop_from source (qc_rules.py:336) is already uint8 and unaffected.

The test-gap claim is confirmed: every flag channel in tests/test_perturb_qc_gate.py is constructed with dtype=np.uint8 (lines 60,72,73,83,102,117,141,167); no float/fractional drop_from channel is exercised, so line 85's cast is untested.

Severity confirmed as minor: a real correctness defect on a reachable, untested, opt-in path (requires hotel-injected flag channels referenced in *_drop_from, which is an advanced/empty-by-default config), with bounded impact (edge-of-region under-flagging) rather than systematic flag loss.

title: fp07_cal _compute_RT_R0 default adc_fs (5) disagrees with channels.py (4.096)
description: _compute_RT_R0 defaults adc_fs to 5 and e_b to 0, while the authoritative therm converter in rsi/channels.py:91,97 defaults adc_fs to 4.096 and requires e_b (defaulting to 0.68 with a warning). When a channel config is missing adc_fs, the in-situ calibration computes ln(R_T/R_0) on a different voltage scale than the factory conversion, biasing the Steinhart-Hart fit. When e_b is missing, the `(G*E_B)==0` guard (line 66) skips the 2/(G*E_B) scaling entirely and silently produces a wrong-scale Z with no warning. In practice the same RSI config supplies both code paths so the keys are normally present, hence minor; but the silent divergent defaults are a latent inconsistency.
suggested_fix: Share a single therm-conversion helper (or constants) between channels.py and fp07_cal.py so adc_fs/e_b defaults and the missing-key warnings match, rather than maintaining two divergent default sets.
verify_reasoning:
The factual claims are all confirmed by reading the code:
- fp07_cal.py:58 defaults adc_fs to 5; channels.py:91 defaults adc_fs to "4.096" (divergent).
- fp07_cal.py:54 defaults e_b to 0 (no warning); channels.py:97 uses _require_float(params,"e_b",0.68,"therm") which WARNS on a missing key (channels.py:31-37). So the defaults and the missing-key handling genuinely diverge.
- fp07_cal.py:66 has `factor = factor*2/(G*E_B) if (G*E_B)!=0 else factor`, so when e_b defaults to 0, the 2/(G*E_B) scaling is silently dropped with no warning. Confirmed.
- The same RSI config (pf.config["channels"], via _get_channel_config at line 226) feeds both code paths, so in practice the keys are present — confirming the finding's own "minor in practice" framing.

However, I downgrade blocker/major-sounding parts to a nit because the "biases the Steinhart-Hart fit" mechanism is overstated. _compute_RT_R0 builds the REGRESSOR RT_R0 (lines 246/307), and the SAME ch_config is reused when applying the calibration in _apply_cal (line 312). The lstsq (line 308) fits polynomial coefficients of RT_R0 against the true reference temperature 1/(T_ref+273.15). A consistently wrong/defaulted adc_fs (or a dropped E_B scaling) is applied identically to both the fit and the apply paths, so the fitted coefficients self-consistently absorb the scale; the final calibrated temperature is anchored to T_ref, not to the factory convert_therm scale. So there is no actual temperature bias from the divergent default in the normal in-situ flow — only a genuine latent maintainability hazard: two divergent default sets and a silent (warning-less) guard that could mask a config defect that channels.py would have flagged. The defect is real (divergent defaults + silent guard), but its practical impact is essentially cosmetic/maintainability, hence nit rather than minor. Confidence medium: defaults/guard verified directly; the "no real numerical bias" conclusion depends on the fit-and-apply symmetry argument, which holds for the standard path but I cannot exhaustively rule out every caller.

title: Malformed (non-mapping) config section raises opaque TypeError/ValueError
description: load_config builds `{str(k): (dict(v) if v is not None else {}) for k, v in raw.items()}`. If a user writes a section as a scalar (`epsilon: 5`) or a list, `dict(v)` raises 'int object is not iterable' / 'dictionary update sequence element ... length 1' before validate_config runs, giving a confusing error rather than a clear 'section must be a mapping' message. Verified with ruamel-parsed scalar and list section values.
suggested_fix: Validate that each section value is a mapping (or None) before calling dict(), raising a clear ValueError naming the offending section.
verify_reasoning: Verified empirically against the actual code. config_base.py:89 builds `{str(k): (dict(v) if v is not None else {}) for k, v in raw.items()}` from the ruamel.yaml-parsed mapping, then calls validate_config on line 90. I reproduced the parse+dict() step with ruamel.yaml: a scalar section (`epsilon: 5`) yields `TypeError: 'int' object is not iterable`; a list section (`epsilon: [1,2]`) yields `TypeError: object is not iterable`; a list of single-key mappings yields `ValueError: dictionary update sequence element #0 has length 1; 2 is required`. All occur at line 89 before validate_config (which only checks section names and keys, not the mapping-ness of the value) runs, so the user gets an opaque exception rather than a clear "section must be a mapping" message. The path is user-reachable: rsi/cli.py:86 and perturb/cli.py:33 pass the user-supplied -c/--config YAML path straight into load_config (perturb wraps it at config.py:284 but calls _mgr.load_config first, so the same failure occurs before _validate_instruments). No upstream guard prevents a non-mapping section from reaching line 89. Correctly classified as a nit: it is purely error-message quality — the code still fails loudly with no silent corruption or wrong results, and the trigger is only a hand-edited malformed config. The description and reproduction are accurate.

title: Tie-nudge epsilon corrupts column_clusters median, spuriously splitting evenly-spaced casts
description: plot_columns() first calls strictly_increasing() (line 233), which nudges every tied/non-increasing x apart by ~1e-3 of the median spacing, then passes the *nudged* x to column_clusters() (line 237). column_clusters computes the cluster-break threshold as gap_factor * median(diff(x)[diff>0]) (layout.py:166-170). After nudging, the artificial ~1e-3 epsilon spacings are real positive diffs and dominate the median when ties are common, collapsing the threshold toward zero. Genuine cast-to-cast spacings then exceed gap_factor*median and each real cast is split into its own singleton cluster (rendered as a lone column with blank gaps between), even though they form one continuous transect. Reproduced: x=[0]*20 + [1,2,3,4,5] yields median positive diff = 0.001, threshold 0.004, and clusters [(0,20),(20,21),(21,22),(22,23),(23,24),(24,25)] — the five real 1-km-spaced casts each become a separate mesh instead of one. This happens whenever ties (re-occupied stations on a lat/lon/distance/signed_distance axis, the exact case strictly_increasing was added to handle) coexist with normally-spaced casts. The existing test test_plot_columns_tied_x_renders only asserts no crash on [10,10,11]; it does not exercise ties mixed with real spacing, so the bug is untested.
suggested_fix: Compute the clusters on the original (pre-nudge) finite x (median of its strictly-positive diffs, which excludes ties), then apply strictly_increasing only for the per-cluster edge geometry. I.e. call column_clusters(x) before strictly_increasing, or have column_clusters ignore sub-threshold (tie-epsilon-sized) diffs when computing the median spacing.
verify_reasoning:
Verified by reading layout.py and reproducing. plot_columns (layout.py:233) calls strictly_increasing(x) BEFORE column_clusters(x) (layout.py:237). strictly_increasing (176-194) nudges tied/non-increasing values apart by eps = 1e-3 * median(positive diffs). column_clusters (153-173) then computes med = median(diff(x)[diff>0]) and breaks where diff > gap_factor*med (default 4.0). When many ties coexist with normally-spaced casts, the nudged ~1e-3 gaps dominate the median, collapsing the threshold toward zero so every genuine cast-to-cast gap exceeds it.

Reproduced exactly the cited case: x=[0]*20+[1,2,3,4,5] -> nudged median positive diff = 0.001, threshold = 0.004, clusters = [(0,20),(20,21),(21,22),(22,23),(23,24),(24,25)] instead of the correct single [(0,25)]. The five real 1-unit-spaced casts each render as a separate one-wide mesh and the 20 tied casts collapse into a 0.019-wide sliver, fragmenting one continuous transect.

Input is realistic, not degenerate: plot_columns is called at profiles.py:234 with xs from spatial axes (latitude/longitude/distance_from_point/signed_distance; xaxis.py:301-331), where re-occupied stations yield identical x for multiple casts — the exact case strictly_increasing's own docstring (lines 180-181) was written to handle. No upstream guard prevents this; finite/sorted xs (profiles.py:174-179) does not remove ties.

Test gap confirmed: test_plot_columns_tied_x_renders (tests/test_perturb_plot_profiles.py:165-180) only asserts pcm is not None on [10,10,11]; that input gives median positive diff ~0.5, threshold ~2.0, no break, so it passes trivially and never exercises ties mixed with real spacing.

Suggested fix is sound: clustering on the original pre-nudge x (column_clusters([0]*20+[1..5]) = [(0,25)]) yields the correct single cluster.

Severity kept at major (not blocker): this corrupts the rendered figure (mesh segmentation / blank gaps), making a continuous transect look fragmented, but does not corrupt any numerical/scientific output. The candidate's 'major' rating is correct.

title: eps_chi gamma = chi/eps assumes equal bin counts; broadcast ValueError with diss_width != chi_width
description: gamma = np.where((eps > 0) & np.isfinite(chi), chi / eps, np.nan) divides the chi grid by the eps grid element-wise. In the combo path _load_chi_from_combo returns the chi-combo's own depth axis but the caller discards it (line 217: `_depth_chi` unused) and never re-bins chi onto the eps depth grid; the realignment block (lines 233-263) only reconciles the *profile* (column) axis, never the *bin* axis. The pipeline allows diss and chi to be binned at independent widths (pipeline.py:1893 diss_width vs 1905 chi_width, each separately configurable). When a run uses different widths, eps is (n_eps_bin, P) and chi is (n_chi_bin, P) with n_eps_bin != n_chi_bin, so chi/eps raises 'operands could not be broadcast together' and the figure aborts. With default config the two widths match so it works, which masks the latent assumption. (File predates PR#73 but is in the reviewed set.)
suggested_fix: Re-bin chiMean onto the eps depth grid (as the legacy per-profile path already does via depth_edges(depth)) instead of discarding _depth_chi, or assert/raise a clear error when the chi-combo and diss-combo bin coordinates differ rather than letting numpy raise an opaque broadcast error.
verify_reasoning:
Confirmed by reading the code. In eps_chi.py the combo path loads the chi-combo's own depth axis at line 217 (`t_chi, _depth_chi, chi, ...`) and immediately discards `_depth_chi` (grep confirms it is never referenced again). The realignment block (lines 233-263) is gated on a profile-count/time mismatch (line 233) and only reconciles the profile/column axis: line 245 allocates `aligned = np.full((chi.shape[0], len(t_eps)), np.nan)`, explicitly preserving `chi.shape[0]` (the bin row axis) and only resizing columns. There is no reindex/interp/assert between load and the divide. Line 297 then does `gamma = np.where((eps>0)&np.isfinite(chi), chi/eps, np.nan)`, an element-wise divide of eps (n_eps_bin, P) by chi (n_chi_bin, P). When the bin axes differ in length numpy raises 'operands could not be broadcast together' and the figure aborts.

The bin axes can legitimately differ: bin_by_depth (binning.py:431-505) builds bin_centers independently for each combo from its own bin_width and its own global depth range [g_min,g_max]. diss_width (pipeline.py:1893) and chi_width (pipeline.py:1905) are independently configurable, each falling back to bin_width. So unequal lengths occur when (a) the two widths are configured differently, OR — more broadly than the candidate states — (b) widths are equal but chi vs diss profiles span different depth extents (e.g. FP07 calibration drops/trims some chi profiles), since the grid floor/ceil to the data range. Either way chi.shape[0] != eps.shape[0].

The bug is masked under default config because config.py:170,172 set diss_width/chi_width to None, collapsing both to the shared bin_width, and tests/test_perturb_plot_cli.py uses n_bin=5 for both fixtures, so the unequal-bin path is never exercised. Could not refute: no guard, the divergent-width input is reachable via documented config, and the depth-range divergence makes it reachable even with the default equal width. Severity major (not blocker): it does not fire under default config, but when it does the whole figure aborts with an opaque numpy error and no fallback. Note: third-party caveat does not apply — this file is in src/odas_tpw/perturb/plot, not pyturb.

title: N2 forced to LogNorm silently masks negative (overturning/unstable) stratification
description: N2 is listed in _LOG_VARS but not in _DIVERGING, so _make_norm renders it with LogNorm. layout.quantile_limits and the LogNorm path filter to strictly-positive values, so any negative N2 cell (statically unstable / actively overturning water — often the most interesting feature in a microstructure section) is dropped from the color limits and then masked to the set_bad grey, indistinguishable from unsampled water. Confirmed: a grid mixing +/-1e-4 N2 yields a LogNorm with vmin>0 and the negative cells masked. dTdz, the other gradient field, is correctly treated as diverging; N2 is not.
suggested_fix: Either treat N2 as a diverging/symlog field (so negative stratification is visible and distinct from no-data), or use SymLogNorm; at minimum document that negative N2 is hidden so users do not mistake overturning regions for unsampled bins.
verify_reasoning: Verified by reading the code and reproducing the rendering. (1) In src/odas_tpw/perturb/plot/profiles.py:78-83, N2 is in _LOG_VARS but not _DIVERGING, while the sibling gradient field dTdz IS in _DIVERGING — the asymmetry the finding describes is exactly present. (2) _make_norm (profiles.py:120-135) routes N2 to LogNorm via layout.quantile_limits, which filters to finite & z>0 (layout.py:47), producing vmin>0. plot_columns (layout.py:240) wraps the data only in np.ma.masked_invalid (masks NaN/inf, not negatives), but LogNorm itself masks negative finite values; cmap.set_bad('0.85') (profiles.py:233) then colors them. (3) Reproduced empirically: a negative (overturning) N2 cell and a NaN (unsampled) cell both render identical RGBA [0.85,0.85,0.85] — visually indistinguishable. (4) Refutation attempt on whether negative N2 can occur: the combo product's N2 comes from _stable_window (mixing.py:292-319), which Thorpe-sorts parcels by gsw.sigma0 (surface-referenced potential density) before calling gsw.Nsquared (mid-pressure in-situ). I tested 200k realistic density-sorted pairs and ~0.6% yield negative Nsquared due to the sigma0-vs-in-situ (thermobaric) reference mismatch, so the sort does NOT guarantee N2>=0. No clamping of N2 exists anywhere (grep of mixing.py/pipeline.py), and the schema long_name 'buoyancy frequency squared (Thorpe-sorted)' plus docstring 'N2 > 0 is stable stratification' (mixing.py:142) confirm N2<=0 is a representable, documented state. So the defect is real: negative N2 cells are silently masked to the same grey as unsampled water. Severity remains 'minor': it is a visualization-only issue (no data corruption), and the finding's claim that overturning is 'often the most interesting feature' overstates frequency for THIS pipeline — N2 is computed between Thorpe-sorted half-window means, which strongly suppresses overturns, so negative cells are rare residuals rather than raw overturns. Still, when they occur they are hidden and confusable with no-data, matching the report.

title: Reversed --clim (MIN > MAX) silently inverts linear/diverging color scales instead of erroring
description: The log-variable branch of _make_norm guards against lo >= hi and raises SystemExit (profiles.py:125-128), but the diverging branch (113-118) and the plain-linear branch (137-138) pass clim straight into grid.linear_limits and then Normalize(vmin=lo, vmax=hi) with no ordering check. A user typo like `--clim T1 10 5` produces Normalize(vmin=10, vmax=5); matplotlib 3.10 does not raise — it silently renders an inverted color scale — so the mistake is invisible. This is inconsistent with the log path which rejects the same mistake, and with single_var_limit_guard's general intent to fail loudly on bad limits.
suggested_fix: Apply the same lo < hi validation to the diverging and linear branches (or centrally before dispatch) so a reversed --clim is a clear SystemExit rather than a silently inverted colorbar.
verify_reasoning:
Verified by reading the code and exercising the real render path. The log branch of _make_norm (src/odas_tpw/perturb/plot/profiles.py:125-128) explicitly raises SystemExit when lo >= hi, but the diverging branch (113-118) and plain-linear branch (137-138) do not. Traced the data flow: with a per-variable --clim, parse_clim (sections.py:248-258) stores (lo, hi) with no ordering check; grid.linear_limits (grid.py:108-112) returns the provided overrides verbatim when both are not None; for a diverging var, `explicit` is True (name in clim), so the symmetric -m..m path is skipped and the function returns Normalize(vmin=10, vmax=5); the linear branch likewise returns Normalize(vmin=10, vmax=5). single_var_limit_guard (sections.py:340-346) only rejects --vmin/--vmax with multiple vars and never validates ordering, so it does not catch this. No upstream guard exists.

Runtime check on the installed matplotlib 3.10.8: the actual code path is layout.plot_columns -> ax.pcolormesh(..., norm=norm) + fig.colorbar (layout.py:239-244). I reproduced this exact path: it renders with no error and produces an inverted colorbar (colorbar runs 10 at bottom to 5 at top; a mid value maps to the inverted fraction). So the candidate's central claim holds: a reversed --clim is silently accepted on diverging/linear variables and yields a misleading inverted color scale, inconsistent with the log branch which fails loudly. (Note: scalar Normalize.__call__(7.0) does raise 'minvalue must be less than or equal to maxvalue', but that scalar path is never exercised here; the array path inside pcolormesh does not raise, so the finding's 'does not raise' conclusion is correct for this code.)

Severity: minor is appropriate -- it is a usability/consistency defect that yields a misleading plot, not a data-corruption or computation bug; no numbers are wrong, only the colorbar orientation.

title: Magnetometer channels misclassified: code checks type 'mag' but converter registers 'magn'
description: _classify_channels selects magnetometer channels with `pf.channel_info[n]["type"] == "mag"`, but the ODAS channel type and the CONVERTERS registry key are both "magn" (channels.py:309 `"magn": convert_magn`; ODAS odas_p2mat.m:1056 uses `'type','magn'`). channel_info["type"] is set to the config type string ('magn') at p_file.py:534. Because 'magn' != 'mag', mag_names is always empty: the ATOMIX MAG variable (with standard_name=magnetic_field, units=micro_Tesla, sensor_names, on N_MAG_SENSORS/TIME_SLOW) is never written, and the N_MAG_SENSORS dimension is never created (convert.py:502). Magnetometer data instead silently falls through to the supplementary block (written as a generic f4 variable named after the channel with sensor_type='magn'), losing the CF-standard role/metadata that downstream ATOMIX consumers expect. No test exercises this classification path (tests only call convert_magn directly).
suggested_fix: Change the comparison at convert.py:64 to `== "magn"` to match the converter/config type string.
verify_reasoning: Verified by reading the actual code. _classify_channels (src/odas_tpw/rsi/convert.py:64) selects magnetometer channels with pf.channel_info[n]["type"] == "mag", but channel_info["type"] is set at p_file.py:522/534 to info.get("type","raw").strip().lower() — i.e. the raw config type string, lowercased only, with no renaming. The ODAS magnetometer config type is "magn", which is also the CONVERTERS registry key (channels.py:309 "magn": convert_magn) and the key used to find and apply convert_magn (returning units "uT"). Since "magn" != "mag", mag_names is always empty. Downstream this means: the MAG variable (convert.py:352-362, standard_name=magnetic_field, sensor_names, dims N_MAG_SENSORS/TIME_SLOW) is written with data=None and skipped, the N_MAG_SENSORS dimension is never created (convert.py:502-503), and because the channel is absent from `mapped` (built from the empty mag_names at convert.py:85) it falls through to the supplementary block (convert.py:102), losing the CF/ATOMIX magnetic-field role metadata. The suggested fix (compare to "magn") is correct and matches both the converter key and config string. The claim that no test exercises this path holds: tests/test_hard_batch_branches.py:275 asserts ch["mag"]==[] only for an empty stub PFile (passes regardless of the bug), and test_channels.py:763-781 calls convert_magn / checks CONVERTERS directly without going through _classify_channels. Kept at major rather than blocker because triggering requires a processed instrument config that actually declares a magn channel; the documented SN 479 ARCTERX dataset is not stated to carry a magnetometer, so impact is conditional on hardware presence, but the string-mismatch defect itself is unambiguous and would silently drop CF-standard magnetometer output whenever one is present.

title: convert_therm divides by zero when beta_2/beta_3 present but empty in config
description: convert_therm guards higher-order Steinhart-Hart terms with `if beta_2 is not None`. parse_config maps a config line like `beta_2 =` (key present, no value) to the empty string "", which is not None, so the branch executes `inv_T += (1.0/_safe_float(beta_2)) * log_R**2`. _safe_float("") returns the 0.0 default, producing 1.0/0.0 -> inf (RuntimeWarning), which then corrupts inv_T and yields inf/NaN temperatures silently. Same hazard for beta_3 at channels.py:119. The two-coefficient main term is protected because beta_1 routes through _require_float/_safe_float with a nonzero default, but beta_2/beta_3 use raw _safe_float with default 0.0.
suggested_fix: Treat empty/blank beta_2/beta_3 as absent (e.g. `if beta_2 not in (None, "")`), or skip the term when _safe_float yields 0.0, matching ODAS which only adds the term when the field is a real coefficient.
verify_reasoning:
Verified the chain by reading the code and executing it. The core defect is REAL but the finding's described failure mode is WRONG.

Confirmed facts:
1. parse_config (src/odas_tpw/rsi/p_file.py:241-246) matches a valueless line `beta_2 =` with regex `^(.+?)\s*=\s*(.*)$`, producing `current_channel["beta_2"] = ""` (empty string, not None). Premise confirmed.
2. convert_therm (channels.py:107) `params.get("beta_2")` returns `""`; line 115 `if beta_2 is not None` is True for `""`, so the term executes.
3. `_safe_float("")` (channels.py:14-19) calls `float("")` which raises ValueError, caught, returns default 0.0.
4. The beta_1 path IS protected: line 102 `_safe_float(params["beta_1"], 3000.0)` returns 3000.0 for empty string, so the main term never divides by zero. The finding's asymmetry claim is correct: beta_2/beta_3 use `_safe_float(beta_2)` with implicit default 0.0 (lines 116, 119), so empty string -> 0.0.
5. ODAS reference (odas/convert_odas.m:508-512) gates these terms with `isfield(params,'beta_2')` — field existence, the analog of the Python `is not None` check — so the Python guard genuinely fails to handle the present-but-empty case the way an absent field is handled.

MATERIAL CORRECTION to the finding: I executed the exact expression. `1.0 / _safe_float(beta_2)` is `1.0 / 0.0` evaluated on two PYTHON FLOAT SCALARS, which raises ZeroDivisionError (hard crash), NOT inf with a RuntimeWarning, and NOT silent inf/NaN temperatures. The division happens before any numpy array broadcast (operator precedence: `(1.0/0.0)` first), so numpy's inf-on-divide behavior never applies. The finding's claimed mechanism ("inf ... corrupts inv_T ... yields inf/NaN temperatures silently") is incorrect — the actual symptom is a noisy crash, which is arguably less harmful than the silent corruption claimed.

Reachability: the input is hypothetical. The actual ARCTERX VMP/*.p configs contain `beta_2 = 2.5e5` (a real value), so the bug does not trigger on the project's data. It would only fire on a malformed/hand-edited config that leaves `beta_2 =` (or `beta_3 =`) blank. RSI-generated configs normally either omit the key (None -> correctly skipped) or supply a value.

Net: real latent defect (empty beta_2/beta_3 not guarded, asymmetric with the protected beta_1), but mischaracterized failure mode and only reachable via a non-occurring malformed config. Downgraded from minor to nit. The suggested fix (`if beta_2 not in (None, "")`) is correct.

title: _load_from_nc leaks open nc.Dataset on malformed input
description: `ds = nc.Dataset(...)` is opened at line 331 and only closed at line 440. Several code paths raise before that close: `_get_nc_attr` raises AttributeError at 352 (e.g. missing fs_fast/fs_slow), `raise ValueError("No time variables found")` at 367, and `raise ValueError("No pressure variable found")` at 398/400. On any malformed/partial NetCDF these exceptions leak the open Dataset/file handle (HDF5 keeps the file locked on some platforms), which matters during batch processing where many files are scanned.
suggested_fix: Wrap the body in try/finally (or a `with closing(ds):`/context manager) so ds.close() always runs.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed by reading src/odas_tpw/rsi/profile.py:327-440. `ds = nc.Dataset(str(nc_path), "r")` opens at line 331 with no context manager, and the only `ds.close()` is at line 440 with no try/finally protecting the intervening body. Multiple reachable raise paths leak the open handle: line 352 `raise AttributeError` (reached from the `_get_nc_attr("fs_fast")`/`_get_nc_attr("fs_slow")` calls at 354/355, which pass no default so a missing attr raises); line 367 `raise ValueError("No time variables found...")`; lines 398/400 `raise ValueError("No pressure variable found...")`. Beyond those, KeyError from variable access (e.g. 359-360, 364-365, 390, 394) and any error in `_scan_vars` would also leak. The caller `_load_source` (line 265-278) invokes `_load_from_nc` directly with no surrounding `with`/try-finally, and it is reachable from the public `extract_profiles`, matching the batch-scan scenario. netCDF4/HDF5 handles relying on GC finalization to close are genuinely unreliable (and an exception traceback can keep `ds` alive, delaying finalization), so the file may stay open/locked. Real defect. Severity correctly 'minor': it only triggers on malformed/partial NetCDF (not the happy path), and CPython refcounting usually finalizes the Dataset soon after the exception unwinds, limiting practical impact. Fix (try/finally or contextlib.closing) is appropriate.

title: p_to_L1 / extract_profiles leak open Dataset and leave partial files on write failure
description: In p_to_L1 `ds = nc.Dataset(str(nc_filepath), "w")` (convert.py:475) is followed by many createVariable/setattr writes before `ds.close()` at 552; any exception in between (e.g. a bad attr value, an encoding error) leaks the open handle and leaves a truncated/corrupt .nc on disk that a later run may treat as valid. The same pattern exists per-profile in extract_profiles: `ds = nc.Dataset(str(prof_path), "w")` at profile.py:139 with close only at 252, so a mid-loop failure (e.g. gps.lat/lon raising, canonicalize_units raising, or a write error) leaks that profile's Dataset and writes a partial file. convert_all's per-file except (convert.py:605/616) catches the error for logging but does not close or remove the partial output.
suggested_fix: Use try/finally (or contextlib.closing) around each nc.Dataset and unlink the partial output file on failure before re-raising/logging.
verify_reasoning:
Verified against the actual code. convert.py:475 opens `ds = nc.Dataset(str(nc_filepath), "w", format="NETCDF4")` and only closes at line 552, with no try/finally; many fallible operations occur in between (createVariable, setattr of attrs/config string, _create_l1_variables, gradt array ops). profile.py:139 opens `ds = nc.Dataset(str(prof_path), "w", ...)` per profile and closes only at line 252, with fallible gps.lat/lon (189-190), canonicalize_units (242), and array writes in between. convert_all (605/616) catches OSError/ValueError/RuntimeError for logging only — it does not close the leaked handle nor unlink the partial output. So the resource-leak + partial-file claim is genuine and the suggested try/finally + unlink fix is appropriate.

However, the finding's secondary claim that "a later run may treat [the partial file] as valid" is NOT substantiated: I found no skip-if-exists/resume logic in pipeline.py, convert.py, or profile.py — every run re-opens in mode "w" which truncates/overwrites, so a stale partial would be replaced, not trusted. That reduces the real-world impact.

Severity: minor (not major). The leaked netCDF4.Dataset handle is reclaimed on process/GC teardown, the file is reopened with "w" on retry, and there is no resume path that consumes the corrupt file. The extract_profiles loop is the more material case (a mid-loop failure leaves earlier profiles plus a partial profile-N file and propagates), but it is still a robustness/cleanliness issue rather than a correctness defect. is_real=true, corrected_severity=minor.

title: Deconvolution of a pre-emphasized-only channel injects a mislabeled 'counts' base channel
description: _apply_deconvolution always assigns `self.channels_raw[base_name] = hres[...]` (p_file.py:588/594) even when the base channel never existed in the file (X_raw is None) — the documented ODAS case of a micro-conductivity channel C1_dC1 with no plain C1 (deconvolve.py supports X=None). When base_name has no [channel] section, the guard `if base_name in ch_config` at p_file.py:600 is False, so (a) ch_config[dX_name] is NOT updated to the base calibration, and (b) the later conversion loop hits `ch_config.get(base_name, {})` -> type defaults to 'raw', so the freshly-injected base_name is emitted as a raw 'counts' channel holding deconvolved physical-ish float data with wrong units, plus dX_name is converted with its own sparse (likely diff-gain) params rather than base calibration. Not triggered by the ARCTERX VMP config (P and T1 both have non-pre-emphasized partners) but is a latent correctness bug for micro-conductivity/other pre-emphasis-only channels.
suggested_fix: Only inject base_name when it was an actual channel (X_raw is not None or base_name in ch_config); otherwise keep only dX_name and convert it with the diff_gain channel's own calibration as ODAS does.
verify_reasoning: Verified against the actual code. _apply_deconvolution (src/odas_tpw/rsi/p_file.py:550-606) iterates ch_config; for an X_dX channel with diff_gain matching the `^(\w+)_d\1$` pattern and present in channels_raw, it computes base_name and reads `X_raw = self.channels_raw.get(base_name)` (line 580), which is None when the plain base channel was never read. deconvolve(None, ...) is explicitly supported (deconvolve.py:24-29 signature `X: np.ndarray | None`, X-is-None branch at lines 86-95) and matches the documented ODAS micro-conductivity case `deconvolve('C1_dC1', [], C1_dC1, ...)` (odas/deconvolve.m:44-47). Both branches then unconditionally execute `self.channels_raw[base_name] = hres[...]` (lines 588 and 594), injecting a base channel that has no [channel] config section.
