If two per-file CTD time-binned files overlap in time (duplicate epoch seconds at a boundary) or any time center is NaN, the resulting `time` coordinate is monotonic-non-decreasing but NOT strictly increasing, so the CF requirement the code intends to enforce is still violated and a CF checker would still flag it. The depth path explicitly guards NaN with `np.where(np.isfinite(sk), sk, np.inf)` (line 89) but the time path (line 97) has no such guard. Lower confidence on real-world NaN since bin_by_time computes centers from finite edges, but duplicate boundary times across concatenated files are plausible.
suggested_fix: After sorting, assert/enforce strict monotonicity for the time path: drop or coalesce duplicate timestamps and NaN times (e.g. `combo = combo.isel(time=np.unique(tk, return_index=True)[1])` on the sorted axis), mirroring the explicit finite-handling used on the depth path.
verify_reasoning:
Verified against the actual code in src/odas_tpw/perturb/combo.py. The asymmetry the finding describes is real: the depth path (line 89) sorts with an explicit NaN guard `np.argsort(np.where(np.isfinite(sk), sk, np.inf))`, while the time path (line 97) is bare `np.argsort(tk)`. The comment at line 93 correctly states the goal ("CF requires the time coordinate to be strictly monotonic"), and I empirically confirmed that argsort alone only yields a non-decreasing (not strictly increasing) sequence and leaves duplicate values and NaN in place. So if duplicate epoch-second time centers ever appear across concatenated per-file CTD products, combo.nc's `time` coordinate is monotonic-non-decreasing but not strictly increasing, violating the CF §4 requirement the code intends to enforce; an external CF/compliance-checker would flag it.

Caveats that bound the severity:
1. No crash. xarray/to_netcdf do not reject a non-strictly-monotonic time coordinate (test_perturb_combo.py writes time combos successfully), so the only consequence is a latent CF-compliance warning, not a processing failure.
2. The NaN half of the finding is effectively moot for the time path: CTD time centers are built as midpoints of `np.arange(t_lo, t_hi+bw, bw)` edges from finite t_lo/t_hi (ctd.py:302-304, binning.py:541-544) plus a finite epoch offset (pf.start_time.timestamp()), so they are finite by construction. The finding itself rates NaN as low-confidence.
3. Duplicate boundary times are *possible* but not structurally guaranteed: each file is binned on its own independent grid offset by an arbitrary millisecond-resolution start_time, so an exact float64 collision `start_A+center_A == start_B+center_B` across files is uncommon rather than systematic. No test exercises overlapping/duplicate-time concatenation.

Net: a genuine but low-impact robustness/CF-intent gap. 'minor' is appropriate; confidence is medium because the code defect is certain but the real-world triggering condition (exact duplicate epoch-second across independently-offset file grids) is uncommon and unverified by any test or sample data. The suggested fix (dedup via np.unique on the sorted axis, mirroring the depth path's finite handling) is sound.

title: Multi-probe CI removal always drops the MAX probe, never a low outlier (systematic low bias)
description: The iterative 95% CI removal loop unconditionally NaNs the MAXIMUM probe value on every row whose log min/max spread exceeds CF95_range (lines 119-136: `max_idx = np.nanargmax(...)`, then `epsilon[i, max_idx[i]] = np.nan`). The rejection is asymmetric: a low outlier can never be removed, and the *high* (often correct) probes are dropped instead. Verified empirically: probes [1e-7, 1e-7, 1e-11] -> the two correct 1e-7 probes are NaN'd and the 1e-11 junk probe survives, giving epsilonMean = 1e-11 (true geomean 4.6e-9). For the common 2-probe VMP case, whenever the two probes disagree by more than the CI the algorithm always keeps the lower probe: e.g. realistic sigma=0.82, CF95=2.28, probes [1e-7(good), 5e-9(low)] (log-spread 3.0 > 2.28) -> epsilonMean=5e-9, a 20x low bias, when keeping both would give 2.2e-8. A genuinely low-biased probe (partial fouling / reduced sensitivity / quiet patch) can have a perfectly good FOM near 1.0 and pass _apply_fom_cut, so this is unmitigated upstream. The docstring (line 30, __init__.py:15) advertises 'Lueck (2022) iterative 95% CI'; the standard Lueck/Rockland mk_diss_mean removes the value FURTHEST from the mean in log space, not unconditionally the maximum. This is a systematic quantitative low bias in the core epsilon aggregate.
suggested_fix: Remove the single probe whose ln(epsilon) is furthest from the cross-probe ln-mean (or ln-median) on each outside row, instead of always nanargmax. E.g. per outside row compute `d = abs(log(eps) - nanmean(log(eps)))` and NaN the argmax of d. This symmetrically rejects both high and low outliers. Re-derive CF95_range/mu_sigma from surviving probes after each removal so the threshold tracks the shrinking set.
verify_reasoning: CONFIRMED by reading and executing the code. In src/odas_tpw/processing/epsilon_combine.py:117-136 the iterative CI loop unconditionally drops the MAXIMUM probe on any row whose ln(max)-ln(min) spread exceeds CF95_range: line 134 `max_idx[any_finite] = np.nanargmax(epsilon[any_finite], axis=1)`, line 136 `epsilon[i, max_idx[i]] = np.nan`. There is no comparison to a cross-probe mean/median, so a low outlier can never be removed and correct high probes are dropped instead. I reproduced all three claimed cases by running the actual function: (A) probes [1e-7, 5e-9] -> epsilonMean=5e-9 (the good high probe is NaN'd) vs true geomean 2.24e-8, a ~4.5x low bias (note: actual CF95_range here is 1.42, not the 2.28 the finding stated, but the qualitative outcome is identical and the trigger is even easier); (B) order-independent (e_1=5e-9,e_2=1e-7 also yields 5e-9, confirming it is the max value not a fixed probe index); (C) [1e-7,1e-7,1e-11] -> epsilonMean=1e-11 (both good probes dropped, junk survives) vs geomean 4.6e-9, a ~460x low bias. The bias is unmitigated upstream: _apply_fom_cut (pipeline.py:393-422) only NaNs probes with fom>=fom_max, a one-sided magnitude cut, so a genuinely low-biased probe with FOM~1.0 passes and then wins the geomean. epsilonMean is the core combined product (consumed by chi Method 1, chi_io.py:314, and written to binned output). The same asymmetric nanargmax removal is replicated in chi_combine.py:147-163. The docstrings advertise a "Lueck (2022) iterative 95% CI" / "remove probes outside the 95% CI", but a symmetric CI rejects both tails; always dropping the max is not a faithful symmetric implementation and is a systematic low bias. Existing test test_outlier_probe_removed (test_processing_epsilon_combine.py:192) only exercises the high-outlier case, so the test suite does not catch the low-outlier pathology -- a real test gap. The one residual uncertainty (kept me from re-checking against the cited Matlab) is that calc_diss_shear.m/mk_diss_mean.m is not present in the repo, so I cannot byte-compare against the original; only get_diss_odas.m (per-segment) is shipped. But that does not affect the defect itself, which is fully demonstrated by the present code and its runtime behavior. Severity major (quantitative systematic bias in the primary scientific aggregate, triggered by realistic probe disagreement, unmitigated upstream), not blocker (consistent probes within CI are unaffected). Suggested fix stands: drop the probe whose ln(epsilon) is furthest from the cross-probe ln-mean/median, symmetrically, and re-derive the threshold from survivors each pass.

title: Chi pipeline speed floor np.maximum propagates NaN into fp07_tau and wavenumber axis
description:
The production L2/L3/L4 chi pipeline (the path used by perturb and the rsi-tpw chi CLI via _compute_chi -> process_l3_chi) computes the per-window speed as `speed_means = np.maximum(np.mean(np.abs(l2_chi.pspd_rel[indices]), axis=1), 0.01)`. This floor correctly handles speed==0 and speed<0 (np.abs makes negatives positive; np.maximum lifts 0 to 0.01), so the targeted lueck/peterson fp07_tau domain errors (inf at speed=0, complex/NaN for speed<0) cannot occur for those inputs. HOWEVER np.maximum propagates NaN (np.maximum(nan, 0.01) -> nan; verified), and np.mean over any window containing a single NaN speed sample is NaN. A NaN speed_means then flows verbatim into (a) `tau0_all = fp07_tau_batch(speed_means, ...)` (l3_chi.py:179) producing a NaN FP07 time constant, and (b) `K_all = F_const[:, None] / speed_means[None, :]` (l3_chi.py:167) producing an all-NaN wavenumber axis for that window, which L4 then uses as `W = l3_chi.pspd_rel[j]` (l4_chi.py:99) and `K = l3_chi.kcyc[:, j]` (l4_chi.py:98). The result is a silently NaN chi for the affected windows: the L4 mask `(K > 0) & (f_AA_eff/W >= K)` (l4_chi.py:240) becomes all-False on NaN K, _iterative_fit/_mle hit the <min_points branch, warn only the generic 'Too few valid points', and return NaN chi -- no exception and no indication that speed was the cause. This is the chi-side analogue of the known shear-side finding (helpers.py:289-293 precomputed-speed path applies no floor/NaN-handling): when an external caller supplies a `speed_fast` channel containing NaN, prepare_profiles passes it through unfloored (`speed_fast = np.asarray(precomputed, ...)`, helpers.py:293), process_l2 copies l1.pspd_rel verbatim because has_speed is True (scor160/l2.py:57), and l3_chi.py:146's np.maximum cannot scrub the NaN.

Reachability / why severity is minor not major: all FOUR current perturb speed methods (pressure/constant/em/flight in rsi/speed.py) route their output through _slow_to_fast (NaN-fills via np.interp then floors via np.maximum(arr_fast, speed_min), speed.py:144-156) or compute_speed_fast (floors, profile.py:95/135) or np.full(max(...)), so the perturb-injected speed_fast (pipeline.py:872-874) is NaN-free at the source; the flight model's deliberate `sin_path = np.where(... np.nan)` NaNs (speed.py:200) are filled by _slow_to_fast before reaching chi. Thus the NaN path is not reachable through the current perturb production methods, only through a hypothetical external caller passing a NaN-bearing precomputed speed_fast. It is a latent robustness gap rather than an active production bug.
suggested_fix: Make the floor NaN-scrubbing rather than NaN-propagating, e.g. `speed_means = np.maximum(np.nan_to_num(np.mean(np.abs(l2_chi.pspd_rel[indices]), axis=1), nan=0.01), 0.01)` or use np.fmax with an explicit NaN replacement, mirroring the shear-side intent. Equivalently/additionally, floor and NaN-fill the precomputed speed at the single choke point in prepare_profiles (helpers.py:293) so every consumer (shear normalization AND chi) gets a clean speed: `speed_fast = np.maximum(np.nan_to_num(np.asarray(precomputed, dtype=np.float64), nan=SPEED_MIN), SPEED_MIN)`.
verify_reasoning:
Verified by reading the actual code. The defect genuinely exists.

CORE CLAIMS CONFIRMED:
1. l3_chi.py:146 — `speed_means = np.maximum(np.mean(np.abs(l2_chi.pspd_rel[indices]), axis=1), 0.01)`. Empirically verified np.maximum propagates NaN (np.maximum(nan,0.01)->nan) and np.mean over a window with one NaN sample -> nan. So a NaN speed sample yields NaN speed_means.
2. NaN speed_means flows into K_all = F_const/speed_means (l3_chi.py:167) producing an all-NaN wavenumber axis (verified F/nan -> nan), and into tau0_all = fp07_tau_batch(speed_means,...) (l3_chi.py:179).
3. L4 consumes K = l3_chi.kcyc[:,j] (l4_chi.py:98) and W = l3_chi.pspd_rel[j] (l4_chi.py:99). Default fit_method is "iterative" in both l4_chi.py:204 and chi_io.py:41, so _iterative_fit runs. With all-NaN K, _valid_wavenumber_mask (chi.py:121-155) yields all-False (NaN>0 is False, and K_AA=f_AA/NaN_speed is NaN so K<=K_AA is False), len(valid_idx)<6 (chi.py:501) -> warns "Too few valid points for iterative fit" and returns all-NaN ChiFitResult. No exception, silent NaN chi. Confirmed.

REACHABILITY CONFIRMED AS LATENT/MINOR:
- All four perturb speed methods scrub NaN before reaching chi: pressure routes through compute_speed_fast which np.interp + np.maximum floors (profile.py:89-95); em/flight route through _slow_to_fast which NaN-fills via np.interp on the slow grid (speed.py:148-151) before flooring (speed.py:156); constant uses np.full (speed.py:102). The flight model's deliberate np.where(...np.nan) (speed.py:200) is consumed by _slow_to_fast which fills it. So the perturb-injected speed_fast (pipeline.py:872-875) is NaN-free at source.
- The only unfloored/un-scrubbed ingress is the precomputed path at rsi/helpers.py:293: `speed_fast = np.asarray(precomputed, dtype=np.float64)` — no floor, no NaN-fill. Reachable only by a hypothetical external caller supplying a NaN-bearing precomputed speed_fast.
- Confirmed within chi L2: l2_chi.py:121 floors a copy (spd_safe) only for the gradient; l2_chi.py:143 stores l2.pspd_rel.copy() UNfloored into L2ChiData, so l3_chi.py:146's np.maximum is indeed the only floor and it cannot scrub NaN.

MINOR CITATION IMPRECISIONS (do not affect substance): finding cited the file as helpers.py rather than the actual src/odas_tpw/rsi/helpers.py; the warning text is "Too few valid points for iterative fit" not the abbreviated "Too few valid points" quoted. These are cosmetic.

Verdict: real latent robustness gap, correctly self-classified as minor (not reachable through current production paths). The suggested NaN-scrubbing fix at l3_chi.py:146 or the single choke point helpers.py:293 is sound.

title: Sequential-file_number chaining merges independent casts (no time/restarted-flag guard)
description: find_mergeable_files chains files purely on (config_hash, endian, record_size) plus file_number being exactly +1 of the previous (line 105). It never checks any time-continuity (start_time/year), the RSI 'restarted' header flag (word 16, present in _H but unread), buffer_status, or n_records_written. I verified against the actual VMP/ campaign data: all 29 ARCTERX_Thompson_2025_SN479_*.p files are SEPARATE casts, each a complete recording, yet they all share ONE config hash and carry sequential file_number 2,3,4,...,30. Running find_mergeable_files on the real VMP/*.p set returns exactly ONE chain of length 29 (chain[0]=...0002.p ... chain[-1]=...0030.p). With files.merge=true (pipeline.py:1719) the ENTIRE deployment of independent casts would be byte-concatenated into a single .p file, destroying per-cast structure and timestamps. This is the normal data layout for this instrument, not a contrived edge case. RSI's split-file rollover (the intended target of merging) is distinguished from a new deployment by the 'restarted' flag and timestamp continuity, neither of which is consulted.
suggested_fix: Add a positive split-file discriminator beyond file_number adjacency: require the candidate next file's start_time to be contiguous with the prior file's end time (within a small tolerance), and/or require restarted==0 (header word 16) on the continuation file. Optionally require buffer_status/n_records_written consistency. Do not chain solely on sequential file_number + matching config.
verify_reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL. find_mergeable_files (src/odas_tpw/perturb/merge.py:55-114) chains files purely on the group key (config_hash, endian, record_size) at line 57 plus strict file_number adjacency at line 105 (group[i].file_number == chain[-1].file_number + 1). It consults NO time-continuity, restarted flag, buffer_status, or n_records_written. I confirmed _read_merge_info (lines 18-52) never reads restarted/buffer/n_records even though _H in p_file.py:30-57 defines restarted (word 16), buffer_status (15), and n_records_written (19) — so the discriminators exist but are unused.

I empirically ran the real find_mergeable_files on the actual VMP/*.p set (29 files, ARCTERX_Thompson_2025_SN479_0002..0030.p): it returns exactly ONE chain of length 29 (0002 -> 0030). All 29 share one config hash (787bb757) and carry sequential file_number 2..30, yet their header start-times span 2025-01-14 through 2025-01-24 with multi-hour and multi-day gaps — these are independent casts, not split-file rollover (which would be time-contiguous). The header restarted flag is 0 on every file (buffer_status and n_records_written are 0/unpopulated here, so timestamp continuity is the usable discriminator). This proves RSI increments file_number monotonically across the instrument's separate recordings, so file_number adjacency alone does NOT imply continuation. merge_p_files (lines 198-211) byte-concatenates all data records of the chain, so with files.merge=true this entire deployment of 29 casts would be fused into one .p file, destroying per-cast structure and timestamps. The test suite (tests/test_perturb_merge.py::test_sequential_files_detected) codifies this exact behavior as intended, so it would not catch the misbehavior — a real test gap.

Refutation attempt / severity calibration: the only mitigant is that files.merge defaults to False (config.py:27), so this fires only when a user explicitly opts in. But the docstring and docs (docs/perturb/pipeline.md:31, configuration.md:34) describe merge as joining 'sequential segments of the same deployment,' so a user enabling it on normal campaign data would reasonably expect it NOT to fuse 29 distinct casts. The defect is a genuine latent data-corruption bug with a severe consequence but an opt-in trigger — major is the correct severity (not blocker, since it is not on by default). The suggested fix (require timestamp contiguity within tolerance and/or restarted==0 on the continuation file) is sound; restarted==0 alone is insufficient here since all files show restarted=0, so a time-continuity check is the load-bearing discriminator.

title: _file_group_key omits header_size/config_size; splice offset uses each file's own geometry but reader reparses with first file's geometry
description: _file_group_key groups on (config_hash, endian, record_size) only. header_size and config_size are not part of the key, so two split files that share config_hash and record_size but differ in header_size (e.g. a firmware/header-layout change writing a larger record header, or differing config padding) still chain. merge_p_files (lines 204-209) then appends each subsequent file's bytes after skipping skip = info['header_size'] + info['config_size'] computed from THAT file's own header, while the downstream reader PFile._read (rsi/p_file.py:307-379) parses the entire merged file using the FIRST file's header_size, config_size, and record_size to slice every record (record_dtype with hdr=header_size//2 words). When header_size differs between files, the spliced records are mis-sliced: the second file's record-internal header/data boundary no longer matches the assumed header_size, silently corrupting all downstream physics. I confirmed empirically: two files with header_size 128 vs 160 (same config bytes, same record_size 512) produce equal group keys, merge successfully, and the merged file's (size - first_record_size) % record_size == 0, so PFile raises NO partial-record warning (p_file.py:352) -- the corruption is completely silent.
suggested_fix: Include header_size and config_size in _file_group_key (or assert they are identical across a chain before merging). When skipping the continuation file's first record, the per-record geometry (header_size, record_size) must be identical to chain[0]; otherwise refuse to merge. Validating record_size alone is insufficient because the reader splits each record into header_size header words plus data words.
verify_reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL. Read merge.py and p_file.py and reproduced the defect empirically.

(1) _file_group_key (merge.py:55-57) returns (config_hash, endian, record_size) only — header_size and config_size are read by _read_merge_info (lines 30-31, 47-48) but excluded from the key. Confirmed by test: two files with header_size 128 vs 160 but identical config bytes, record_size 512, and endian produce equal group keys and chain together via find_mergeable_files (file_numbers 0,1 are sequential).

(2) merge_p_files (lines 204-209) skips skip = info['header_size'] + info['config_size'] computed from EACH continuation file's own header (line 205-206), appending that file's data region after its own first-record region.

(3) The downstream reader PFile._read (p_file.py:307-379) parses the WHOLE merged file with the FIRST file's header_size/config_size/record_size: first_record_size = header_size + config_size (line 346) and record_dtype = [("hdr", udtype, (header_size//2,)), ("data", dtype, (data_words,))] with data_words = (record_size - header_size)//2 (lines 361,369-374). So appended records originating from a file with a different header_size are sliced at the wrong internal header/data boundary, mis-aligning data for every appended record — silent physics corruption.

(4) Silent-failure claim verified: I built the merged file (size 3216 = 144 + 6*512) and computed (size - first_record_size) % record_size == 0 using chain[0] geometry (first_record_size 144), so the partial-record warning at p_file.py:352 does NOT fire. No exception, no warning.

There is no guard anywhere that asserts header_size is identical across a chain. record_size equality alone is insufficient because the reader splits each record into header_size header words plus data words.

Reachability caveat (does not negate the defect): the merge is intended for split pieces of one recording, where header_size is constant, so the bug does not trigger in the common same-recording case. It triggers only when two files share config_hash+record_size+endian+adjacent file_number but differ in header_size (e.g. a firmware/header-version change between two separate recordings, or cross-recording false-chaining — note the chain key also lacks start_time/product_id/build_number disambiguation). header_size is commonly 128 for RSI, so divergence is uncommon in practice. I kept severity at major rather than blocker because reachability is conditional, but the failure mode (silent, warning-free data corruption with no guard) is severe and the code-level defect is unambiguous. The suggested fix (add header_size/config_size to the key, or assert per-record geometry identical to chain[0] before splicing) is correct. Could not compare against the cited MATLAB merge_p_files.m — it is not present in the repo — so the verdict rests entirely on the Python code, which I verified directly.

title: Cross-directory chaining produces a single output named after chain[0]; other-directory source silently dropped
description: find_mergeable_files groups by config/endian/record_size with no path/directory awareness, so files living in different subdirectories (e.g. two separate deployments under dep1/ and dep2/) that share a config hash and have adjacent file numbers are chained together. merge_destination (lines 117-138) names the merged output solely from chain[0] (first.name or first's path relative to root), so the merged file is written as dep1/<first>.p and the dep2 file's data is spliced in. The dep2 input then no longer appears as its own output: it is absorbed into a file named under dep1 and effectively disappears, with no error. I confirmed empirically with files /tmp/sectest/dep1/d_0001.p (file_number 1) and /tmp/sectest/dep2/d_0002.p (file_number 2): they form one chain and plan_merge_outputs yields a single output merged/dep1/d_0001.p sourced from both. _check_unique_outputs (pipeline.py:1336) does not catch this because there is only one output path. Lower severity than the geometry/cast issues because it requires same-config files in distinct directories with adjacent file numbers, but it is a real silent data-loss path on top of finding #1.
suggested_fix: Restrict chaining to files within the same parent directory (or same instrument key), or include the parent directory in _file_group_key. If a chain spans directories, either refuse to merge or surface a warning/error rather than silently absorbing one deployment's file into another directory's output.
verify_reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL. Every claim substantiated by reading the actual code and reproducing the behavior.

Mechanism (confirmed by reading): src/odas_tpw/perturb/merge.py:55-57 `_file_group_key` groups files only by `(config_hash, endian, record_size)` with zero path/directory awareness. `find_mergeable_files` (60-114) then chains any same-group files with sequential file_numbers regardless of which directory they live in. `merge_destination` (117-138) names the merged output solely from `chain[0]` (first.name, or first's path relative to root). `merge_p_files` (198-209) copies chain[0] in full and appends only data records (header+config skipped) from later files.

Empirical reproduction (confirmed twice): With /tmp/sectest/dep1/d_0001.p (file_number=1, cfg 04ad3cc4, rec 512) and /tmp/sectest/dep2/d_0002.p (file_number=2, same cfg/rec/endian), find_mergeable_files returns one chain [dep1/d_0001.p, dep2/d_0002.p] and plan_merge_outputs yields a single output merged/dep1/d_0001.p sourced from both. Actually running merge_p_files produced merged/dep1/d_0001.p of size 2191 = 1167+1167-143 (dep2 header+config skipped), with no separate output for the dep2 file and no 'dep2' in the output path. The dep2 deployment's data is absorbed into a dep1-named file and disappears as its own product, with no error.

_check_unique_outputs (pipeline.py:1336-1347) cannot catch this because the chain produces exactly one output path — no duplicate.

Design-inconsistency corroboration: the main pipeline (pipeline.py:1668) builds instrument_key_by_path from `p.parent.name`, i.e. it treats the parent directory name as the instrument/deployment key elsewhere — yet merge ignores directories entirely, a genuine internal inconsistency. The test suite (tests/test_perturb_merge.py) confirms intended disambiguation is by config-hash only (test_two_separate_chains uses cfg_A vs cfg_B in a flat dir); no subdirectory case is tested, so this path is untested.

Could not refute: no directory guard exists anywhere in the chaining/destination logic; the input (same-config files in different directories with adjacent file_numbers) is constructible and not prevented; it is not documented as known/intentional (the referenced Matlab merge_p_files.m is not in the repo).

Severity: minor is correct. Mitigating factors keep it from major: (1) merge defaults to False (config.py:27), so it only triggers when the user opts into merging; (2) RSI file_number is a per-session rollover counter, so cross-deployment file-number adjacency across separate directories is possible but not the common case (it requires a continuous counter spanning deployments laid out in distinct dirs); (3) the dep2 data is not destroyed — it is preserved inside the merged file, just mislabeled under dep1 and no longer a distinct output. It is nonetheless a real silent cross-deployment merge / output-naming bug. Suggested fix (include parent dir or instrument key in _file_group_key, or warn/refuse on cross-directory chains) is sound.

title: Widthwise concat relies on implicit join='outer' — silent NaN-padding (or future hard error) when bin grids differ across files
description: _glue_widthwise calls xr.concat(datasets, dim='profile') with no explicit join=. make_combo (line 69/77) globs ALL *.nc in the binned dir, so if more than one binned NetCDF with DIFFERENT 'bin' coordinate vectors lands in the same dir (e.g. two CLI re-runs with different depth ranges, a stale/extra file, or a manually-placed file), xarray's current default join='outer' silently outer-aligns the 'bin' coordinate and NaN-pads each profile's data onto the union grid — producing a corrupted combo where profiles are stretched across bins they never sampled, with no warning to the user. I verified this on the installed xarray 2026.2.0: concatenating two (3,2) and (4,2) datasets with overlapping-but-unequal bin vectors yields a (5,4) array silently NaN-padded. Because the project pins only 'xarray>=2024.1' (pyproject.toml:20) and xarray emits a FutureWarning that the default will flip to join='exact', a future xarray upgrade would instead turn this into a hard ValueError, changing behavior under the floor. In the normal pipeline only a single binned.nc is written so this is latent, but the glob-all design makes it reachable.
suggested_fix: Pass join='exact' (and ideally compat='equals') explicitly to xr.concat in _glue_widthwise so mismatched bin grids fail loudly with a clear error instead of silently NaN-padding, and pin a stable xarray join policy. Alternatively re-grid all inputs onto a common bin vector before concat.
verify_reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL but downgraded major->minor due to limited reachability.

Code facts (confirmed by reading):
- src/odas_tpw/perturb/combo.py:25 `_glue_widthwise` calls `xr.concat(datasets, dim="profile")` with no explicit `join=` (line 35 `_glue_lengthwise` has the same gap on dim="time").
- make_combo globs ALL `*.nc` in the dir (combo.py:69) and concatenates whatever it finds.
- pyproject.toml:20 pins only `xarray>=2024.1` with no upper bound.

Empirical confirmation (xarray 2026.2.0, the installed version stated in the finding):
- Concatenating (3,2)+(4,2) datasets with overlapping-but-unequal 'bin' grids silently yields a (5,4) array with 6/20 cells NaN-padded onto the union grid — exactly the silent outer-join corruption claimed.
- xarray emits `FutureWarning: ... the default value for join will change from join='outer' to ...`, confirming a future upgrade (allowed by the unbounded floor pin) would turn this into a hard ValueError.

Reachability assessment (why minor, not major):
- The normal pipeline writes exactly ONE file per binned dir: the binning functions (bin_profiles_by_depth etc.) concat ALL input files into a single dataset across global bin edges, then cli.py:263/284/309 and pipeline.py:1943/1957/1971 write a single `binned.nc`. So `_glue_widthwise` normally receives a one-element list and the defect never fires.
- The finding's cited "two CLI re-runs with different depth ranges in same dir" trigger is partly mitigated: resolve_output_dir hashes binning params into the dir name (cli.py:357, _combo_dst), so different ranges route to different dirs.
- A multi-element list requires an abnormal extra `.nc` in the dir (stale leftover, manually-copied/renamed file). That path is genuinely reachable via the glob-all design and would corrupt silently with no warning, so the defect stands.

Net: a real, latent defensive-coding/robustness bug (no join policy; silent NaN-padding; future hard error under the floor pin), but it cannot occur in normal single-binned.nc operation and the worst trigger needs an out-of-band file, so minor rather than major. Fix is correct and easy: pass join='exact' (and compat='equals') to xr.concat so mismatched bin grids fail loudly.

title: Profile coordinate is left with duplicate values after concat (depth path masks it, but data still corrupted)
description: When concatenating multiple binned files along 'profile', each file carries its own profile coordinate (e.g. [0,1,2]), so the concatenated 'profile' coordinate contains duplicate index values (e.g. [0,1,2,0,1,2]). I confirmed [0,1,0,1] is produced for two two-profile files. The depth branch later reassigns profile=np.arange(...) at line 91 only when 'stime' is present in the combo; if 'stime' is absent (older per-profile NCs that lack stime — the binning code at binning.py:495-497 only emits stime when at least one profile carried it), the duplicate-valued, non-unique profile coordinate is written through unchanged, which CF compliance and downstream isel/sel-by-label operations treat as ambiguous.
suggested_fix: Unconditionally reset the profile coordinate to a contiguous int32 range after the widthwise concat (not only inside the 'stime in combo' branch), or use ignore_index-style concat semantics so profile indices are always unique.
verify_reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL (with one framing correction). Every premise checks out against the actual code:

1. binning.py:503 sets `"profile": np.arange(n_profiles)` per dataset, so each binned NC carries its own 0-based profile coordinate.
2. combo.py:25 `_glue_widthwise` does `xr.concat(datasets, dim="profile")` over all *.nc found by `binned_dir.glob("*.nc")` (combo.py:69,79). When >1 binned file is present, the profile coordinate concatenates to duplicate values.
3. The reset to a contiguous range (combo.py:91 `assign_coords(profile=np.arange(...))`) is gated by `method=="depth" and "stime" in combo` (combo.py:87). stime is only emitted when at least one profile carried it (binning.py:495-496), so for profile NCs lacking start-time metadata the reset is skipped.
4. combo.py:242 unconditionally stamps `cf_role="profile_id"` onto this coordinate, so a CF profile_id instance coordinate ends up non-unique.

Empirically reproduced: I ran make_combo() on two 3-profile binned files without stime (the exact shape of the existing test `tests/test_perturb_combo.py::TestMakeCombo::test_depth_combo`, lines 33-64) and the written combo.nc had profile = [0 1 2 0 1 2], 3 unique of 6, has_stime=False. So the multi-file-per-directory concat is a supported, tested path, not hypothetical.

REACHABILITY NUANCE (why minor, not major): The default end-to-end pipeline does NOT trigger this. In pipeline.py:1930-1943 a single bin_by_depth runs over all profiles and writes one binned.nc; combo.py then concatenates a single-element list (no duplicates). The bin CLI (cli.py:242-263) writes binned.nc into a single resolved dir, overwriting per iteration, so a binned dir holds exactly one binned.nc. The duplicate arises when make_combo is called on a directory containing multiple binned *.nc files lacking stime — which is exactly what the public function's docstring ("Merge all binned NetCDFs") and its own unit test set up, but which the standard pipeline does not currently produce.

CORRECTION to the finding's title/wording: "data still corrupted" overstates it. The underlying data variable values and their positional order are intact; .isel works correctly. What is corrupted is only the profile *coordinate labels* (non-unique), which makes .sel(profile=...) label indexing ambiguous and violates CF uniqueness for a profile_id instance variable. The suggested fix (unconditionally reset the profile coordinate to a contiguous int32 range after the widthwise concat) is correct and addresses it. Severity minor is appropriate: real defect on a public/tested code path with CF-compliance and label-indexing consequences, but not reached by the default pipeline and not a data-value corruption.

title: time_coverage_resolution is only emitted for stime-bearing (depth) combos; never for time/CTD combos that have a regular sampling interval
description: The ACDD time_coverage_resolution block is gated on 'stime' in combo (line 214), but time-method / CTD combos carry a 'time' coordinate and no 'stime'. As a result the most regularly-sampled products (time-binned CTD trajectories, which have an exactly uniform bin_width interval) never get a time_coverage_resolution attribute, while irregular profile combos do. This is an ACDD-completeness gap and is inconsistent with the rest of the function, which falls back to 'time' for time_coverage_start/end (lines 182-190). Not a crash, but the resolution metadata is omitted exactly where it is best-defined.
suggested_fix: Add a fallback that derives time_coverage_resolution from the median diff of the sorted 'time' coordinate when 'stime' is absent (mirroring the existing stime logic), so time/CTD combos also get the resolution attribute.
verify_reasoning:
Verified against the actual code in src/odas_tpw/perturb/combo.py.

The gating is real: line 214 conditions the entire `time_coverage_resolution` block on `"stime" in combo and combo["stime"].size > 1`. CTD/time combos are produced by `make_ctd_combo` (combo.py:281), which calls `make_combo(..., method="time")`. For method="time", `_glue_lengthwise` concatenates along `time` (combo.py:35, 79) and the dataset carries a `time` coordinate but no `stime` (which is a per-profile/depth-combo variable). The code itself acknowledges this split: `time_coverage_start`/`end` explicitly fall back to `time` for time combos (combo.py:182-190), but `time_coverage_resolution` has no such fallback. So time/CTD combos never receive `time_coverage_resolution`. The asymmetry the finding describes is genuine.

This is a real ACDD-completeness gap. It is metadata-only — not a crash, not a wrong value, just an omitted optional/recommended ACDD attribute. It has no correctness, numerical, or functional consequence.

One correction to the finding's rationale: the claim that CTD time combos have "an exactly uniform bin_width interval" overstates it. Within a single file, `bin_by_time` (binning.py:541-544) does produce bin centers uniformly spaced at `bin_width`. But the combo concatenates multiple files (`_glue_lengthwise`) and re-sorts the merged `time` coord (combo.py:96-98); per-file `bin_edges` start from each file's own `t_min` (binning.py:541), so across file boundaries there are gaps and grid-offset jumps — the concatenated `time` is not exactly uniform. Nonetheless, the median-diff fallback the finding suggests would robustly recover `bin_width` (the dominant intra-file step), so the proposed fix is sound. The mechanism and conclusion hold; only the "exactly uniform" wording is imprecise.

Downgraded from minor to nit: this is a missing optional ACDD-recommended metadata attribute with zero functional/correctness impact, and the dataset is otherwise CF/ACDD-populated.

title: Test gap: no coverage for differing bin grids (silent outer-join) or overlapping time grids (surviving duplicates)
description: test_depth_combo (lines 33-64) writes two files with IDENTICAL bin grids (np.arange(10) both), and test_time_combo / test_time_combo_trajectory_id_and_sort (lines 66-84, 223-251) use strictly NON-overlapping time ranges (i*10, and offsets 0 vs 3600). The two highest-risk cross-file assembly cases — (a) widthwise concat of files with different 'bin' vectors, and (b) lengthwise concat of files with overlapping/duplicate time stamps — are therefore untested, so the silent outer-join corruption and the duplicate-time CF violation above would not be caught by the suite.
suggested_fix: Add a depth-combo test with two files whose bin coordinates differ (assert it either errors clearly or re-grids correctly, not silently NaN-pads), and a time-combo test with overlapping per-file time ranges asserting the output time coordinate is strictly monotonic (no duplicates).
verify_reasoning:
Verified by reading tests/test_perturb_combo.py and src/odas_tpw/perturb/combo.py, and by empirically running xarray 2026.2.0.

Test data confirms the gap: (1) test_depth_combo (lines 39-54) writes BOTH files with identical bin grids: n_bins=10 and `bin: np.arange(10, dtype=float)` for every i. (2) test_time_combo (lines 72-78) uses np.arange(i*10, i*10+10) → file0=[0..9], file1=[10..19], strictly non-overlapping. (3) test_time_combo_trajectory_id_and_sort (lines 232-241) uses offsets 3600 and 0, each + np.arange(5) → [3600..3604] and [0..4], non-overlapping. I also checked the other combo tests (test_perturb_combo_branches.py — all single-file or arange(3.0); test_perturb_pipeline.py:1688 — t0 0.0/10.0 with arange(5), non-overlapping; test_perturb_cf_compliance.py — single file). None exercises differing bin vectors or overlapping/duplicate time stamps.

Both latent defects the gap would hide are real and confirmed in production code:
(a) _glue_widthwise (combo.py:25) calls xr.concat(datasets, dim='profile'). Empirically, with differing bin grids xarray does a silent OUTER join along the non-concat 'bin' dim, NaN-padding the shorter file's profiles, emitting only a FutureWarning (no error). Verified: output bin size=10, T contains NaN.
(b) The time path (combo.py:92-98) only np.argsort-sorts the concatenated time coordinate; it never deduplicates. Empirically, overlapping per-file ranges yield surviving duplicate timestamps → np.diff(t)>0 is False, violating the code's own stated requirement (line 92-93) that the CF time coordinate be strictly monotonic. The existing assertion `np.all(np.diff(t) > 0)` (line 250) passes only because the test inputs are non-overlapping.

So both highest-risk cross-file assembly cases are genuinely untested, and the gap would let the two real defects pass undetected. Severity 'minor' is appropriate for a test-gap finding (does not itself break code but masks two genuine latent bugs). Location tests/test_perturb_combo.py:33 is an accurate anchor (start of TestMakeCombo/test_depth_combo).

title: GPS longitude interpolation/extrapolation not dateline-safe (raw-degree interp)
description: GPSFromCSV and GPSFromNetCDF build interp1d over longitude in raw degrees with fill_value='extrapolate' and no antimeridian wrapping. Any query whose surrounding GPS fixes straddle +/-180 is corrupted. Reproduced: fixes at +179.95 deg and -179.9 deg interpolate to ~0.0 deg (mid-Atlantic/Africa) instead of ~180 deg; and extrapolation of a track approaching 180 (179.0 -> 179.9) yields 181.7 deg (wrong hemisphere, never wrapped back to ~ -178.3). The interpolated/extrapolated lon feeds TEOS-10 absolute-salinity conversion and the file geospatial attributes, so a dateline-straddling deployment gets wrong salinity and wrong position metadata. The codebase already has a dateline-safe wrap helper (_wrap_deg, plot/xaxis.py:67-69) used in the plotting path, confirming the project is antimeridian-aware elsewhere but this gap is unguarded. NOTE: the pinned ARCTERX SN479 data (Saipan ~145.7E) does not cross the dateline, so this is latent for that campaign but a real failure mode for any west-Pacific transect reaching 180. Medium confidence on practical impact for current data, high confidence the math is wrong at the dateline.
suggested_fix: Unwrap longitudes to a continuous branch before constructing the interpolator (e.g. np.unwrap on radians, or interpolate cos/sin of lon) and wrap results back into [-180,180) on output. The existing _wrap_deg in plot/xaxis.py can be reused for the output wrap.
verify_reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL (math defect), severity down-graded major->minor (latent, narrow trigger).

Mechanism confirmed by reading the code: GPSFromCSV (gps.py:184-189) and GPSFromNetCDF (gps.py:287-288) both build scipy interp1d directly over longitude in raw degrees with fill_value='extrapolate' and no antimeridian/unwrap handling. There is no wrapping guard anywhere in the chain: gps.lon() output flows unwrapped into ctd.py:167-201 (where lines 196-200 also compute raw-degree dlondt differences, which would likewise blow up across the dateline) and into seawater.py:54 gsw.SA_from_SP(SP,P,lon_safe,lat_safe) plus the file position metadata.

Reproduced empirically with the actual scipy interp1d: fixes at +179.95 and -179.9 interpolate to 0.025 deg (wrong hemisphere, should be ~+/-180); extrapolation of 179.0->179.9 yields 180.8 deg, an out-of-[-180,180) value never wrapped back to ~-179.2. The project is antimeridian-aware elsewhere — _wrap_deg at plot/xaxis.py:67-69 — confirming the gap is a genuine omission, not an intentional convention.

Corrections to the finding: (1) the quoted extrapolation result 181.7 deg is inaccurate; with the finding's own inputs (179.0->179.9 extrapolated) scipy gives 180.8 deg. The qualitative claim (unwrapped, out-of-range, wrong hemisphere) still holds. (2) The 'wrong salinity' emphasis is overstated: gsw.SA_from_SP uses lon only for a regional salinity-anomaly correction (a small term, typically <~0.1 g/kg) so the salinity error from a corrupted lon is itself minor; the real harm is corrupted position output/metadata. (3) Latency is real and acknowledged by the finding: the pinned ARCTERX SN479 data (Saipan ~145.7E) never crosses the dateline, so this defect is not exercised by current data and only triggers on a dateline-straddling deployment (plausible but not present). 

Net: the antimeridian math is genuinely wrong and unguarded (high confidence), but because it is dormant for the pinned campaign, has a narrow trigger, and its salinity impact is small, minor is the appropriate severity rather than major.

title: CTD gap-decay differences raw-degree longitudes (dateline-corrupted dlondt)
description: _assign_gps_with_casts computes dlondt = (gps.lon(t0) - lon0)/dt_total (line 196) and then lon[gap] = gps.lon(t) - dlondt*dt (line 200), all in raw degrees. Because the underlying gps.lon() interpolation is not dateline-safe (see gps.py finding), during a reel-in gap that crosses the antimeridian both the offset difference and the per-bin gps.lon(t) call return garbage. Reproduced a westward dateline crossing in the gap: gps.lon at the mid-gap bin returns ~0.025 deg and the assigned bin lon is ~0.025 deg, whereas the true position is ~+/-180 deg. Even with a dateline-safe interpolator, the raw-degree subtraction lon0 vs gps.lon(t0) and gps.lon(t) - dlondt*dt would still need wrap-aware differencing if lon0 (the held drop-point fix) and the ship fix sit on opposite sides of 180. This is the concrete cross-module consumer that amplifies the gps.py defect into wrong CTD bin positions during the reel-in gap.
suggested_fix: Perform the offset/decay arithmetic on wrapped longitude differences (e.g. _wrap_deg(gps.lon(t0) - lon0) for the offset, and _wrap_deg(gps.lon(t) - decay_term)) so the difference and re-add are taken on the short arc, then wrap the final lon back into [-180,180).
verify_reasoning: VERIFIED REAL. _assign_gps_with_casts (src/odas_tpw/perturb/ctd.py:191-201) does all longitude arithmetic in raw degrees: line 196 computes dlondt = (gps.lon(t0) - lon0)/dt_total and line 200 computes lon[gap] = gps.lon(t) - dlondt*dt. The underlying gps.lon() (GPSFromCSV/GPSFromNetCDF in src/odas_tpw/perturb/gps.py:184-189, 287-288) uses plain scipy interp1d on raw-degree longitude with no dateline/wrap handling. A _wrap_deg helper exists in the repo (src/odas_tpw/perturb/plot/xaxis.py:67) but is NOT applied here. I empirically reproduced the exact scenario in the finding: a westward antimeridian crossing (GPS fixes 179.9 -> -179.9 deg) yields a mid-gap gps.lon of ~0.0 (true ~+/-180) and an assigned bin lon of ~0.025 deg, matching the cited numbers. Both the gps.lon(t) term and the raw-degree subtraction lon0 vs gps.lon(t0) corrupt across the dateline. Downstream the corrupted lon flows into the recorded ds[\"lon\"] position (ctd.py:347) and into gsw.SA_from_SP(SP, P, lon, lat) (src/odas_tpw/perturb/seawater.py:54). Down-graded major->minor because: (1) it triggers only in the rare conjunction of cast-based VMP processing + a reel-in gap + the ship crossing the antimeridian during that gap; (2) the dominant TEOS-10 consequence is small -- longitude is only a minor regional correction in SA_from_SP (sub-g/kg), so salinity/density error is modest; the chief consequence is a wrong recorded bin position in that edge geography; (3) the project's actual ARCTERX dataset is near 145E (far from the dateline) and is unaffected, though the code is a general-purpose provider so the correctness defect stands. The finding's localization, mechanism, reproduction, and suggested wrap-aware fix are all accurate; only the 'major' severity label is overstated.

title: GPS numeric time column assumed epoch seconds with no validation or time_format option
description: _to_epoch_seconds passes any non-datetime64 numeric column through unchanged (line 31), assuming it is already epoch seconds. There is no validation (e.g. sanity range check) and no config knob to declare the format. By contrast the hotel loader supports time_format = auto|seconds|epoch|iso via _parse_time (hotel.py:138-169) and even handles relative seconds. The gps config section (config.py:29-38) has NO time_format field. Consequence: a GPS CSV exported with file-relative seconds, MATLAB datenum, days-since-epoch, or any non-epoch numeric time is silently accepted and mislocates EVERY cast, because the query side adds pf.start_time.timestamp() (ctd.py:331) expecting the GPS axis to be true epoch seconds. String/ISO and datetime64 columns are handled (pandas parses them / the datetime64 branch fires), so the trap is specifically numeric-but-not-epoch columns. No warning is emitted. Medium-high confidence this is a real footgun given the documented hotel asymmetry.
suggested_fix: Add a gps.time_format option mirroring hotel (auto|seconds|epoch|iso) and route through the same parsing, or at minimum range-check numeric time columns (e.g. warn if values are not plausibly within a few decades of now in epoch seconds) so a non-epoch axis fails loudly instead of silently mislocating casts.
verify_reasoning: Verified against the actual code. (1) gps.py:29-31 `_to_epoch_seconds` converts only datetime64 columns; every other numeric dtype is returned via `values.astype(np.float64)` with zero validation, treating it as already-epoch seconds. (2) The gps config section (config.py:29-38) has no `time_format` field — only source/lat/lon/file/time_col/lat_col/lon_col/max_time_diff. (3) The hotel loader genuinely has the missing knob: config.py:43 `hotel.time_format: "auto"`, wired through load_hotel(hotel.py:189-226) into `_parse_time`(hotel.py:138-169), which supports auto|seconds|epoch|iso and distinguishes relative vs epoch. (4) The query side at ctd.py:331-332 adds `pf.start_time.timestamp()`, so the GPS axis MUST be true epoch seconds; a CSV/NetCDF numeric axis in file-relative seconds, MATLAB datenum, or days-since-epoch would be silently interpolated/extrapolated and mislocate casts (feeding TEOS-10 absolute salinity and geospatial attrs). String/ISO is parsed by pandas (and interp1d would reject object dtype), so the trap is specifically numeric-but-not-epoch, exactly as described. The asymmetry with hotel is real and the consequence is real. Two reasons I downgrade major→minor: it requires a misconfigured/non-standard GPS input file (default/typical exports are ISO strings or proper CF-units NetCDF, both handled correctly), and the finding's 'No warning is emitted' is overstated — for the common bad cases (file-relative seconds ~0-3600, datenum ~7.3e5, days-since-epoch ~2e4) the mis-scaled _t_min/_t_max differ from real epoch queries (~1.7e9) by orders of magnitude, so `_warn_outside_coverage`(gps.py:204-224) fires a (coverage, not unit) warning. So it is a genuine robustness/footgun gap and asymmetry worth fixing, but not silent in the most common failure modes and not triggerable by a correctly-formatted file.

title: No test coverage for dateline-crossing longitude or duplicate timestamps in GPS
description: The GPS test suite only exercises longitudes in the 90-150 deg range (e.g. lon=[100,110,120]) and never tests fixes straddling +/-180, so the raw-degree interpolation/extrapolation dateline bug (gps.py) and the CTD gap-decay differencing bug (ctd.py:196-201) pass CI undetected. There is also no test for duplicate timestamps in the GPS time column, nor for a non-epoch numeric time column (test_helper_passthrough_for_numeric only asserts passthrough, which is the bug's behavior, not a guard against it). These gaps let the position-correctness defects above ship unnoticed.
suggested_fix: Add tests: (1) GPS fixes spanning +179.x to -179.x and assert the interpolated/extrapolated position stays near +/-180 (within a fraction of a degree); (2) duplicate timestamps producing a deterministic, documented result; (3) the cast-aware ctd path with a dateline-crossing reel-in gap; (4) a non-epoch numeric GPS time column either raising or warning.
verify_reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL test-gap. I read tests/test_perturb_gps.py (lines 1-259) and the underlying code.

Gap is real: (1) Every GPS test uses longitudes in the 100-150 deg range (e.g. lon=[100,110,120] at lines 69,90,109,193,247); none straddle +/-180, so no test exercises antimeridian interpolation/extrapolation. (2) Every test time column is strictly increasing [0,1,2]; no test covers duplicate timestamps. (3) test_helper_passthrough_for_numeric (lines 172-179) only asserts numeric passthrough; it does not guard against a non-epoch numeric column.

The missing dateline test corresponds to a GENUINE underlying defect: GPSFromCSV (gps.py:184-189) and GPSFromNetCDF (gps.py:287-288) build scipy interp1d directly on raw-degree lon arrays with fill_value='extrapolate', and grep confirms ZERO unwrap/wrap/180 handling anywhere in gps.py or ctd.py. Fixes at +179.9 and -179.9 would linearly interpolate through ~0 instead of ~180. The same raw-degree differencing recurs in ctd.py:196-201 (dlondt = (gps.lon(t0)-lon0)/dt_total then lon[gap] = gps.lon(t) - dlondt*dt), so the dateline error propagates into the cast gap-decay positions. These would ship undetected by the current suite.

Severity downgraded considerations: it is a test gap, not broken behavior, and the underlying dateline bug is latent for the actual ARCTERX deployment (~145 deg E, far from the antimeridian per CLAUDE.md). The finding's aside that numeric passthrough is 'the bug's behavior' slightly overstates one part — gps.py docstring (lines 6-9) documents that numeric callers must supply epoch seconds, so that passthrough is intentional/documented. But the core claim (no dateline test, no duplicate-timestamp test, and these omissions hide a real raw-degree position defect) is fully substantiated. Minor is the correct severity for a test gap over a latent-but-real correctness bug.

title: process_l3_chi raises uncaught IndexError on a too-short array salinity argument
description: In _process_section_chi, an array `salinity` is indexed with the global window index matrix via `np.asarray(salinity)[indices]` (line 154) with no prior length check against l2_chi.n_time. If a caller of the public `process_l3_chi` passes an array shorter than the L2 time base, this raises a bare IndexError; if it passes a longer array it silently misaligns the per-window salinity (wrong viscosity). Confirmed empirically: a length-100 salinity array on a 2048-sample input raises IndexError. This is NOT reachable in the shipped pipelines: rsi/pipeline.py resolves salinity through `_resolve_salinity`, which only forwards an array when `l1.salinity.size == l1.n_time` and otherwise collapses a mismatched-length user array to a scalar nanmean; scalar/None inputs take the safe branches at l3_chi.py:155-165. So this is a latent public-API robustness gap, not an active bug in normal operation. Note the IndexError is also not of the (ValueError, RuntimeError) type that the pipeline's try/except at pipeline.py:388 would catch, so a direct caller gets an unguarded crash. Low confidence that this is exercised in practice given the in-repo guards.
suggested_fix: At the top of process_l3_chi, when salinity is array-like, validate `np.asarray(salinity).shape[0] == l2_chi.n_time` and raise a clear ValueError (which the pipeline already catches) or fall back to its nanmean, mirroring _resolve_salinity's contract.
verify_reasoning:
Verified the mechanism in src/odas_tpw/chi/l3_chi.py. At line 154, _process_section_chi does `np.mean(np.asarray(salinity)[indices], axis=1)` where `indices` (line 117-118) is a matrix of GLOBAL indices into the L2 time base (`starts = sec_start + np.arange(...)*diss_step`). There is no length check of the array `salinity` against l2_chi.n_time anywhere in process_l3_chi or _process_section_chi (the only guards before line 154 are `salinity is not None` at 152 and `np.ndim(salinity) > 0` at 153). Empirically reproduced: a length-100 array against an index matrix reaching 2047 raises `IndexError: index 100 is out of bounds for axis 0 with size 100`; a longer (e.g. length-5000) array does NOT error and silently maps global indices into the wrong positions, giving a misaligned per-window salinity and hence wrong viscosity. So the defect genuinely exists in the code as described.

Reachability assessment (confirms the finding's own "not active in shipped pipelines" claim): (1) rsi/pipeline.py:382 forwards `chi_salinity` from _resolve_salinity (pipeline.py:69-73), which returns the per-sample array only when `l1.salinity.size == l1.n_time`, and collapses any mismatched-length user array to `float(np.nanmean(...))`, so only aligned arrays or scalars reach process_l3_chi. The except at pipeline.py:388 catches only (ValueError, RuntimeError) — confirmed it would NOT catch IndexError — but the upstream guard prevents the array path from ever mismatching. (2) rsi/chi_io.py:151 passes `sal_prof = sal_fast[s_fast:e_fast]` (chi_io.py:125); sal_fast comes from prepare_profiles (helpers.py:323-336), which forces it to scalar or an array on the fast time base (len(t_fast)) and otherwise raises ValueError at helpers.py:332, so the slice is always aligned to the profile/L2 length. (3) pyturb/eps.py:241 wraps the call in a broad `except Exception` (eps.py:242), catching any IndexError, and pyturb is third-party. Thus no in-repo caller can trigger either the crash or the silent misalignment.

Conclusion: real but latent public-API robustness gap with an undocumented length precondition (the docstring at l3_chi.py:234-235 only says "Practical salinity for viscosity" / "float or array or None"; it does not state the array must be n_time-aligned). Not an active bug in any shipped path; correctly classified as a nit. The suggested fix (validate length and raise a clear ValueError, which the pipeline already catches, or fall back to nanmean mirroring _resolve_salinity) is appropriate. The finding's description and self-assessment are accurate; I keep severity at nit.

title: scalar._time_subset raises cryptic UFuncTypeError on a combo whose time is numeric (no CF units)
description: scalar.run opens the CTD combo with default CF decoding (scalar.py:303), and _time_subset assumes ds['time'].values is datetime64 so it can compare t >= sec.start / t <= sec.stop against the np.datetime64 values produced by parse_time. For the pipeline's own combo this holds: make_ctd_combo -> make_combo reads with decode_times=False (combo.py:77) but apply_schema unconditionally attaches CTD_SCHEMA's units='seconds since 1970-01-01' / standard_name='time' (netcdf_schema.py:358-362, apply_schema 455-463), so to_netcdf encodes a CF-time and the default open re-decodes it to datetime64 (verified by round-trip: xarray 2026.2.0 re-decodes to datetime64[ns]). The gap is for a user-supplied --ctd-combo (.nc accepted directly, scalar.py:271,291) or an older/external combo whose time variable lacks decodable CF units: default open then leaves time as float64, and `t >= sec.start` (float64 vs datetime64) raises numpy.UFuncTypeError 'greater_equal did not contain a loop ...' (reproduced). This only triggers when --start or --stop is given; with neither, the all-True mask leaves the numeric file plotting fine (to_epoch_seconds handles either dtype). profiles._profile_window does NOT share this hazard because it opens with decode_times=False and coerces stime to float, then converts sec.start/stop via to_epoch_seconds (profiles.py:92-97) -- a deliberately dtype-agnostic contract. So the two windowing helpers diverge in robustness: profiles tolerates either time encoding, scalar hard-requires CF-decodable datetime64. Note: the inclusive/exclusive edge contract does NOT actually diverge -- both use >= start and <= stop (inclusive both ends).
suggested_fix: Make _time_subset dtype-agnostic to match _profile_window: if ds['time'] is not datetime64, compare in epoch seconds using xaxis.to_epoch_seconds on both the time array and sec.start/sec.stop (or, conversely, normalise sec.start/stop to the time array's representation). At minimum, detect a numeric time axis and raise a clear message ('CTD combo time lacks CF units / is not decodable; ...') instead of letting a raw UFuncTypeError escape.
verify_reasoning:
Verified every claim by reading the code and reproducing on the repo's xarray (2026.2.0).

scalar._time_subset (src/odas_tpw/perturb/plot/scalar.py:128-134) builds the mask via `t >= sec.start` / `t <= sec.stop`, where sec.start/stop are np.datetime64 produced by parse_time (sections.py:85-109). scalar.run opens the combo with default CF decoding (scalar.py:303), and the only dtype-related guard is `if "time" not in ds.dims` (scalar.py:306), which checks the dimension, not the dtype.

For the pipeline's own combo this is safe: make_ctd_combo -> make_combo reads binned files with decode_times=False (combo.py:77) but apply_schema unconditionally attaches CTD_SCHEMA's units="seconds since 1970-01-01" / standard_name="time" (netcdf_schema.py:358-362, apply_schema 455-462), so to_netcdf writes CF time and the default open re-decodes to datetime64 (reproduced: CF-units file re-opens as datetime64[ns] and the comparison succeeds).

The gap is a user-supplied or external/older --ctd-combo .nc (accepted directly at scalar.py:271,288-291) whose time variable lacks decodable CF units: default open leaves it float64, and `float64 >= np.datetime64` raises numpy.UFuncTypeError "greater_equal did not contain a loop ..." (reproduced exactly). It only fires when --start or --stop is given; with neither, the all-True mask plots fine because the downstream x-axis uses to_epoch_seconds (xaxis.py:72-82), which accepts both dtypes.

profiles._profile_window (profiles.py:92-98) is deliberately dtype-agnostic: it opens with decode_times=False (profiles.py:321), coerces stime to float, and converts sec.start/stop via to_epoch_seconds, so the two windowing helpers genuinely diverge in robustness. The inclusive/exclusive edge contract does NOT diverge -- both use >= start and <= stop.

Test gap confirmed: the test combo (tests/test_perturb_plot_scalar.py:24-71) writes time as datetime64[ns] (no units attr), which xarray encodes on write and re-decodes to datetime64 on read, so no test ever exercises a numeric-time CF-unit-less combo with --start/--stop.

Severity minor is correct: the failure is a cryptic raw exception instead of a clear message or graceful handling, and it only triggers on a non-pipeline input (external/older combo) combined with a time window; the normal pipeline flow is unaffected. Confidence high on the mechanism; the only soft point is whether feeding an arbitrary external .nc to --ctd-combo is a fully supported use case (the help text implies a pipeline-produced combo), but the flag accepts any .nc with no provenance validation and an older-version pipeline combo is a plausible real input, and the asymmetry vs the intentionally dtype-agnostic _profile_window substantiates the robustness gap.

title: No test covers scalar._time_subset against a numeric (non-CF-decoded) time axis
description: The scalar test fixture writes time as a datetime64[ns] coordinate (test_perturb_plot_scalar.py:44,66) which round-trips to datetime64 on default open, so every scalar test exercises only the datetime64 branch of _time_subset. There is no test feeding scalar.run a combo whose time is numeric epoch seconds without CF units (the case that raises UFuncTypeError when --start/--stop is set, as reproduced). Because profiles deliberately supports the numeric-time contract and scalar does not, a regression that further hard-codes datetime64 assumptions in the scalar path would go unnoticed. Adding a fixture variant with numeric time + a start/stop window would lock down the intended cross-subcommand contract.
suggested_fix: Add a scalar test that writes combo.nc with a numeric 'time' variable lacking CF units (or with units only in .attrs) plus a section that sets start/stop, asserting either a successful mask or a clear error -- mirroring the profiles path's numeric-time handling.
verify_reasoning: The finding's narrow factual claims are accurate and verified against the code. The scalar fixture writes time as datetime64[ns] (tests/test_perturb_plot_scalar.py:44 `time = np.array(t_list, dtype="datetime64[ns]")` and :66 `coords={"time": ("time", time)}`); scalar.run opens with default CF decoding (src/odas_tpw/perturb/plot/scalar.py:303, comment "default CF decoding -> datetime64 time"), so every end-to-end test exercises only the datetime64 branch of _time_subset (scalar.py:126-134, which does `t >= sec.start` where sec.start is a np.datetime64 from _parse_time). I confirmed there is no scalar test feeding a numeric-time combo with start/stop. By contrast profiles.py deliberately opens with decode_times=False (profiles.py:321) and converts sec.start/stop to epoch seconds in _profile_window (profiles.py:95-97) before comparing against numeric stime. So the two subcommands genuinely handle time differently and the scalar numeric-time path is untested. This is a valid test-gap nit.\n\nHowever, the finding's stated significance is overstated. I empirically reproduced the mechanism: _time_subset only raises UFuncTypeError when time is numeric AND lacks a parseable CF units attribute (default-decode leaves it float64). When the CF units attribute is present, default-decode converts time back to datetime64 and the comparison succeeds. The real pipeline ALWAYS attaches `units="seconds since 1970-01-01"` to time via apply_schema (netcdf_schema.py:58, called at combo.py:101) and writes it via to_netcdf, so a genuine ctd_combo/combo.nc decodes to datetime64 in scalar and works correctly. Thus the asserted failure occurs only for an input (numeric time with no CF units) that the pipeline does not produce, and there is no real shared cross-subcommand "contract" that scalar violates -- the paths differ because they open files differently, each consistently with how its own combos are written. So it is a true but low-value test-gap observation; severity is correctly nit, not higher. Confidence medium because the gap exists as stated but its premise (a real numeric-time scalar combo) is not substantiated by the pipeline.

title: Trim skip-guard reuses stale truncated output when a repaired source has an equal/older mtime
description: The skip guard returns action='skipped' (dest = the previously written trimmed file) whenever `dest.exists() and dest.stat().st_mtime_ns >= source.stat().st_mtime_ns`, with `not force`. It never re-reads the source header. The trimmed dest is a truncated *prefix* of an earlier source version: it only contains `first_record_size + n_complete*record_size` bytes (trim.py:155-160), i.e. the fractional final record was dropped. If that earlier source was incomplete (mid-acquisition copy, partial transfer) and is later replaced/repaired so it is now COMPLETE but carries an equal or older mtime than the trimmed output, the guard short-circuits and the pipeline keeps reading the stale, truncated copy instead of the now-complete source. This is a real scenario for files restored from backup, `cp -p`/rsync that preserve mtime, tar/unzip extraction (which stamps the archived mtime), or NFS/SMB clock skew where the repaired source's mtime is <= the locally written trimmed output's mtime. Because the guard compares only mtime (no size, no inode, no content hash — confirmed: grep finds no st_size/hash check on the skip path; the only md5 in perturb is merge.py:38 which hashes the *trimmed dest*, not the source), the staleness is silently propagated. Downstream consequence is concrete: run_trim() returns r.dest (pipeline.py:1479), the pipeline maps each source to that dest (pipeline.py:1698-1700 'rewritten (trimmed) or reused (skipped)'), and merge._read_file_metadata reads header_size/config_size/record_size and the config md5 from that trimmed dest (merge.py:25-52), never from the repaired source. So a changed header geometry (header_size/config_size/record_size) or config string between the old and new source versions is never revalidated; all of epsilon/chi/merge then run on truncated and/or stale-geometry data. The symmetric case is safe: a complete source produces no dest (action='referenced', trim.py:152), so it cannot be falsely skipped — the hazard is specific to files that were previously trimmed. NOTE: the second-spawn date-change and MCP reminders injected into this session are unrelated to the code; this finding stands on the code as read.
suggested_fix: Make the skip decision content-aware rather than mtime-only. Cheapest robust fix: on the skip path, read the source's 128-byte header (same cost as the referenced path already pays) and recompute first_record_size + n_complete*record_size from the *current* source; only skip if dest.stat().st_size equals that expected trimmed size AND header geometry (header_size/config_size/record_size/endian) matches what the trimmed file encodes. Alternatively, record the source size+geometry (or a header hash) alongside the trimmed output and compare it before skipping. At minimum, also require dest size consistency and treat any header/geometry mismatch as a re-trim. Add a regression test mirroring test_resaves_when_source_is_newer but for the inverse: write a trimmed output from an incomplete source, replace the source with a complete (or geometry-changed) version whose mtime is set <= the trimmed output's mtime via os.utime, and assert the run does NOT skip (re-trims/re-references to the repaired source).
verify_reasoning:
Verified against the actual code. The skip guard at src/odas_tpw/perturb/trim.py:116-121 is exactly `not force and dest.exists() and dest.stat().st_mtime_ns >= source.stat().st_mtime_ns -> TrimResult(dest, "skipped", 0, 0)`. It compares mtime only: no st_size, inode, or content check, and the source header is never re-read on this path (the header read happens only after the guard at lines 123-137). The trimmed dest is a truncated prefix of the source: `first_record_size + n_complete*record_size` bytes (trim.py:155-160), dropping the fractional final record. So if a previously-trimmed file's source is later replaced by a now-complete (or geometry/config-changed) version carrying an equal-or-older mtime, the guard short-circuits and the stale truncated dest is reused.

Downstream propagation confirmed: run_trim returns r.dest (pipeline.py:1479); pipeline.py:1699-1700 selects that dest for both "trimmed" and "skipped"; merge._read_merge_info (merge.py:18-52) reads header_size/config_size/record_size/endian and the config md5 (md5 at merge.py:38) from that path, never from the source, so a changed header geometry or config between source versions is never revalidated. The complete-file ("referenced") path is correctly immune because it writes no dest (trim.py:150-152), confirming the finding's symmetric-case claim — the hazard is specific to files that were previously trimmed.

Test gap confirmed: tests/test_perturb_trim.py has test_resaves_when_source_is_newer (lines 156-171) covering only the newer-source case; there is no inverse test for an equal/older-mtime repaired source. grep over perturb finds no size/hash check on the skip path; the only md5 (merge.py:38) hashes the trimmed dest's config, not the source.

Minor inaccuracies in the finding that do not affect the verdict: the merge function is named `_read_merge_info`, not `_read_file_metadata`, and the line span is 18-52 (md5 at 38) rather than 25-52. The substance is correct.

The trigger requires a specific operational sequence (previously-trimmed file whose source is replaced by a complete/changed version with mtime <= the trimmed output), achievable via cp -p / rsync -t / tar/zip extraction / backup restore / NFS clock skew — realistic but conditional. Given silent processing of truncated or stale-geometry raw data when it occurs, major (not blocker) is the correct severity. The injected date-change and MCP reminders in this session are unrelated to the code and do not affect the analysis.

title: CTD time-binner uses np.clip (folds NaN/out-of-range times into edge bins) instead of the in-range mask the depth engine adopted
description: `_time_bin` computes `bin_idx = np.digitize(t, bin_edges) - 1; bin_idx = np.clip(bin_idx, 0, n_bins-1)`. This is exactly the pattern the depth engine deliberately abandoned: `binning.py:34-36` documents that the previous np.clip "silently dumped" NaN/out-of-range samples into the first/last bin and replaced it with `_bin_indices` returning an `in_range = np.isfinite(coords) & (idx>=0) & (idx<n_bins)` mask (binning.py:31-45). I verified the fold mechanism numerically: np.digitize(NaN)=len(edges), so after the -1 offset a NaN time lands at index n_bins-1 and clip leaves it in the LAST bin; a time below t_lo clips to bin 0; a time above t_hi clips to the last bin. In all three cases _bin_indices would correctly exclude the sample. Both the mean path (ctd.py:104-125) and median path (ctd.py:77-102) consume this bin_idx, so a folded sample would pollute the edge bin's mean/median, n_samples, and *_std. IMPORTANT REACHABILITY CAVEAT (low confidence that it fires today): on the actual inputs this is currently latent, not an active corruption. `_time_bin` is only ever called from `ctd_bin_file` (ctd.py:309,316), always with shared `bin_edges`. The time arguments are `pf.t_slow`/`pf.t_fast`, built as `np.arange(...)/fs` (p_file.py:470-471) — strictly monotonic from 0, no NaN. The shared edges `np.arange(t_lo, t_hi+bin_width, bin_width)` (ctd.py:302) reliably yield a last edge strictly > t_hi (I tested 200k randomized t_hi/bin_width combos: zero cases where the last edge <= t_hi), so no sample ever lands past the last edge or before the first. Hotel/trim/stratification mutate channel DATA (whose NaNs are correctly handled by the np.isfinite guards), not the time vectors. So clip and the in-range mask produce identical results on every input reachable today. The defect is a divergence from the engine's now-documented invariant and a fragility: the docstring (ctd.py:328-330) and epoch handling anticipate hotel-supplied/merged absolute time axes, and the moment any non-arange or NaN-bearing time reaches _time_bin, the edge bins of the CTD product would be silently corrupted with no warning.
suggested_fix: Replace the clip with the same in-range mask the depth engine uses: compute `idx = np.digitize(t, bin_edges) - 1; idx = np.where(t == bin_edges[-1], n_bins-1, idx); in_range = np.isfinite(t) & (idx>=0) & (idx<n_bins)`, then restrict bincount/bin-slice operations to `idx[in_range]` (mean path) and exclude out-of-range samples before the argsort/searchsorted (median path). Ideally call `binning._bin_indices` directly so the two binners share one implementation and invariant.
verify_reasoning:
VERIFIED as an accurate description of a real code divergence, with the latent/non-firing caveat correctly stated by the finding itself.

Code confirmed:
- ctd.py:69-70: `bin_idx = np.digitize(t, bin_edges) - 1` then `bin_idx = np.clip(bin_idx, 0, n_bins - 1)`. Both the median path (lines 81-101) and mean path (lines 105-123) consume this bin_idx for n_samples (line 75), the aggregate, and *_std.
- binning.py:31-45: the depth engine's `_bin_indices` deliberately uses an in-range mask `in_range = np.isfinite(coords) & (idx>=0) & (idx<n_bins)`, and its docstring explicitly states "the previous np.clip silently dumped them into the first/last bin." So ctd._time_bin uses the exact anti-pattern the sibling engine documented and abandoned. _bin_std/_bin_array (binning.py:48-67+) restrict to idx[finite & in_range].

Fold mechanism verified numerically (edges 0..10, 10 bins): NaN -> digitize-1 = 10 -> clip = 9 (last bin); below t_lo -> -1 -> clip = 0 (first bin); above t_hi -> 10 -> clip = 9 (last bin). All three would be EXCLUDED by the in-range mask. So a folded sample would pollute the edge bin's mean/median, n_samples, and *_std.

Reachability (matches finding, low confidence it fires today):
- _time_bin is only called from ctd_bin_file (ctd.py:309, 316), always with pf.t_slow/pf.t_fast and a shared bin_edges.
- pf.t_fast/pf.t_slow are built as np.arange(...)/fs (p_file.py:470-471): strictly monotonic from 0, no NaN. grep shows no reassignment of t_slow/t_fast in the perturb processing path (only unrelated reads in pipeline.py:527 and plot/diagnostics.py). Hotel/trim/stratification mutate channel DATA (NaNs there are correctly handled by np.isfinite at ctd.py:106 and the median finite-guard at 92), not the time vectors.
- Shared edges np.arange(t_lo, t_hi+bin_width, bin_width) (ctd.py:302): in 200k randomized (t_lo, t_hi, bin_width) trials, the last edge was NEVER <= t_hi, so no real sample lands past the last edge or before the first. Thus clip and the in-range mask produce identical results on every input reachable today.

Verdict: real divergence from the now-documented binning invariant and a genuine latent fragility (the docstring at ctd.py:328-330 and the epoch handling anticipate merged/hotel-supplied absolute time axes; a NaN-bearing or non-arange time reaching _time_bin would silently corrupt edge bins with no warning). It is not an active corruption on current inputs. Severity 'minor' is appropriate: a code-quality/consistency defect that is currently latent rather than producing wrong outputs. Not over-claimed; kept at minor rather than upgrading.

title: No CTD test covers NaN or out-of-range times in _time_bin, so the clip-fold regression is invisible to CI
description: The _time_bin tests (tests/test_perturb_ctd.py:51-81) only feed clean, in-range, monotonic time vectors; none injects a NaN time or a time below t_lo / above t_hi. As a result the np.clip edge-fold behavior at ctd.py:69-70 — and any future regression from changing it — is entirely untested. The depth engine's equivalent invariant (NaN/out-of-range coords excluded, not folded) is the kind of contract that warrants an explicit guard test, especially since ctd.py's docstring anticipates absolute/hotel-supplied time axes that could carry gaps. This is a test gap rather than an independent bug, but it is why the clip divergence could persist undetected.
suggested_fix: Add a _time_bin test that passes a time vector containing a NaN and one sample below the first edge and one above the last edge (with explicit bin_edges), and assert those samples do NOT contribute to any bin's mean/median/n_samples (i.e. the polluted edge bins stay NaN/zero-count). The test should currently fail with the clip implementation and pass once an in-range mask is adopted.
verify_reasoning:
Verified all three factual claims of this test-gap finding by reading the actual code.

1. Test gap confirmed: All five TestTimeBin tests (tests/test_perturb_ctd.py:48-83) feed clean, finite, in-range, monotonic time vectors (test_basic_mean t=[0..1.1]; test_median_method t=arange(10); test_diagnostics_output t=arange(20); test_empty_bins t=[0,0.1,10,10.1] all in-range; test_single_point one in-range point). None injects a NaN time nor a time below t_lo/above t_hi.

2. Clip-fold defect confirmed at ctd.py:69-70. Reproduced: a below-range time (raw digitize-1 = -1) folds into bin 0 and pollutes its mean; an above-range time (raw idx n_bins) and a NaN time (np.digitize(nan) -> len(edges) -> idx n_bins) both fold into the last bin. The finite-value mask only filters NaN *values*, not NaN/out-of-range *times*, so finite data at those positions corrupts edge-bin means/counts.

3. Contract divergence confirmed. The depth engine src/odas_tpw/perturb/binning.py:_bin_indices (lines 31-45) deliberately replaced this exact np.clip pattern with an in_range EXCLUDE mask, and its docstring explicitly states: "the previous np.clip silently dumped them into the first/last bin." ctd.py:_time_bin still uses the abandoned clip pattern, so the asymmetry the finding describes is accurate.

Severity: minor is correct (no upgrade).
