The clip-fold is currently dormant: _time_bin is only ever called (ctd.py:309,317) with pf.t_slow/pf.t_fast, which are np.arange(...)/fs (p_file.py:470-471) — finite, monotonic, evenly spaced — and bin_edges is derived from nanmin/nanmax of those same vectors (ctd.py:297-304), so every sample is in-range and the only clip that fires is the benign fold of the lone t_max sample into the last bin. The finding correctly self-categorizes this as a test gap rather than an active bug. Minor caveat: the finding's appeal to the docstring "anticipating hotel/absolute time axes" overstates the live exposure — _time_bin is not wired to those external axes today, so manifesting the bug requires a future code change routing a gappy time axis into _time_bin; that makes this a defensive/regression-guard test, consistent with minor.

title: Reel-in gap decay differences raw longitudes — multi-bin antimeridian corruption distinct from the lon0 selection
description: In `_assign_gps_with_casts`, the reel-in/steam gap loop computes the ship-relative longitude offset and decays it linearly across the gap entirely in raw (wrapped) degrees. Line 196 computes `dlondt = (float(_g(gps.lon, t0)[0]) - lon0) / dt_total` and line 200 sets `lon[gap] = _g(gps.lon, t) - dlondt * dt`. When the ship crosses the antimeridian during a reel-in gap (e.g. lon0 = drop point ~179.9 deg E vs gps.lon(t0) ~ -179.95 after the ship just crossed into the western hemisphere), the difference `(gps.lon(t0) - lon0)` jumps by ~360 deg. Because that bogus ~360-deg step is divided by dt_total and re-applied per bin via `dlondt * dt`, the error is smeared linearly across EVERY bin in the gap, not confined to a single difference. I verified numerically: with lon0=179.9, gps.lon(t0)=-179.95 over a 100 s gap, dlondt = -3.5985 deg/s (a -359.85 deg total spurious drift), and gap bins that should all read ~ -179.9 instead read 176.3 deg (t near gap start), 0.005 deg (gap mid-point — a ~180 deg error, placing the bin in the Gulf of Guinea), and -176.3 deg (t near gap end). Line 200 also mixes a wrapped `gps.lon(t)` with the unwrapped degree offset, compounding the discontinuity. This is a DISTINCT mechanism from the lon0 = lon[first-1] selection at lines 191-192: those lines merely copy a previously-stored array value (no degree subtraction, no intrinsic dateline hazard), whereas the corruption originates in the differencing at 196 and the per-bin decay at 200. The cited '191-201' lon0 finding therefore does not cover this decay arithmetic. The corrupted lon flows lat,lon -> add_seawater_properties (ctd.py:351) -> gsw.SA_from_SP(SP, P, lon_safe, lat_safe) (seawater.py:54), which uses a global lon/lat atlas for the Absolute Salinity Anomaly; a bin mislocated by up to ~180 deg pulls the wrong region into the SA conversion, so SA, sigma0, and rho (rho derives from SA/CT) are all perturbed in the CTD binned product for any cast/reel-in spanning the antimeridian. The lat path (197/201) is dateline-safe (no ±90 wrap), but the lon path is not. No test exercises the dateline: all GPS stubs in tests/test_perturb_ctd.py use small monotonic longitudes (10..88 deg), so this slips through CI.
suggested_fix: Difference and decay longitude in an unwrapped frame. Before line 196, unwrap relative to lon0: compute the GPS endpoint and per-bin GPS longitudes, subtract lon0, wrap each difference into (-180, 180] (e.g. `d = ((x - lon0 + 180) % 360) - 180`), do the linear decay on those wrapped deltas, then add lon0 back and re-wrap the result into (-180, 180]. Equivalently, work in a local tangent frame anchored at lon0. Add a regression test with a GPS provider whose longitude crosses ±180 during a reel-in gap and assert all gap-bin longitudes stay within ~1 deg of the drop point / ship track (no excursion toward 0 deg).
verify_reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL. The cited code at src/odas_tpw/perturb/ctd.py:196-200 computes the ship-relative longitude offset (dlondt = (gps.lon(t0) - lon0)/dt_total, line 196) and decays it linearly across every gap bin (lon[gap] = gps.lon(t) - dlondt*dt, line 200) entirely in raw wrapped degrees. I grep-confirmed there is NO longitude wrapping/unwrapping anywhere in ctd.py, gps.py, or seawater.py. The GPS providers (gps.py:191-300) do plain scipy interp1d on raw stored longitudes with no unwrap.

Numerics reproduced exactly: with lon0=179.9 and gps.lon(t0)=-179.95 over a 100 s gap, dlondt = -3.5985 deg/s (a -359.85 deg spurious total drift); gap bins read +176.35 (start), +0.025 (mid-point, ~180 deg error), -176.30 (end) — matching the finding's stated values to the digit.

Triggerability confirmed: lon0 = lon[first-1] is the prior cast's stored bin = gps.lon(cast_start); the offset gps.lon(cast_end) - lon0 is the ship movement during the cast. If the ship crosses +/-180 during the down-cast (or cast_start and cast_end straddle the antimeridian), the two wrapped fixes differ by ~360 deg and that bogus step smears across the whole reel-in gap. This is realistic for any West/Central Pacific deployment near the dateline.

Distinctness confirmed: lines 191-192 merely copy a stored array value (no degree subtraction); the corruption arithmetic genuinely lives in the differencing (196) and per-bin decay (200), so this is a separate mechanism from the lon0-selection finding.

Downstream flow confirmed: corrupted lon -> add_seawater_properties (ctd.py:351) -> gsw.SA_from_SP(SP, P, lon_safe, lat_safe) (seawater.py:54), which uses lon via the SA-anomaly atlas. Lat path is dateline-safe (no +/-90 wrap; a cast cannot straddle +/-90). No test exercises the dateline: tests/test_perturb_ctd.py uses _MovingGPS (lon=2t, range 10..88) and GPSFixed (145/0/134), so CI misses it.

Severity downgraded major -> minor for two substantiated reasons: (1) the trigger is narrow — the shipped ARCTERX dataset is at ~145 deg E, nowhere near 180, so it never fires on real data; this is latent in general-purpose code. (2) The finding overstates the seawater consequence: I measured gsw.SA_from_SP at the correct vs corrupted lon (179.9 vs 0.025) and SA shifts only ~0.001 g/kg (~1 part in 35,000), so SA/sigma0/rho are perturbed but negligibly. The genuinely substantive defect is the position metadata (written lat/lon) being wrong by up to ~180 deg — a real data-integrity bug, but bounded in blast radius. Net: real, correctly diagnosed mechanism, minor practical impact.

title: _iterative_fit returns a finite chi even when the Batchelor fit failed (kB=NaN), unlike the other two chi paths
description: LOW CONFIDENCE / structural asymmetry, could not trigger with realistic input. In _iterative_fit the loop variable chi=chi_obs (line 582) is always seeded to a finite, positive raw-integration estimate (lines 511-518; reset to 1e-14 if <=0). If the per-iteration MLE returns a non-finite kB_best (kB_best=NaN at line 538) the loop breaks on the FIRST iteration, leaving epsilon=NaN (line 580 guard), spec_batch=zeros (line 583), fom=NaN (line 589 guard) — but the function still RETURNS the finite chi=chi_obs at line 605. By contrast _mle_fit_kB (lines 408-413) returns chi=NaN when kB is non-finite, and _chi_from_epsilon returns chi=NaN on its failure branches. The downstream consumer _compute_chi_final (l4_chi.py:276) accepts any chi with np.isfinite(chi) & (chi>0) and never checks kB/epsilon validity, so an iterative-method chi with no valid Batchelor wavenumber would silently enter the per-spectrum geometric-mean chi_final, whereas the same failure under the 'mle' method would be correctly dropped as NaN. I could NOT construct a triggering input: _mle_find_kB only returns NaN when (a) <6 valid points — but _iterative_fit's own entry guard at line 501 already requires >=6, so the masks match — or (b) np.all(np.isinf(nll_coarse)) at line 340, which requires spec_obs to be NaN/inf at every fit point (the np.maximum(...,1e-30) clamp at line 336 keeps the NLL finite for any finite spec_obs). So in practice this requires NaN-contaminated spec_obs reaching chi.py, which upstream spectral estimation normally prevents. Reporting as a robustness/consistency gap, not a demonstrated runtime failure.
suggested_fix: Make _iterative_fit consistent with the other two paths: when kB_best is non-finite, return chi=NaN (e.g. set chi = chi_obs if np.isfinite(kB_best) else np.nan before the return at line 582/605), so a failed Batchelor fit cannot contribute a finite chi to _compute_chi_final.
verify_reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL. The structural asymmetry exists exactly as described. In _iterative_fit (chi.py:454-605): chi_obs is seeded finite and positive (lines 511-518, clamped to 1e-14 if <=0). When the kB fit fails, the loop breaks (line 538/539) leaving kB_best=NaN, after which epsilon stays NaN (line 580 guard), spec_batch=zeros (line 583), and fom=NaN (line 589 guard) — yet chi=chi_obs (line 582) is returned finite at line 605. By contrast _mle_fit_kB returns all-NaN on the same kB failure (lines 408-413), and the consumer _compute_chi_final (l4_chi.py:276) admits any value with np.isfinite(chi) & (chi>0) without checking kB/epsilon/fom, so an iterative chi with no valid Batchelor wavenumber would silently enter the per-spectrum geometric mean. So the inconsistency and its downstream consequence are confirmed by the actual code.

REACHABILITY (where the candidate undersold itself): the candidate claimed it could not construct a trigger and reasoned only about the two NaN-return branches of _mle_find_kB. Its argument that the <6-points branch is unreachable is correct — _iterative_fit's entry guard (line 501, min_points=6) matches _mle_find_kB's mask (line 324, min_points=6) — and the np.all(np.isinf(nll_coarse)) branch (line 340) is effectively unreachable due to the np.maximum(...,1e-30) clamp (line 336) for finite spec_obs. HOWEVER, the loop break at line 538 is `if not np.isfinite(kB_best) OR kB_best < 1`. The kB_best < 1 cpm condition is a realistic, physically-motivated 'degenerate/failed fit' break that does NOT require any NaN-contaminated input: a finite kB_best returned below 1 cpm trips it on the first iteration, leaving kB_best finite-but-rejected, epsilon=NaN, spec_batch via grad_func(K, kB_best<1, chi), and chi=chi_obs returned finite. So a finite chi with a rejected/implausible Batchelor wavenumber CAN reach _compute_chi_final via normal data, making this more than a purely theoretical robustness gap.

SEVERITY: minor is right. The leaked chi is the raw above-noise integration estimate (not arbitrary garbage), fom is correctly NaN, and any QC layer that filters on fom would drop it — but _compute_chi_final does not filter on fom, so it can enter the geometric mean. The suggested fix (return chi=NaN when kB_best is non-finite, ideally also when kB_best<1 to match the line 538 reject) is correct and makes the three chi paths consistent. Confidence medium: the code-level facts are certain; the practical frequency of kB_best<1 occurring on real data is unquantified.

title: Per-cast N2/dTdz flat-extrapolated into cast extremes (fabricated stratification)
description: _compute_slow_stratification interpolates each cast's coarse, Thorpe-sorted N2/dTdz onto the slow grid with np.interp(P_c, target_P[good], N2[good]) (lines 488 and 491 for dTdz). np.interp clamps to the endpoints of its xp array (flat extrapolation), it does not return NaN outside the xp range. target_P is np.arange(p_lo, p_hi+step, step) over the cast's finite pressure range (mixing.py:399), so the first/last TARGET CENTRES sit at p_lo and p_hi; but the FINITE subset target_P[good] can be narrower than [p_lo, p_hi] whenever the leading/trailing windows produce NaN. A window yields NaN when it has fewer than min_samples within +/-half_w, when ptp(Pw) < min_dp, or (for N2) when the half-mean pressure separation < min_dp/2 (mixing.py:404-409, 314). When that happens, every cast sample shallower than target_P[good].min() (or deeper than its max) receives the nearest finite N2/dTdz value rather than NaN. I reproduced this: with 2 slow samples in the top window, profile_stratification returns N2[0:2]=NaN, target_P[good].min()=4.0, and np.interp writes the first finite N2 (2.49e-4) onto the cast samples at P=2.0/2.5 dbar. These fabricated values are stored directly in pf.channels['N2'/'dTdz'] (pipeline.py:987,1001) and flow into BOTH the per-profile NetCDF (extract_profiles) and the depth-binned CTD product (ctd.py:268 bins N2/dTdz). Because the clamped value is a real interior magnitude it generally clears the dTdz_min / N2_min floors in mixing_coefficients (mixing.py:474-475), so it is NOT masked and produces fabricated K_T/Gamma/K_rho at the cast extremes. The docstring (pipeline.py:450) advertises 'NaN outside casts', so emitting non-NaN where the windowed method genuinely could not compute a value is inconsistent with the intended contract. Severity is minor because in normal dense slow data (~64 Hz, ~64 samples/dbar) the extreme windows almost always succeed and target_P[good] spans the full cast, so there is no extrapolation; the defect is confined to genuinely sparse/degenerate cast extremes (descent stall, surface bob, or a ptp-failing overturn at the top/bottom).
suggested_fix: Disable extrapolation so out-of-range samples become NaN, e.g. np.interp(P_c, target_P[good], N2[good], left=np.nan, right=np.nan) for both the N2 (line 488) and dTdz (line 491) calls. This preserves interior interpolation but stops fabricating stratification (and the K_T/Gamma derived from it) where the Thorpe-window method produced no estimate.
verify_reasoning: Verified and reproduced. At src/odas_tpw/perturb/pipeline.py:488 and 491, `_compute_slow_stratification` calls `np.interp(P_c, target_P[good], N2[good])` (and the dTdz analogue) with no `left=`/`right=` arguments. np.interp clamps to its xp endpoints (flat-extrapolation), not NaN, outside the xp range. `target_P` (processing/mixing.py:399, np.arange(p_lo, p_hi+step, step)) spans the full finite cast, but the FINITE subset `target_P[good]` is narrower whenever leading/trailing windows yield NaN — which genuinely happens via three independent paths: <min_samples within +/-half_w (mixing.py:405), np.ptp(Pw) < min_dp (mixing.py:408), and the half-mean pressure separation < min_dp/2 in _stable_window (mixing.py:314). I reproduced the defect directly: a cast with 2 sparse surface samples (P=2.0, 2.5) plus dense data from 4 dbar produced N2[0:2]=NaN, target_P[good].min()=4.0, and `np.interp` wrote the first finite N2 (2.50e-4) onto the P=2.0/2.5 samples; with the suggested `left=np.nan, right=np.nan` those become NaN. These fabricated values are stored in pf.channels["N2"]/["dTdz"] (pipeline.py:987,1001) and flow unguarded into both the per-profile NetCDF (extract_profiles) and the depth-binned CTD product (ctd.py:268 selects N2/dTdz into channels_to_bin, ctd.py:291/293 reads pf.channels directly, then time-bins them — no re-masking). Because the clamped value is a real interior magnitude, it clears the dTdz_min/N2_min floors in mixing_coefficients (mixing.py:474-475), so it is NOT masked and yields fabricated K_T/Gamma/K_rho at cast extremes. The docstring (pipeline.py:450) advertises "NaN outside casts", so emitting non-NaN where the windowed method could not compute contradicts the stated contract. Note the finding's location references used a mixing.py path that is actually src/odas_tpw/processing/mixing.py, but every cited line number and behavior matches there. Severity minor is correct: with dense ~64 Hz slow data the extreme windows almost always succeed so target_P[good] spans the full cast and no extrapolation occurs; the defect is confined to genuinely sparse/degenerate cast extremes (surface bob, descent stall, or a ptp-failing overturn at top/bottom). The suggested fix is correct.

title: np.trapezoid hazard under declared numpy>=1.26 floor breaks the ENTIRE chi subsystem, quick-look, and 3 more shear-L4 sites (not just l4.py:366)
description: pyproject.toml:17 declares `numpy>=1.26,<3`, which permits installing numpy 1.26.x. The code calls `np.trapezoid` (with `import numpy as np`) at 17 sites, but `np.trapezoid` was added in numpy 2.0.0 (verified: the installed numpy's `np.trapezoid.__doc__` carries `.. versionadded:: 2.0.0`; the pre-2.0 name was `np.trapz`). On any 1.26.x install the calls raise `AttributeError: module 'numpy' has no attribute 'trapezoid'`. I verified there is NO shim, alias, version guard, or compatibility import anywhere in the package (searched all of src/odas_tpw/, every __init__.py, and tests/conftest.py). The previously-confirmed finding cited ONLY scor160/l4.py:366, drastically under-scoping the blast radius. ACTUAL affected call sites: chi/chi.py lines 102, 109, 237, 287, 430, 443, 444, 514, 559, 592, 593 (11 sites); chi/l4_chi.py:246; rsi/quick_look.py:145; and scor160/l4.py lines 366, 411, 412, 522 (4 sites, of which 411/412/522 were ALSO not cited). The chi/chi.py sites sit on ALL THREE chi computation paths: `_chi_from_epsilon` (Method 1: 237, 287), `_mle_fit_kB` (Methods 2/3 MLE: 430, 443, 444), `_iterative_fit` (iterative: 514, 559, 592, 593), each ALSO via `_variance_correction` (102, 109) invoked at lines 242/421/569. These are reached by the production chi path (rsi/window.py:310 imports `_chi_from_epsilon` and `_iterative_fit`) and the quick-look viewer (rsi/quick_look.py:63 imports `_mle_fit_kB`; line 145 uses trapezoid directly). Net effect on a conformant numpy-1.26.x install: every chi method for every probe crashes, the chi L4 stage crashes, the quick-look viewer crashes, and the additional shear-L4 epsilon variance integrals at l4.py:411/412/522 crash — far beyond the single line 366 originally reported. Tests pass locally only because the dev env has numpy 2.4.6 (confirmed: has `trapezoid`, lacks `trapz`).
suggested_fix: Either (a) raise the declared floor to `numpy>=2.0,<3` in pyproject.toml:17 so np.trapezoid is guaranteed present, or (b) add a single compatibility shim (e.g. `trapezoid = getattr(np, "trapezoid", np.trapz)` in a shared util imported everywhere, replacing all 17 `np.trapezoid` calls). Option (a) is the minimal, lowest-risk fix and matches the dev environment. Whichever is chosen, add a CI matrix entry pinning the lowest supported numpy so this is caught.
verify_reasoning:
Verified all core claims against the actual code. (1) pyproject.toml:17 declares `numpy>=1.26,<3`, permitting 1.26.x installs. (2) `np.trapezoid` was added in numpy 2.0.0 — confirmed directly from the installed numpy docstring (`versionadded:: 2.0.0`); the pre-2.0 name was `np.trapz`. (3) The dev env has numpy 2.4.3 (has `trapezoid`, lacks `trapz`), so tests pass locally while a conformant 1.26.x install would raise AttributeError. (4) No shim/alias/version-guard/compat module exists anywhere in src/odas_tpw/ — grep for `trapz`, `getattr(...trapz)`, `def trapezoid`, and compat modules all came up empty; every module uses bare `import numpy as np`.

The 17 src call sites are confirmed exactly as cited: chi/chi.py {102,109,237,287,430,443,444,514,559,592,593}, chi/l4_chi.py:246, rsi/quick_look.py:145, scor160/l4.py {366,411,412,522}. Reachability/blast-radius claims are substantiated: _variance_correction (chi.py:77, calls at 102/109) is invoked from all three chi paths at lines 242, 421, 569; _chi_from_epsilon (chi.py:163) and _iterative_fit (chi.py:454) are imported and called in the production path at rsi/window.py:310/374/399; _mle_fit_kB (chi.py:359) is imported by rsi/quick_look.py:63, and quick_look.py:145 uses np.trapezoid directly. So on a numpy 1.26.x install every chi method, the chi L4 stage, the quick-look viewer, and the additional shear-L4 variance integrals at l4.py:411/412/522 all crash — far beyond the single line 366 originally reported.

Caveats that hold severity at major rather than blocker: this is a latent/packaging defect — it only manifests when someone actually installs with numpy 1.26–1.x; the pinned dev env (2.4.3) means CI never exercises it, so no current user is necessarily broken. The finding's category label `resource` is a mild mislabel (it is really a dependency-floor / packaging-correctness bug), but that does not affect whether the defect is real. The declared compatibility contract (claims 1.26 support yet uses a 2.0-only API) is genuinely broken across the entire chi subsystem, so the finding is real and its expanded scope is accurate. Suggested fix (raise floor to numpy>=2.0,<3, or add a `getattr(np,'trapezoid',np.trapz)` shim, plus a CI lowest-numpy matrix entry) is appropriate.

title: N2 omits a valid CF standard_name (square_of_brunt_vaisala_frequency_in_sea_water)
description: The N2 schema entry (lines 273-277, reused by CHI_SCHEMA:339 and CTD_SCHEMA:379) sets units 's-2' and long_name 'buoyancy frequency squared (Thorpe-sorted)' but omits 'standard_name'. The CF Standard Name Table v89 (which GLOBAL_ATTRS at line 386 advertises via standard_name_vocabulary) contains the exact entry id='square_of_brunt_vaisala_frequency_in_sea_water' with canonical_units 's-2' -- an unambiguous, unit-matching standard name for this exact quantity (the table description explicitly notes 'Brunt-Vaisala frequency is also sometimes called buoyancy frequency'). Verified by downloading the official CF v89 XML (version_number 89 confirmed) and grepping: the entry exists with canonical_units s-2. Other measured variables in the same schema (T, P, density, sigma0, epsilon, salinity, etc.) all carry standard_name, so N2 is an inconsistent metadata gap that loses CF discoverability for the stratification field. Real but low-severity omission. IMPORTANT CONTEXT: the task's stated premise -- that the epsilon standard_name 'specific_turbulent_kinetic_energy_dissipation_in_sea_water' (lines 213/219/225) is NOT a real CF name -- is FACTUALLY INCORRECT and was NOT reproduced. That string IS a genuine CF v89 entry (id='specific_turbulent_kinetic_energy_dissipation_in_sea_water', canonical_units 'W kg-1', matching the schema's W/kg). The name the task claimed is canonical, 'tendency_of_specific_turbulent_kinetic_energy_of_sea_water_due_to_dissipation', does NOT exist anywhere in CF v89. The epsilon entries are therefore correct and are explicitly NOT a defect.
suggested_fix: Add "standard_name": "square_of_brunt_vaisala_frequency_in_sea_water" to the N2 schema entry at line 273. Leave dTdz, Gamma, and chi without standard_name -- no applicable CF v89 name exists for any of them (verified: no temperature-gradient, no thermal/temperature-variance-dissipation, and no mixing-efficiency standard name in the table).
verify_reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL. All load-bearing claims confirmed by reading the actual code and the official CF v89 XML.

Code facts (src/odas_tpw/perturb/netcdf_schema.py):
- N2 schema entry lines 273-277 sets units "s-2" and long_name "buoyancy frequency squared (Thorpe-sorted)" and OMITS standard_name. Confirmed by direct read.
- Reused by CHI_SCHEMA line 339 (N2: COMBO_SCHEMA["N2"]) and CTD_SCHEMA line 379. Confirmed.
- The module is explicitly CF-targeted: docstring says "CF-1.13 / ACDD-1.3 compliant NetCDF output"; GLOBAL_ATTRS (line 386) advertises standard_name_vocabulary "CF Standard Name Table v89". Confirmed.
- Peer measured variables carry standard_name (T line 90, P line 84, rho line 195, sigma0 line 189, SP line 170, SA line 176, CT line 182, JAC_C line 164, DO line 113, and the three epsilon entries lines 213/219/225), so N2 is an inconsistent gap. Confirmed.

CF table facts (downloaded official src XML, version_number 89 confirmed, 4.39 MB, first_published 2025-01-21):
- entry id="square_of_brunt_vaisala_frequency_in_sea_water" EXISTS at XML line 15367, canonical_units "s-2", description states Brunt-Vaisala "is also sometimes called buoyancy frequency". This is an unambiguous, unit-matching standard name (schema units "s-2" == canonical "s-2"). Confirmed.
- The premise the parent task apparently disputed is itself wrong, and the candidate flagged this correctly: id="specific_turbulent_kinetic_energy_dissipation_in_sea_water" EXISTS at XML line 15297 with canonical_units "W kg-1" (matches schema W/kg), so the epsilon entries are CORRECT and not a defect. And "tendency_of_specific_turbulent_kinetic_energy_of_sea_water_due_to_dissipation" has 0 matches in v89 (does not exist).
- dTdz: 0 temperature-gradient ids in v89 -> no applicable name, correctly left bare. chi: 0 thermal/temperature-variance-dissipation ids -> correctly bare. Gamma: no mixing-efficiency/coefficient id -> correctly bare.

One refinement to the candidate's suggested fix (not part of the core N2 finding, so it does not change the verdict): the candidate also asserted K_T and K_rho have no applicable CF name, but they were not part of this finding's core claim and v89 actually DOES contain ocean_vertical_heat_diffusivity and ocean_vertical_tracer_diffusivity (canonical m2 s-1, matching their units). Whether those map cleanly to Osborn-Cox/Osborn diffusivities is a judgment call outside this finding's scope and does not affect the N2 verdict.

Severity: minor is defensible because CF discoverability/compliance is an explicit, advertised design goal of this module; it is metadata-only (no data-correctness impact), so it sits at the minor/nit boundary. Keeping minor.

title: Profile/CTD background N2 computed at lat/lon=0/0 while diss/chi window N2 uses the cast's real position
description: `_compute_slow_stratification` (pipeline.py:449) calls `profile_stratification(P_c, T_c, S=S_c, window=window)` at pipeline.py:485 WITHOUT lat/lon, so it defaults to lat=0/lon=0 inside profile_stratification (mixing.py:326-327). The resulting N2/dTdz are injected into pf.channels['N2']/['dTdz'] (pipeline.py:987,1001) and propagate to BOTH the per-profile product AND the CTD product. In contrast, `_window_stratification_for_profile` (pipeline.py:495) reads the profile's real lat/lon (pipeline.py:530-531) and passes them into `sorted_stratification(..., lat=lat, lon=lon)` (pipeline.py:544), which feeds the diss/chi window products and the K_T/Gamma/K_rho mixing coefficients. The same cast's water therefore yields a different background N2/dTdz in the profile/CTD product vs the window products, via gsw.z_from_p (gravity, lat-dependent) and gsw.SA_from_SP (absolute-salinity anomaly, lon/lat-dependent) and gsw.Nsquared (gravity). I verified the magnitude with gsw for a representative ARCTERX/Saipan upper-thermocline window (~15N/145E vs 0/0): N2 differs by ~+0.07% (dominated by gravity; the SA-anomaly term is only ~0.003%) and dTdz by ~+0.035% (via the z_from_p span). The docstring at pipeline.py:457-458 hand-waves '<0.3% gravity and negligible salinity-anomaly effect' but does not note that this also makes the profile/CTD product INTERNALLY INCONSISTENT with the window products for the same cast. gps is in scope at the pipeline.py:978 call site (it is a process_file parameter, pipeline.py:801, and exposes .lat()/.lon() as used at profile.py:189-190), so the real position could be passed. Confidence: the inconsistency is real and substantiated; the numerical magnitude is small (sub-0.1%, below gradient-estimation noise).
suggested_fix: Pass the cast's real lat/lon into _compute_slow_stratification (evaluate gps.lat()/gps.lon() at each cast's midpoint time, matching how profile.py:188-190 derives the per-profile lat/lon) and forward them to profile_stratification(..., lat=lat, lon=lon), so the background and window products use a consistent TEOS-10 position. At minimum, update the pipeline.py:457-458 docstring to state the resulting profile-vs-window inconsistency explicitly.
verify_reasoning:
Code-level claims all verified. `_compute_slow_stratification` (pipeline.py:485) calls `profile_stratification(P_c, T_c, S=S_c, window=window)` with no lat/lon, so it uses the mixing.py:326-327 defaults lat=0.0/lon=0.0. Those defaults feed three lat/lon-dependent TEOS-10 calls: `gsw.SA_from_SP(S_vals, P_arr, lon, lat)` (mixing.py:389), `gsw.z_from_p(P_arr, lat)` (mixing.py:392), and `gsw.Nsquared(..., lat)` (mixing.py:318 inside `_stable_window`). The result is injected into pf.channels["N2"]/["dTdz"] (pipeline.py:987,1001) before the CTD/profile forks (comment at 972-974), so it reaches both per-profile and CTD products. The window path `_window_stratification_for_profile` instead reads the profile's real lat/lon (pipeline.py:530-531) and forwards them to `sorted_stratification(..., lat=lat, lon=lon)` (pipeline.py:544). So the same cast yields background N2/dTdz computed at 0/0 (profile/CTD) vs real position (diss/chi windows) — a genuine internal inconsistency. `gps` is in scope (process_file param, pipeline.py:801) with .lat()/.lon() available (used at profile.py:189-190), so the fix is feasible.

I independently reproduced the magnitude with gsw for a representative ~15N/145E Saipan upper-thermocline window vs 0/0: N2 differs by ~0.061% and dTdz by ~0.036%, matching the finding's ~0.07% / ~0.035% claim. This is sub-0.1%, far below the inherent uncertainty of Thorpe-sorted coarse-window gradient estimates, so there is no practical scientific consequence.

Downgrading from minor to nit: the 0/0 default and its negligible-impact rationale are already explicitly documented in the docstring (pipeline.py:457-458) as an intentional design choice, nothing produced is meaningfully wrong, and the effect is below gradient-estimation noise. It is a real-but-trivial consistency/documentation refinement, not a correctness defect.

title: Docstring claims diss and chi products report 'identical' window-scale stratification, but they use different window widths by default
description: The `_window_stratification_for_profile` docstring (pipeline.py:503-504) states it is 'Shared by the diss and chi products so they report identical, window-scale stratification.' While the same function is shared, it is invoked with DIFFERENT half-window widths for the two products: the diss path passes `diss_length_seconds / 2` (pipeline.py:1187) and the chi path (via _add_mixing_quantities, pipeline.py:634) uses `0.5 * chi_ds.attrs['diss_length'] / fs_fast`. With the defaults these differ by a factor of 2 (epsilon diss_length = 4*fft_length with fft_length=256 -> 1024 samples = 2.0 s window; chi diss_length = 4*512 = 2048 samples = 4.0 s window; see pipeline.py:1127-1132). Because the window half-widths differ, each window selects a different sample set and different shallow/deep half-means, so the diss-window N2/dTdz and chi-window N2/dTdz are NOT identical whenever epsilon and chi are configured with different diss_length/fft_length (the default case). The word 'identical' in the docstring is inaccurate; they are each correct at their own respective window scale. This is a documentation defect, not a computational one.
suggested_fix: Reword pipeline.py:503-504 to say the function reports stratification at each product's own window scale (so diss and chi values coincide only when their diss_length/fft_length match), rather than claiming they are 'identical'.
verify_reasoning:
Verified against the actual code. The docstring at src/odas_tpw/perturb/pipeline.py:503-504 states _window_stratification_for_profile is "Shared by the diss and chi products so they report identical, window-scale stratification." But the two products invoke it with different half-window widths:

- Diss path (line 1187): _attach_window_stratification(..., diss_length_seconds / 2, ...). _attach_window_stratification (line 561) passes half_w straight through to _window_stratification_for_profile. diss_length_samples defaults to 4*fft_length with fft_length=256 -> 1024 samples (line 1127); diss_length_seconds = 1024/fs_fast (line 1128); so diss half_w = 512/fs_fast s.
- Chi path (lines 634, 642): half_w = 0.5 * chi_ds.attrs["diss_length"] / chi_ds.attrs["fs_fast"]. The chi dataset's diss_length attr is set in chi_io.py:627 from _compute_chi's diss_length param, which in the pipeline comes from chi_kwargs/chi_cfg (line 1208-1215, 1259-1264). Under defaults this differs from the diss diss_length.

Because half_w differs, each window selects a different sample set and different Thorpe-sorted shallow/deep half-means, so the diss-window and chi-window N2/dTdz are NOT identical under default config. The word "identical" is inaccurate; the values coincide only when epsilon and chi share the same diss_length. Confirmed via sorted_stratification call at line 544 using half_w.

One caveat on the finding's reasoning (does not change the verdict): the finding attributes the chi 4.0s window to the pipeline variable chi_diss_length_samples (lines 1129-1132, fft_length default 512). That variable is used for chi QC, not for the chi stratification half-window. The chi stratification half-window actually reads chi_ds.attrs["diss_length"] (line 634), which is set by _compute_chi's parameters (default fft_length=1024 -> diss_length=4096 when chi_cfg specifies nothing). So the real default discrepancy can be even larger than the finding's 2x estimate, but the central claim — different half-widths -> not "identical" — is correct.

This is a documentation-only defect (no computational error), correctly rated minor.

title: Failed Batchelor fit (NaN kB) still yields finite chi that survives all chi QC
description: In the iterative fit (Method 2), if _mle_find_kB returns a non-finite kB or kB<1 on the FIRST iteration, the loop breaks at lines 538-539 with kB_best=np.nan. Execution falls to lines 580-582: epsilon stays NaN, fom is forced NaN (line 601), K_max_ratio is NaN (line 603), but chi=chi_obs is still the finite band-integrated value (the initial estimate from lines 511-518, possibly noise-dominated). _process_l4_chi stores this finite chi in chi_out[ci,j] (l4_chi.py:113) regardless of the NaN kB/fom. This finite chi then (a) enters _compute_chi_final's geometric mean (l4_chi.py:276 only checks finite & >0, ignoring kB/fom/K_max_ratio), and (b) escapes the perturb FOM cut because _apply_fom_cut masks only cells where np.isfinite(fom) & fom>=fom_max (perturb/pipeline.py:396) and here fom is NaN. There is no K_max_ratio cut for chi anywhere (only fom_max is configurable; config.py:404/434). Net: a spectrum where the Batchelor wavenumber fit failed entirely still contributes a (potentially spurious, noise-driven) chi to chi_final/chiMean and thus to K_T/Gamma, with no QC gate able to remove it. Epsilon does not have this gap because a failed shear fit yields NaN epsilon, not a finite fallback. Low-confidence on real-world frequency, high-confidence on the code path existing.
suggested_fix: When the Batchelor/kB fit fails (kB non-finite), return chi=NaN (not chi_obs) from _iterative_fit, or gate _compute_chi_final/mk_chi_mean on finite kB (or finite fom) in addition to chi>0, so failed-fit windows are excluded from the combined chi rather than contributing a fom=NaN value that no cut can reach.
verify_reasoning: Verified against actual code. In src/odas_tpw/chi/chi.py _iterative_fit, a first-iteration kB failure breaks the loop (lines 538-539) with kB_best=NaN. The return (line 605) then carries epsilon=NaN, fom=NaN (forced at line 601), K_max_ratio=NaN (line 603), but chi=chi_obs (line 582) — the finite band-integrated initial estimate from lines 511-518 (6*KAPPA_T*trapezoid(max(spec_obs-noise_K,0),K)), floored only when <=0. This finite chi is stored unconditionally in chi_out[ci,j] (chi/l4_chi.py:113), enters _compute_chi_final's geometric mean which only checks finite & >0 (l4_chi.py:276), and survives the perturb FOM cut because _apply_fom_cut masks only cells where np.isfinite(fom) & fom>=fom_max (perturb/pipeline.py:396) — a NaN fom is never masked. mk_chi_mean (processing/chi_combine.py) only floors chi<=chi_minimum (1e-13) and otherwise includes all finite chi in chiMean with no kB/fom gate. grep confirms there is no K_max_ratio cut for chi anywhere in perturb/ or processing/. fit_method='iterative' is the default (config.py:53,116), so this is the production Method-2 path. The candidate's file path was rsi/l4_chi.py but the file is actually at chi/l4_chi.py (cited line numbers 113/276 match exactly). Additionally, the default chi config has no fom_max key, so _apply_fom_cut is skipped entirely by default (pipeline.py:1267), exposing the gap even more than described. Epsilon lacks this gap because a failed shear fit yields NaN epsilon, not a finite fallback. The defect is genuine: a window with a wholly failed Batchelor/Kraichnan kB fit emits a finite, potentially noise-driven chi that no QC gate (fom, K_max_ratio, chi_minimum) can remove, propagating into chiMean/chi_final and thence K_T/Gamma. Major is appropriate (silent QC bypass producing meaningless values in a derived geophysical product); real-world frequency depends on how often outright fit failure coincides with above-noise spectra, hence not blocker.

title: rsi mixing uses unfiltered chi_final while perturb uses CI-filtered chiMean (estimator divergence)
description: The same physical chi feeds Gamma=N2*chi/(2*eps*dTdz^2) via two structurally different estimators depending on which pipeline runs. rsi/pipeline.py:449 uses l4_chi_eps.chi_final, computed by _compute_chi_final as the geometric mean of ALL probes with chi>0 — no outlier rejection at all. The perturb pipeline instead writes chiMean from mk_chi_mean, which (when epsilon_T is present, have_eps_T True) runs the SAME drop-the-max-probe 95% CI loop as epsilon (chi_combine.py:147-163), and _add_mixing_quantities consumes chiMean (perturb/pipeline.py:680), not chi_final. So for identical input: rsi's chi_final keeps a high outlier probe (no rejection) while perturb's chiMean may drop it, and conversely rsi's chi_final is NOT subject to the systematic drop-max low bias that epsilon and perturb-chi share. Two co-located dissipation rates (eps via drop-max, chi_final via plain gmean) are combined into one Gamma by philosophically different estimators. Note chi_final is still written to the per-profile chi NetCDF (pipeline.py:715-722) even in perturb runs, where it is stale relative to chiMean (the FOM cut and CI filter never update chi_final), so any downstream consumer that reads chi_final instead of chiMean gets the unfiltered value. This is a design/consistency hazard rather than a crash.
suggested_fix: Pick one combination policy for chi and route both pipelines through it. Either drop chi_final from the rsi mixing path in favor of an mk_chi_mean-equivalent, or stop writing chi_final to NetCDF in the perturb path (or recompute it after the FOM/CI filtering) so a stale unfiltered estimate cannot be picked up downstream.
verify_reasoning:
The central observation is substantiated by the code. The same physical chi feeds the identical Gamma formula (mixing_coefficients) through two structurally different estimators depending on which pipeline runs:

- rsi run_pipeline (rsi/pipeline.py:449) passes l4_chi_eps.chi_final to mixing_coefficients. chi_final comes from _compute_chi_final (chi/l4_chi.py:271-279), which is a PLAIN geometric mean of all probes with chi>0 — verified: no outlier rejection, no FOM cut, no CI filter applied anywhere before line 449.
- perturb pipeline (perturb/pipeline.py:680, via _add_mixing_quantities) passes chi_ds["chiMean"] from mk_chi_mean (processing/chi_combine.py). When have_eps_T is True, mk_chi_mean runs the drop-the-max-probe 95% CI removal loop (chi_combine.py:147-163), exactly mirroring the epsilon path; and the perturb path additionally applies _apply_fom_cut before mk_chi_mean. So for identical input the two pipelines can produce different combined chi (and thus different Gamma/K_T). That estimator divergence is real.

However, one of the finding's two pillars is FALSE. The claim that "chi_final is still written to the per-profile chi NetCDF (pipeline.py:715-722) even in perturb runs, where it is stale relative to chiMean ... any downstream consumer that reads chi_final gets the unfiltered value" does not hold. The perturb chi dataset is built by _compute_chi -> _build_chi_ds_from_pipeline -> _build_chi_dataset (rsi/chi_io.py:440-637), whose variable list contains NO chi_final variable at all (it has chi, epsilon_T, kB, K_max_T, fom, K_max_ratio, spectra, plus chiMean/chiLnSigma added later). The pipeline.py:715 chi_final write is in rsi/pipeline.py's separate single-file run_pipeline path, which perturb never invokes. So there is no stale chi_final in any perturb NetCDF and no downstream consumer can pick one up. The cited "pipeline.py:715-722 even in perturb runs" is a misattribution of an rsi-only code path to the perturb pipeline.

Severity downgraded to nit: within any single pipeline run the chi estimator is self-consistent; the divergence only matters when a user compares or mixes outputs across the two pipelines. It is a cross-pipeline design/consistency observation, not a within-run correctness bug or crash, and the more alarming "stale unfiltered chi_final leaks into output" hazard is refuted. The estimator-divergence kernel is genuine and worth a consistency note, so is_real=true, but the finding as written overstates the impact.

title: mixing_look._mixing_chi is a third, QC-free chi combination strategy diverging from chi_final and chiMean
description: _mixing_chi computes per-thermistor chi as the arithmetic mean of the Batchelor and Kraichnan estimates ((chi_b+chi_k)/2, line 167) then the geometric mean across thermistors (line 174), with only a chi>0 filter and no FOM/CI rejection. Its docstring claims it matches chi_final, but chi_final (_compute_chi_final) operates on a single spectrum_model's chi array, never averages Batchelor and Kraichnan, and the production chi pipeline runs one model (spectrum_model), not both blended 50/50. Meanwhile _mixing_epsilon right above it (lines 131-154) DOES apply the Lueck FM<=1.15 reject before geometric-meaning. So within this same diagnostic, epsilon is QC-gated and chi is not, and the chi blending rule differs from both chi_final and chiMean. This is a plotting/diagnostic class so impact is limited to the look tool, but it means the mixing scatter shown to the user uses a chi estimator that exists nowhere in the production output, and the docstring 'matching chi_final' is inaccurate.
suggested_fix: Either make _mixing_chi delegate to the same _compute_chi_final / mk_chi_mean logic used by the pipeline (single model, same floor/QC), or fix the docstring to state it is a Batchelor/Kraichnan-blended diagnostic estimator distinct from chi_final, and consider applying the chi FOM cut for parity with _mixing_epsilon.
verify_reasoning: Verified against actual code. (1) `_mixing_chi` (src/odas_tpw/rsi/mixing_look.py:156-175) blends Batchelor and Kraichnan per-thermistor as `(chi_b+chi_k)/2` (line 167), then geometric-means across thermistors with only a `chi>0` filter (lines 172-174), no FOM cut. (2) Production `chi_final` = `_compute_chi_final` (src/odas_tpw/chi/l4_chi.py:271-279) operates on a single-model `chi_out` array (built with one `spectrum_model`, l4_chi.py:108; default kraichnan, config.py:54; emitted by pipeline.py:715-717) and never blends the two spectra. So the (b+k)/2 per-probe estimator does not exist in any production output, and the docstring's "matching chi_final" (mixing_look.py:157-158) is inaccurate. (3) The sibling `_mixing_epsilon` (lines 131-154) applies the Lueck FM<=1.15 reject (line 150) before its geometric mean, so QC handling is asymmetric within the same diagnostic. The viewer's `compute_windowed_diss` deliberately computes both models (viewer_base.py:221, 301-320) and even exposes `chi_fom_batchelor/kraichnan` (lines 331-332) that `_mixing_chi` ignores. One minor over-statement in the finding: it implies `chi_final` is QC-gated while `_mixing_chi` is not, but `_compute_chi_final` itself applies no FOM cut either (just `chi>0` geometric mean, l4_chi.py:276) — so the cross-probe combine actually matches production; the sole real divergence is the per-probe (b+k)/2 blend plus the epsilon/chi asymmetry. This does not change the verdict. Impact is limited to the diagnostic look tool (no product/NetCDF output is affected), so minor is the correct severity. The docstring is genuinely misleading and the blended estimator genuinely differs from production.

title: LnSigma quadrature combine is silently wrong under median aggregation
description: For *LnSigma variables (epsilonLnSigma, chiLnSigma -- both stored as sigma-of-log standard deviations, units "1"; see processing/epsilon_combine.py:147 and processing/chi_combine.py:180), _bin_snapshot computes sqrt(_bin_array(sigma**2, ..., agg)). The square-then-root wrapper averages variances in quadrature ONLY when agg is np.nanmean: sqrt(mean(sigma^2)) = RMS(sigma), which is what the comment at lines 305-306 ('Standard deviations combine in quadrature: average the variances (RMS of sigma)') claims. But _bin_array receives the SAME agg_func chosen for the whole dataset, and that is np.nanmedian whenever aggregation/diss_aggregation/chi_aggregation == 'median' (_get_agg_func at binning.py:155-159; config.py:489-493 documents 'mean | median'; reachable via cli.py:233/272/293 and pipeline.py:1865/1894/1907). Under median, the result is sqrt(median(sigma^2)), which is NOT the documented quadrature RMS. For odd in-bin counts it collapses exactly to median(|sigma|) (verified: sqrt(median([0.3,0.5,0.9]^2)) == median == 0.5), i.e. the square/root pair is a no-op and the value is just the median sigma. For even counts it is sqrt of the mean of the two middle squared sigmas -- neither the median sigma nor the RMS. Numerically verified divergence from the intended quadrature: sigmas [0.2,0.4,3.0] give intended RMS=1.751 but median-path=0.40, a ~4x understatement of the combined log-uncertainty. The mislabeled output silently understates (or in even-count cases distorts) the combined epsilon/chi uncertainty whenever a user selects median binning, with no warning. The dispatch is correct only for the default 'mean'.
suggested_fix: Decouple the LnSigma combine from the dataset's value-aggregation choice: always average the variances with the arithmetic mean (e.g. call _bin_array(sigma**2, ..., np.nanmean) then sqrt, regardless of `agg`), or for median datasets combine sigmas with a statistic that is actually meaningful as a robust quadrature (e.g. median of variances is acceptable only if the comment/long_name is corrected to say 'median variance, not RMS'). Simplest correct fix: pass np.nanmean explicitly for the LnSigma branches at both binning.py:307 and binning.py:563-565 so the quadrature identity always holds; update the comment if a robust alternative is intentionally desired.
verify_reasoning:
Verified in actual code. binning.py:307 and binning.py:563-565 both compute sqrt(_bin_array(sigma**2, ..., agg)) for *LnSigma variables, where `agg` is the dataset-wide aggregation function returned by _get_agg_func (binning.py:155-159). _bin_array applies agg_func to the squared sigmas (binning.py:98-120): the np.nanmean fast path yields sqrt(mean(sigma^2)) = RMS (correct, matches the comment at 305-306), but the general fallback (line 119) applies np.nanmedian to sigma**2, producing sqrt(median(sigma^2)) -- NOT the documented quadrature RMS.

Target variables confirmed: epsilonLnSigma ("sigma of ln(epsilon)", units "1", epsilon_combine.py:147-152) and chiLnSigma ("sigma of ln(chi)", units "1", chi_combine.py:173-180). These live in the diss/chi NetCDFs that bin_diss/bin_chi process.

Reachability confirmed: config.py:489-493 documents aggregation: 'mean | median' plus diss_aggregation/chi_aggregation overrides; pipeline.py:1865/1894/1906-1909 resolve diss_agg/chi_agg and pass them into bin_diss/bin_chi (pipeline.py:1947+), which thread them through _get_agg_func into np.nanmedian.

Numerics reproduced exactly: odd-count [0.3,0.5,0.9] -> median-path 0.5 == median(|sigma|) (square/root is a no-op); [0.2,0.4,3.0] -> intended RMS 1.751 vs median-path 0.40 (~4.4x understatement); even-count [0.2,0.4,0.6,3.0] -> 0.510, neither median(|s|)=0.5 nor RMS=1.546. Mean path correctly returns 1.751.

Could not refute: the comment/long_name claim RMS, but the identity holds only for mean. Under median binning (user opt-in via aggregation/diss_aggregation/chi_aggregation = 'median') the combined epsilon/chi log-uncertainty is silently understated or distorted with no warning. Major (not blocker) because it only triggers for non-default median aggregation; the default 'mean' path is correct.

title: Per-window salinity averaged with np.mean (not nanmean) poisons chi viscosity from NaN surface salinity
description: The chi pipeline derives per-sample practical salinity in adapter.py:87-90 via gsw.SP_from_C(JAC_C, JAC_T, P) and interpolates it onto the fast time base with np.interp (adapter.py:90). gsw.SP_from_C emits NaN for out-of-range conductivity, which is the normal situation at a VMP profile's start/end: when the instrument is in air or the conductivity cell is not yet flushed, raw conductivity is ~0 or slightly negative. I verified empirically that gsw.SP_from_C([0, 1e-6, -0.5, ...]) returns NaN for the 1e-6 and -0.5 entries (0.0 maps to 0.0, but any tiny-positive or negative C gives NaN). np.interp then constant-extends/propagates that NaN: a NaN at the first (surface) slow conductivity node spreads to every fast sample up to the next finite node (verified: 4 leading fast samples NaN in a constructed case). _resolve_salinity (rsi/pipeline.py:69-70) passes the whole array through as long as ANY element is finite (`np.isfinite(l1.salinity).any()`), so a partly-NaN salinity array reaches process_l3_chi unmodified. There, line 154 computes `sal_means = np.mean(np.asarray(salinity)[indices], axis=1)` using np.mean (NOT np.nanmean), so ANY single NaN sample inside a chi FFT window makes that window's sal_means[i] NaN. That NaN flows into visc(T_means[i], sal_means[i], P_means[i]) (line 158); I verified visc(12, NaN, 50) returns NaN (it does not hit the visc35 fast-path because P!=0 and S!=35, so it goes through the gsw branch and yields NaN). The NaN viscosity then produces NaN/biased Batchelor wavenumber kB and a corrupted chi estimate for every window overlapping the NaN salinity region. This is asymmetric with the epsilon path, which explicitly sanitizes non-finite per-window TEMPERATURE before viscosity in scor160/l4.py:154-160 (replacing non-finite temp with 10 degC and warning); the chi salinity path has no equivalent guard. The same sink is shared by the perturb 'chi.salinity: measured' path (perturb/pipeline.py:795 _profile_practical_salinity returns gsw.SP_from_C(C,T,P) with no NaN handling -> process_l3_chi) and the chi_io.py:151 path, so all three measured-salinity producers are affected. Net effect: silent loss/biasing of chi (and derived K_T) in the near-surface windows of VMP profiles that begin in air or before the CT cell is flushed, exactly where many mixing studies want data.
suggested_fix: Use np.nanmean instead of np.mean for the per-window salinity reduction at l3_chi.py:154 (windows where every salinity sample is NaN will still need a fallback). More robustly, sanitize the salinity array where it is produced/resolved: in adapter.py after line 90, or in _resolve_salinity, replace non-finite salinity with a sensible default (e.g. nanmean of the finite samples, or 35 PSU) and warn, mirroring the temperature handling in scor160/l4.py:154-160. Optionally clamp conductivity to a physical floor before SP_from_C so air/unflushed samples map to a finite low salinity rather than NaN.
verify_reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL. Every link in the chain was confirmed by reading the actual code and by empirical runs.

CODE PATH (read, not assumed):
- src/odas_tpw/rsi/adapter.py:87-90 produces per-sample practical salinity via gsw.SP_from_C(JAC_C, JAC_T, P) then np.interp onto the fast time base. grep confirms NO isfinite/nanmean/nan_to_num sanitization in adapter.py (only production at L90, assignment at L195).
- src/odas_tpw/rsi/pipeline.py:69-70 (_resolve_salinity) returns l1.salinity unchanged whenever np.isfinite(l1.salinity).any() is True, so a partly-NaN array passes through verbatim.
- src/odas_tpw/chi/l3_chi.py:215 process_l3_chi forwards salinity to _process_section_chi, where line 154 computes sal_means = np.mean(np.asarray(salinity)[indices], axis=1) — np.mean, NOT np.nanmean. There is no NaN guard between line 154 and the visc() call at line 158 (read lines 145-165).
- The two other measured-salinity producers are confirmed un-sanitized: perturb/pipeline.py:795 _profile_practical_salinity returns gsw.SP_from_C(C,T,P) raw; rsi/chi_io.py:147-152 feeds sal_prof from prepare_profiles into process_l3_chi. helpers.py:323-336 (prepare_profiles) does np.interp(t_fast, t_slow, salinity) with no NaN handling.

EMPIRICAL CONFIRMATION (python3 + this repo's gsw/ocean):
- gsw.SP_from_C([0, 1e-6, -0.5, ...]) returns [~0, nan, nan, ...] — tiny-positive and negative conductivity (air / unflushed cell) yield NaN.
- np.interp with a leading NaN slow node propagates NaN to every fast sample before the first finite node (4 leading NaN in the constructed case).
- np.mean over a window with one NaN -> NaN (np.nanmean -> 30.0).
- visc(12, NaN, 50) -> NaN. Confirmed by reading ocean.py:60-79: the visc35 fast-path requires np.all(S==35) and np.all(P==0); a NaN S fails S==35, so it goes through the gsw/Sharqawy branch and yields NaN. The NaN viscosity then corrupts the Batchelor wavenumber kB and the chi estimate for every window overlapping the NaN salinity region.

ASYMMETRY CONFIRMED: the epsilon path (scor160/l4.py:154-160) explicitly replaces non-finite per-window temperature with 10 degC and warns; the chi salinity path has no equivalent guard.

SEVERITY: Major (not blocker) — it silently biases/NaNs chi (and derived K_T) in near-surface or unflushed-cell windows rather than crashing, and the corruption is localized to windows that actually contain a non-finite salinity sample (any single NaN sample in a window is sufficient; an all-NaN window would also defeat a naive np.nanmean swap and still needs a fallback). The defect is unambiguous at the code level and affects all three measured-salinity producers. I could not refute it: no upstream clamp, no profile-slice guarantee that excludes unflushed-cell or interior-dropout samples, and the visc fast-path cannot rescue a NaN-salinity window.

title: Goodman singular-AA fallback mixes cleaned and uncleaned bins, then bias-scales the uncleaned ones
description: When the batched np.linalg.solve(AA, ua_H) at line 130 raises LinAlgError, it does so for the ENTIRE batch the moment ANY single frequency's accelerometer auto/cross-spectral matrix AA[f] is exactly singular (verified: batched solve raises if any sub-matrix is singular). The except block at lines 132-137 then re-solves every frequency one at a time, and for any frequency where AA[f] is still exactly singular the contextlib.suppress(LinAlgError) silently leaves clean_UU[f] = UU[f] (the UNCLEANED auto-spectrum, from the np.copy(UU) at line 134). The result is a single spectrum that mixes Goodman-cleaned bins with raw uncleaned bins. Worse, line 142 then multiplies the WHOLE array (including the uncleaned bins) by the Goodman bias-correction factor R = 1/(1 - 1.02*n_accel/fft_segments) (e.g. ~1.78-2.40 for typical configs), which is only physically valid for cleaned spectra; the uncleaned bins are inflated by ~2x. This diverges from ODAS clean_shear_spec.m (line 140 uses mrdivide unconditionally and has no per-frequency skip). Impact is limited because (a) it requires AA[f] to be EXACTLY singular (ill-conditioned-but-nonsingular does NOT trigger it -- np.linalg.solve returns without error), and (b) the affected bins feed only the per-bin spectral integral/QC, not a wholesale failure. Confirmed by direct test that batched solve raises on a singular sub-matrix and that a suppressed frequency retains UU[f].
suggested_fix: In the fallback, track which frequencies were successfully cleaned vs. suppressed (e.g. set suppressed bins to NaN instead of leaving the raw UU, or record a mask) so downstream code (l4._estimate_epsilon's NaN handling at l4.py:347) can treat them as bad bins rather than valid cleaned spectra. At minimum, do not apply the bias-correction factor to bins that were not actually cleaned. Consider using a pinv/lstsq with rcond for singular AA[f] instead of skipping.
verify_reasoning:
Verified against the actual code (src/odas_tpw/scor160/goodman.py:128-144) and confirmed every mechanical claim by direct test.

(1) Batched np.linalg.solve(AA, ua_H) at line 130 raises LinAlgError for the WHOLE batch if any single sub-matrix is singular — confirmed by test (a 3-element batch with one singular 2x2 raises "Singular matrix").

(2) The except block (lines 132-137) re-solves per-frequency under contextlib.suppress(np.linalg.LinAlgError); for a singular AA[f] it silently leaves clean_UU[f] = UU[f], the UNCLEANED auto-spectrum from np.copy(UU) at line 134 — confirmed by test (suppressed bin retains UU[f], cleaned bin differs).

(3) Line 142 then multiplies the WHOLE array (including suppressed/uncleaned bins) by the Goodman bias factor R = 1/(1 - 1.02*n_accel/fft_segments). End-to-end test with a dead (all-zero) accelerometer channel reproduced the bug: clean_UU == UU * R with all bins finite. The same defect exists in the batched variant (clean_shear_spec_batch, lines 188-204).

(4) The "downstream catches it" refutation FAILS: suppressed bins are finite, real, positive values (not NaN), so _estimate_epsilon's ~np.isfinite bad-bin detection (l4.py) does NOT flag them; they pass through as valid cleaned spectra. This makes the suggested fix (set suppressed bins to NaN / mask) correct, and confirms the bug silently masquerades as good data — no NaN, no error, no warning.

(5) ODAS divergence confirmed: clean_shear_spec.m:140 uses unconditional mrdivide (/AA) with no per-frequency skip, and :156 applies R to the whole array (correct there because all bins are always cleaned).

(6) Reachability/realism: the non-batched clean_shear_spec is used by rsi/window.py:145 in the default RSI epsilon path (do_goodman defaults True, window.py:65; no upstream guard rejecting a degenerate accel channel). The realistic trigger is a dead/disconnected accelerometer (all-zero, or all-constant which detrends to exactly zero) making AA exactly singular at all bins; with n_accel==1, AA is 1x1 [[Saa(f)]], singular iff Saa(f)==0 exactly, which a zero channel produces (verified |X|^2==0 for a zero detrended segment).

Caveat (does not change the verdict): the finding's cited bias-factor range ~1.78-2.40 is config-dependent; for nfft=256/N=4096 it is ~1.034. The magnitude varies but the mechanism (bias-scaling uncleaned bins) holds for any config. The trigger requires EXACTLY singular AA, not merely ill-conditioned — so it is a degenerate-input edge case, consistent with the 'minor' label. I concur with minor: a quiet correctness defect (uncleaned, mis-biased spectrum feeding epsilon with no NaN/warning) gated behind a rare degenerate input.

title: No rcond regularization or negativity handling in Goodman cleaning; near-singular AA yields negative clean_UU passed unguarded to FOM and initial-epsilon integral
description: clean_shear_spec / clean_shear_spec_batch use np.linalg.solve with no rcond/regularization. When an accelerometer spectral matrix AA is ill-conditioned but not exactly singular (e.g. a quiet/saturated or redundant near-collinear accelerometer channel -- cond(AA) ~1e12-1e18 in tests), solve returns without raising, so the LinAlgError fallback never engages. The Schur-complement form UU - UA inv(AA) UA^H is self-stabilizing in the upward direction (verified: clean_UU never exceeds UU; max (clean-UU)/UU overshoot = 0 across 200 adversarial trials), so the prompt's 'explode' concern is NOT borne out. However in the low-segment regime (noisy, inconsistent UA vs AA estimates) clean_UU diagonal bins go NEGATIVE (12768 negative bins / 200 trials at n_seg~3; still occurs at individual bins even at the default diss_length=4*fft_length giving n_seg=7). These negatives flow unguarded into l4._estimate_epsilon: the initial integral e_10 (l4.py:366) and the FOM obs_var/nas_var (l4.py:411-413) trapezoid-integrate spec_safe including negative bins. Only l4._variance_method's polyfit excludes non-positive bins (l4.py:495) and the integrals are floored via max(e_3, EPSILON_FLOOR) (l4.py:523) and e_10<=0 -> EPSILON_FLOOR (l4.py:367). Note: ODAS clean_shear_spec.m also does not clamp negatives, so this is partly inherent to the algorithm rather than a port defect; severity is reduced accordingly. Low-confidence as a 'bug' -- it is more a robustness gap: negative cleaned-spectrum bins can bias the FOM/MAD QC metrics (a negative bin makes spec_ratio<0, silently dropped at l4.py:409, shrinking the MAD sample) without any diagnostic.
suggested_fix: Either (a) clamp/flag negative clean_UU diagonal bins after cleaning (e.g. set to NaN so the existing l4.py:347 bad-bin handling treats them as corrupted, or set to 0 to match the polyfit's zero-bin exclusion), or (b) document explicitly that negative bins are expected and ensure all downstream consumers (initial integral, FOM) handle them consistently rather than only the variance-method polyfit. Consider lstsq/pinv with an rcond threshold to bound the inv(AA) amplification for near-singular AA.
verify_reasoning:
Verified by reading the actual code and empirically reproducing the defect.

CONFIRMED CLAIMS:
1. goodman.py:130-131 and 188-190 use np.linalg.solve with no rcond/regularization; the except np.linalg.LinAlgError fallback (132-137, 191-199) only triggers on exact singularity, not ill-conditioning, so a near-singular AA returns silently.
2. Negative clean_UU diagonal bins are REAL and pervasive in the low-segment regime. I reproduced this: with nfft=256, diss_length=2*nfft (n_seg=3), every one of 300 adversarial trials produced negatives (~28k-38k negative bins/300 trials across coherence/collinearity variants). At n_seg=7 (default diss_length=4*fft_length) my synthetic test showed 0 negatives — so I could NOT reproduce the finding's specific claim that negatives "still occur at individual bins even at n_seg=7", though that is input-dependent. The core premise (negatives occur and reach downstream) is solid.
3. The low-segment regime is REACHABLE in real config: io.py:608 floors inferred diss_length at 2*fft_length, giving n_seg=3 exactly.
4. Downstream handling matches the finding precisely: l4.py:347 catches only ~np.isfinite (NOT negatives); e_10 initial integral (366) is floored at 367 (if e_10<=0 -> EPSILON_FLOOR); FOM obs_var=np.trapezoid(spec_safe_pos,...) (411) includes negative bins unguarded; spec_ratio=spec_ratio[spec_ratio>0] (409) silently drops negatives, shrinking the MAD/FM sample N_s; variance-method polyfit excludes spec_safe>0 (495); _inertial_subrange (621-622,638-640,661-662) also drops/NaNs non-positive ratio bins. So only some consumers handle negatives, inconsistently — exactly the described robustness gap.
5. The QC-flag path: l4.py:216 fom_for_flags = FM if num_ffts>0 else fom, so in the normal benchmark case bit-1 is driven by FM (428-434), whose mad_ln uses spec_ratio after negatives were dropped (409) — confirming negatives bias the MAD/FM QC metric by shrinking N_s, without diagnostic.
6. ODAS clean_shear_spec.m:140,145 performs the identical Schur subtraction (UA/AA)*conj(UA).' then real() with NO negativity clamp — so this is partly inherent to the algorithm, correctly noted in the finding (severity reduced).
7. Test-gap confirmed: tests assert only np.all(np.isfinite(clean_UU)) (test_epsilon.py:353,366,381) and variance reduction at a vib bin (402); goodman tests all use n>=4096/nfft=256 (n_seg>=31, high-segment regime). No test exercises the low-segment regime or asserts non-negativity, so negative bins pass isfinite silently.

VERDICT: Real robustness gap, not a false positive. The code genuinely lacks negativity handling; negative bins genuinely occur in reachable configs and flow unguarded into the FOM integral and shrink the MAD/FM QC sample without any diagnostic. Severity minor is appropriate: it matches ODAS reference behavior, the initial integral is floored, the dominant epsilon paths (ISR fit, variance polyfit) already exclude non-positive bins, and the impact is a subtle QC-metric bias rather than a crash or gross epsilon error. The finding's own author correctly self-refuted the 'explode' concern. Confidence high on the existence and reachability of the defect; the only unconfirmed sub-claim is the n_seg=7 occurrence.

title: Single NaN in P flags the entire record as flight-inconsistent (filtfilt NaN smearing)
description: In _evaluate_pitch_w_consistency, W = smooth_fall_rate(P, fs_slow) runs P through scipy.signal.filtfilt (scor160/profile.py:42). filtfilt has no NaN handling, so a single non-finite sample anywhere in P propagates to EVERY element of W. Line 270 then does `flag |= ~np.isfinite(pitch) | ~np.isfinite(W)`, so one bad pressure sample marks the ENTIRE slow record flight-inconsistent and that rule's bit is set on all samples. I verified this empirically: clean P -> 0 non-finite in W (so the prompt's 'filtfilt edge NaN' hypothesis is actually false), but P with one NaN -> all 640/640 W samples NaN -> 60/60 record samples flagged. This is asymmetric with the range rule path (_evaluate_range:166), where a single NaN flags exactly one sample, and with a NaN in pitch here (pitch is unfiltered, so one NaN flags one sample). A single dropout/gap in the slow pressure series therefore silently nukes the whole record from the turbulence estimate via this QC rule, rather than excising only the bad neighborhood. The docstring/design clearly intends a local, per-sample consistency flag, not a whole-record kill switch.
suggested_fix: Restrict the non-finite contribution to genuinely-local badness: flag `~np.isfinite(pitch)` (pitch is unfiltered) but for W use the finiteness of the underlying P/W *before* filtfilt smearing, or compute W with NaN-aware smoothing (mask NaNs, interpolate the gap, or run the filter on finite segments). At minimum, drop `~np.isfinite(W)` from line 270 and instead derive W-finiteness from the raw gradient of P so a single P NaN flags only its local neighborhood, matching the range-rule semantics.
verify_reasoning:
Verified against the actual code. qc_rules.py:236 computes `W = smooth_fall_rate(P, fs_slow)`, and line 270 does `flag |= ~np.isfinite(pitch) | ~np.isfinite(W)`. smooth_fall_rate (scor160/profile.py:39-42) is np.gradient + scipy.signal.filtfilt, neither NaN-aware. Empirically reproduced: clean P -> 0 non-finite W (so the prompt's "filtfilt edge NaN" hypothesis is indeed false, as the candidate noted); a single NaN anywhere in P -> all 640/640 W samples non-finite -> entire slow record flagged flight-inconsistent. This is asymmetric with _evaluate_range (line 166), which flags ~isfinite per-sample, and with the unfiltered `pitch` on this same path (one NaN -> one sample). So a single bad/dropped P sample silently sets this rule's bit on the whole record instead of a local neighborhood. The range rule has an explicit per-sample NaN test (tests line 96); the consistency rule has no NaN test, so this whole-record smearing is untested and clearly contrary to the per-sample design intent.

One material caveat that moderates severity rather than refuting it: reachability requires a non-finite value in pf.channels["P"]. In the standard VMP path, P is produced from a single raw .p file via convert_poly (channels.py:139-152) = np.polyval of finite int16 counts with finite coefficients -> always finite, no NaN source; so the default pipeline cannot trigger it. The realistic trigger is a hotel-injected P (pipeline.py:859 runs merge_hotel_into_pfile before the QC rules; hotel.py:466-473 can overwrite the "P" channel), where NaN enters only if the hotel source data itself contains NaN (interp1d/PchipInterpolator propagate input NaN; out-of-bounds is filled with boundary values, so edges are fine). Thus the defect is genuine and the code does have the hazard, but it is conditionally reachable via processed/hotel-supplied pressure rather than the primary raw-file path. Net: real numerical-hazard bug; major is appropriate given the silent whole-record loss when it does occur, with the reachability caveat noted.

title: Auto-polarity median tie (median==0) silently breaks ambiguous records nose-down
description: Auto polarity does `med = median(sign(pitch[moving]*W[moving]))` then `sign = 1.0 if med >= 0 else -1.0`. When the moving samples split exactly evenly between sign-agreement and sign-disagreement, np.median returns 0.0 and the `>= 0` branch arbitrarily picks nose-down (+1). I verified an even 40/40 split yields med==0.0, sign=nose-down, and 40/80 samples flagged; the identical balanced record with the opposite true mount polarity would be mislabeled. The tie itself is a knife-edge condition (exactly equal counts) and the rule's auto-detection is documented as inherently ambiguous for non-healthy records, so this is low-frequency, but the `>=` makes the tie resolve deterministically to one polarity rather than declining to flag. Combined with the auto path's own docstring noting steady flight must dominate, a genuinely balanced/ambiguous record gets a confident-but-arbitrary verdict.
suggested_fix: On an exact tie (med == 0, or |sum of signs| below a small confidence threshold), treat the polarity as undetermined: skip the rule (return all-zeros) and log a warning that polarity could not be inferred, rather than defaulting to nose-down and flagging half the record.
verify_reasoning:
The numerical claim is verified: at src/odas_tpw/perturb/qc_rules.py:257-258, `med = float(np.median(np.sign(pitch[moving]*W[moving])))` returns exactly 0.0 on an even split of +1/-1 signs (confirmed: 40/40 -> 0.0; also 0.0 with a zero straddling the center), and `sign = 1.0 if med >= 0 else -1.0` then deterministically picks nose-down (+1). On a perfectly balanced record the opposite true mount polarity would be mislabeled and roughly half the moving samples flagged via `sign*pitch*W < 0`. So the described behavior genuinely exists.

However, the defect is a measure-zero knife-edge: it requires the EXACT equality of sign-agreement and sign-disagreement counts down to a single sample (39/41 already yields med=-1.0). With the hundreds-to-thousands of slow samples in a real deployment, exact parity is astronomically improbable, so practical frequency is effectively nil. Moreover the auto path's docstring (lines 197-200) explicitly states it assumes "steady flight dominates a healthy record" and that the choice is logged (logger.info at 261-265); a genuinely 50/50 record is, by the code's own documented framing, outside what auto is designed to resolve, with the explicit `nose_down`/`nose_up` config provided for ambiguous mounts. The consequence is a soft QC-mask quality degradation (over-flagging / mislabeled polarity), not a crash, NaN, or data corruption, and only on an input the mode is documented not to handle. Real but trivial; downgraded from minor to nit.

title: Auto-polarity defaults to nose-down with no low-confidence signal when no moving samples exist
description: When a record has no 'moving' samples (parked, very short, or all sub-threshold), the auto branch sets sign = 1.0 (nose-down) and logs only at INFO that it 'auto-detected' nose-down, with no indication the detection had zero evidence. I verified a parked record (W~0) produces 0 flags in this case, so it is benign in isolation (the consistency term cannot fire and clean P/pitch add no NaN flags); the concern is purely that a confident-sounding INFO ('auto-detected inclinometer polarity: nose-down') is emitted from an empty sample set, which could mislead downstream interpretation if combined with later edits. Not a behavioral bug today.
suggested_fix: When `not np.any(moving)`, log at WARNING that polarity could not be determined (no in-flight samples) and that the default was applied, or skip the rule entirely for that record.
verify_reasoning:
Verified against src/odas_tpw/perturb/qc_rules.py:255-265. In the auto-polarity branch, when `not np.any(moving)` the code falls through to `sign = 1.0` (qc_rules.py:259-260), then unconditionally logs at INFO "auto-detected inclinometer polarity: positive pitch = nose-down" (qc_rules.py:261-265). The log message is identical whether the polarity was inferred from the median sign of real moving samples or defaulted with zero evidence -- it gives no indication the sample set was empty. The finding's description is accurate.

I confirmed the "benign in isolation" claim by reading the flag logic: at qc_rules.py:269 `flag = (sign * pitch * W < 0) & moving`. When no samples are moving, `moving` is all-False, so this term is all-False regardless of `sign`; the chosen default of 1.0 therefore has no behavioral effect. Line 270 only adds NaN-derived flags (`~np.isfinite(pitch) | ~np.isfinite(W)`), which are zero for a clean parked record with finite P and pitch. So a parked/sub-threshold record produces 0 flags and the misleading log is the only artifact. This is purely an observability/logging-quality issue with no impact on QC flags or downstream data.

The finding is real but cosmetic, and the author honestly self-rates it as a nit and states it is "Not a behavioral bug today" -- that assessment is correct. Keeping severity at nit.

title: L3 chi T_means uses np.mean not np.nanmean: one NaN slow-temp sample silently zeroes chi for affected windows
description: T_means = np.mean(l2_chi.temp[indices], axis=1) computes the per-window mean slow temperature with a non-NaN-aware mean. A single NaN sample in a window makes T_means[w]=NaN, which flows into visc(T_means[i], sal, P) at line 158 (or visc35 at line 163) producing nu=NaN, and into gradT_noise_batch(F_const, T_means, ...) at line 192. nu=NaN then propagates to chi=NaN in l4_chi.py (which reads l3_chi.nu[j] at line 100 with no guard), silently, with NO warning. Verified the NaN source is real: the L2 slow temperature passed through process_l2_chi (l2_chi.py:130, temp_slow=l1.temp) can contain NaN because (a) the VMP adapter defaults missing T1 to an all-NaN array (adapter.py:74 pf.channels.get('T1', np.full(len(pf.t_slow), np.nan))) which np.interp (line 76) preserves, (b) np.interp of a gappy/NaN-bearing T1 propagates NaN, and (c) the scor160 L1 reader uses np.interp(time, time_temp, temp) (io.py:405) which carries source NaN. Verified empirically that with overlapping windows a single NaN sample poisons every window containing it (2 of 4 in a test), each yielding nu=NaN, while np.nanmean recovers a valid mean. This is the SAME class already known for salinity at line 154, but T_means is a distinct, higher-impact source (feeds both viscosity AND the noise model). Critically, this is asymmetric with the shear epsilon side: process_l4 (scor160/l4.py:154-160) explicitly detects NaN temp and falls back to 10 degC via np.where(np.isfinite(temp), temp, 10.0) with a warning; the chi path has no equivalent fallback, so chi silently goes NaN where epsilon would not.
suggested_fix: Use np.nanmean for the per-window temperature mean (T_means = np.nanmean(l2_chi.temp[indices], axis=1)), and/or add a NaN->fallback temperature guard mirroring scor160/l4.py:154-160 before calling visc/visc35 in the chi path. Consider the same nanmean for sal_means (line 154) to fully close the viscosity NaN-poisoning.
verify_reasoning:
Verified by reading the actual code and running empirical checks.

CODE CONFIRMED:
- src/odas_tpw/chi/l3_chi.py:149 is exactly `T_means = np.mean(l2_chi.temp[indices], axis=1)` — a non-NaN-aware mean over each window's slow temperature.
- T_means feeds visc(T_means[i], sal_means[i], P_means[i]) at line 158 and visc35(T_means[i]) at line 163 to build nu_all, and feeds gradT_noise_batch(F_const, T_means, ...) at line 191.
- nu_all is stored as l3_chi.nu and read at src/odas_tpw/chi/l4_chi.py:100 (`nu = l3_chi.nu[j]`) with NO finiteness guard; it is passed straight into _iterative_fit / _mle_fit_kB / _chi_from_epsilon. The only guards in l4_chi are on epsilon_val (Method 1, lines 180-181) and the MLE mask count (line 242) — none on nu. nu sets the Batchelor wavenumber, so nu=NaN yields chi=NaN.

EMPIRICAL (ran with the repo venv):
- np.mean([10, nan, 12, 13]) -> nan; np.nanmean(...) -> 11.667 (so one NaN sample poisons the whole window mean; nanmean recovers it).
- np.interp with a NaN in the y-values propagates the NaN: [10, nan, 12, 13].
- visc(nan, 35, 0) -> nan and visc35(nan) -> nan.

NaN SOURCE IS REAL: The production VMP chi path src/odas_tpw/rsi/adapter.py:74 defaults a missing T1 to `np.full(len(pf.t_slow), np.nan)`, then np.interp (line 76) preserves NaN into l1.temp; l2_chi.py:130 sets temp_slow = l1.temp; that becomes l2_chi.temp consumed at l3_chi.py:149. Independently, scor160/io.py:392-395 carries a comment that literally documents this failure mode ("Without this, L3 windows get temp=NaN -> visc35(NaN)=NaN -> all epsilon estimates NaN, silently"). Any NaN within a partially-valid T1/TEMP record also survives np.interp.

ASYMMETRY CONFIRMED: scor160/l4.py:154-160 explicitly detects non-finite window temperature and substitutes 10 degC with a warnings.warn; the chi path (l3_chi.py / l4_chi.py) has no equivalent fallback and emits no warning, so chi silently goes NaN where epsilon would not.

Minor correction to the finding's wording: io.py:405 np.interp is a *mitigation* that was added to avoid the slow/fast grid-mismatch all-NaN case (not itself a fresh NaN source); but the rsi/adapter.py:74 all-NaN default and any embedded NaNs are genuine sources that np.interp preserves, so the defect stands.

Severity major (not blocker): the bug silently NaNs chi only for windows whose slow-temp contains a NaN — a realistic and documented input, but not present on every run; impact is silent data loss in a derived product with a clean, asymmetric reference fix already present on the epsilon side. The suggested fix (np.nanmean for T_means, plus a NaN->fallback temperature guard mirroring scor160/l4.py, and ideally nanmean for sal_means) is correct.

title: process_l2_chi dT/dz raises IndexError on a length-1 temp_fast row (dTdt[-1] on empty diff)
description: In the gradient loop, dTdt = np.diff(temp_fast[ci]) * fs then dTdt = np.append(dTdt, dTdt[-1]). For a length-1 row (l1.n_time == 1), np.diff returns an empty array and dTdt[-1] raises 'index -1 is out of bounds for axis 0 with size 0' (verified empirically). temp_fast[ci] is the FULL profile time series, not a section, so this requires the entire L1 profile to be exactly 1 fast sample. Reachability through the production pipeline is effectively nil: get_profiles gates every profile by min_duration (default 7-10 s) at fs_fast ~512 Hz -> min_samples in the thousands (scor160/profile.py:183-194, rsi/profile.py). A length-0 temp_fast is rejected upstream by has_temp_fast (io.py:79 requires size>0). So only a directly-constructed degenerate L1Data (process_l2_chi is a public exported API and is also invoked from chi_io.py:144 and pyturb/eps.py:240) reaches this. Note also that the pipeline's try/except only wraps process_l3_chi (pipeline.py:381-389), NOT the process_l2_chi call at pipeline.py:370, so if this ever fired it would abort the whole-profile run rather than being logged and skipped. Reported as a defensive robustness gap, not a live pipeline crash.
suggested_fix: Guard the length-1/empty case before indexing, e.g. dTdt = np.diff(temp_fast[ci]) * fs; dTdt = np.append(dTdt, dTdt[-1]) if dTdt.size else np.zeros_like(temp_fast[ci]); or early-return/skip when temp_fast.shape[1] < 2. Optionally also wrap the process_l2_chi call in pipeline.py in the same try/except as process_l3_chi.
verify_reasoning:
